source.title: PRD: Optimize Quality Guardrails — Mandatory Quality Gating for Config Changes
source.author: Aditya Rao
source.body:
## Summary
Redwood Optimize can recommend cost-saving configuration changes (batching, caching, quantization profiles) that can materially affect model outputs. This PRD defines **mandatory quality evaluation gates** that must pass before Optimize can apply any supported config change to production traffic (automatically or via one-click). The intent is to prevent silent quality regressions while keeping developer experience fast via tiered gates (smoke → full) and clear override policy with auditability.

**This PRD is for Hosted API gating enforcement.** Dedicated will follow after GA; Private will be manual-first with documentation and UI surfaces.

---

## Problem
Optimize today can surface recommendations that improve unit economics but may change output quality. Without systematic gating, we rely on post-deploy detection (customer complaints, ad-hoc monitoring) and reactive rollbacks.

Common regression modes:
- Quantization profile changes impacting long-context or rare-token behavior
- Batching policy changes changing effective scheduling / latency distribution and causing timeouts, truncation, or downstream tool-calling failures
- Prefix/KV caching settings changing behavior under partial prefix matches or cache eviction edge cases

We need **consistent, measurable, enforceable** guardrails that:
1) block known-bad changes before they reach full traffic,
2) remain fast enough to preserve Optimize’s “one-click value,” and
3) provide a complete audit trail for regulated / enterprise workflows.

---

## Goals
- **Prevent silent quality regressions** caused by cost optimizations by introducing required quality gates.
- **Define consistent pass/fail criteria** using offline evals and (where applicable) online canary signals.
- **Enforce gating at the right boundaries** (defense-in-depth): block Apply and block rollout promotion.
- **Make safe-by-default the fastest path**: provide tiered gates and clear guidance.
- **Traceability and auditability**: record what changed, why, what ran, results, and who approved/overrode.

---

## Non-goals (initial release)
- Fully automated gating for Redwood Private / on-prem deployments (we will support manual workflows + documentation only).
- Overhauling model onboarding or prompt set authoring processes (we integrate with existing eval harness and curated prompt sets).
- Building a new online experimentation system (we integrate with existing Rollouts canary workflows and telemetry).

---

## Personas & primary users
- **Application Engineer / ML Engineer (self-serve):** wants one-click apply for cost savings without breaking their app.
- **Platform Engineer (enterprise):** wants predictable guardrails, audit logs, and strong defaults.
- **Applied ML / Evals owner:** wants rigorous metrics, variance handling, and prompt set coverage control.
- **SRE / Oncall:** needs operational visibility and low-noise alerting (flake detection, timeouts, failure spikes).

---

## User stories
### US1 — One-click apply (Interactive)
**As an engineer**, when Optimize suggests a config change, I can click “Apply,” and Redwood will:
1) automatically run required quality gates for the proposed diff,
2) show pass/fail + key deltas and artifact links,
3) block apply if gates fail (or require an override with justification).

**Success looks like:** I can safely adopt a recommendation within a predictable time budget, with clear confidence signals.

### US2 — Auto-apply (Policy-driven)
**As a platform team**, I can opt into “auto-apply” for certain recommendation types and routes, but only if:
- required gates pass, and
- a canary rollout policy is attached (where applicable), and
- auto-rollback triggers exist.

**Success looks like:** cost savings accrue continuously without quality regressions and with an auditable trail.

### US3 — Gate failure triage
**As an oncall or eval owner**, when a gate fails I can quickly determine whether it is:
- a true regression,
- an eval flake / insufficient sample, or
- an infra / capacity issue (timeouts, region GPU scarcity),
with direct links to artifacts and recommended next steps.

### US4 — Override with accountability
**As an authorized approver**, I can approve a time-bound override when business needs require it, with:
- reason code + free-text justification,
- expiration date,
- required follow-up task (e.g., rerun full gate, create bug ticket), and
- audit log export compatibility.

---

## Supported config change types (in-scope)
Optimize recommendations that propose a diff in one or more of:
1) **Batching policy updates**
   - max batch size, continuous batching toggles/limits, queueing thresholds
2) **Prefix/KV caching settings**
   - prefix cache enablement, cache size/eviction tuning, KV reuse policies
3) **Quantization profile changes**
   - profile selection per model family and GPU SKU (e.g., FP16 → INT8, or INT8 variant A → variant B)

Out of scope for gating MVP:
- Prompt/template changes, tool definitions changes, safety policy changes
- Switching to a different base model family (handled via existing model rollouts + model eval policy)

---

## Product requirements
### R1 — Gating must be mandatory before production apply
- Any supported config change must create a **Gate Run** record.
- The Optimize “Apply” action must be **blocked** unless the gate run is **Passed** or a valid **Override Granted** exists.
- Enforcement must be **server-side** (not only UI), with clear machine-readable failure reasons.

### R2 — Tiered gates: Smoke vs Full
We will support at least two tiers:
- **Smoke gate (fast):** small prompt subset, high-signal checks; aims to catch obvious regressions quickly.
- **Full gate:** larger coverage across prompt sets and metrics, with stronger variance controls.

Default behavior by change type (MVP defaults; configurable in v1):
- Batching + cache diffs: Smoke gate required; Full gate required for enterprise tiers and/or risky knob changes.
- Quantization profile diffs: Smoke gate + Full gate required by default.

### R3 — Pass/fail criteria and variance handling
- Gate results must report:
  - baseline config used,
  - proposed config diff,
  - metric deltas vs baseline,
  - confidence/variance signals (per eval methodology),
  - flake score / rerun count.
- Pass/fail decisions must use thresholds defined in: `quality-gate-metrics-and-thresholds-v1`.

### R4 — Baseline selection rules
- Default baseline is the **current production config** for the same route/model/version.
- Quantization baseline must also match **hardware SKU** (GPU type) and supported execution path.
- Baseline must be immutable for the duration of a gate run; if baseline changes mid-run, rerun is required.

### R5 — Rollout coupling (canary & promotion)
- A config change cannot be promoted from canary to 100% if gates are failing.
- Rollout controller must:
  - halt promotion on gate failure,
  - optionally trigger rollback to baseline for configured routes,
  - notify owners via configured channels.

### R6 — Overrides (exception policy)
- Override capability must be restricted (RBAC), time-bound, and audited.
- Override must require:
  - owner (requester), approver, reason code, text justification,
  - expiration (default 72 hours; max allowed per policy),
  - follow-up task link (ticket).

### R7 — Console/Optimize UX requirements
- Optimize recommendation view must display:
  - gate status (Not started / Running / Passed / Failed / Overridden / Expired),
  - top-level summary (1–3 key metrics),
  - artifact links (eval report, prompts, traces),
  - clear call-to-action (Run gates / View results / Request override).
- Apply button behavior:
  - enabled only when Pass or Override Granted,
  - disabled with tooltip + blocking reasons otherwise.

### R8 — Observability & audit trail
- Gate-run events must be emitted to:
  - audit log (enterprise exportable),
  - metrics/tracing for operational monitoring.
- Required minimal audit events:
  - gate_started, gate_completed, gate_passed, gate_failed,
  - override_requested, override_granted, override_denied, override_expired,
  - apply_blocked_due_to_gate.

---

## MVP vs v1 scope
### MVP (Limited production rollout target: 2026-02)
- Supported diffs: batching, caching, quant profile
- Gate tiers: smoke + full
- Mandatory gating enforcement at Apply boundary
- Rollout promotion blocked on gate failure
- Minimal UI surfaces in Console/Optimize for status + artifact links
- Overrides enabled for restricted approvers with expiration + audit events
- Basic flake handling: rerun policy + flake score surfaced

### v1 (GA target: 2026-03+)
- Per-route / per-tier configurable gate policy (e.g., enterprise requires full gates)
- Better guidance and guardrails: “risk level” classification of diffs (static validation)
- Quarantine workflow for flaky prompt sets + admin tooling
- Expanded online canary quality signals (where reliable) and tighter coupling with rollback triggers
- Stronger reporting: cost saved vs quality protected dashboards

---

## Detailed functional requirements
### Gate policy selection
- Inputs to policy selection (MVP):
  - change type (batch/cache/quant)
  - account tier (self-serve vs enterprise)
  - route criticality flag (optional, default false)
- Outputs:
  - required gate tier(s)
  - timeout budget per tier
  - retry/rerun limits

### Gate state model
Gate run state must include:
- NotStarted → Pending → Running → (Passed | Failed | Canceled)
- OverrideRequested → (OverrideGranted | OverrideDenied)
- OverrideGranted → (Expired)

### Execution constraints
- Smoke gate target runtime: **≤ 20 minutes p95** (excluding queue time)
- Full gate target runtime: **≤ 120 minutes p95**
- Hard timeouts must be enforced; timeouts are treated as failures with clear reason codes (distinguish infra vs quality).

### Failure reason codes (user-facing + API)
At minimum:
- QUALITY_REGRESSION
- INSUFFICIENT_CONFIDENCE / HIGH_VARIANCE
- EVAL_FLAKE_DETECTED
- EVAL_TIMEOUT
- EVAL_INFRA_ERROR
- BASELINE_NOT_AVAILABLE
- CONFIG_DIFF_INVALID / UNSUPPORTED_COMBINATION

---

## Success metrics
### Outcome metrics (business & user)
- **Cost saved (realized):** incremental $ savings / token or per route after gated changes ship (tracked weekly).
- **Quality protected:**
  - # and % of proposed changes blocked due to quality regression
  - reduction in customer-reported quality incidents attributable to Optimize changes
- **Adoption:** % of Optimize recommendations that complete gating and ship (by change type)

### Guardrail health metrics (operational)
- Gate pass rate (smoke / full) by model family and change type
- Gate execution latency p50/p95 (queue + run time)
- Flake rate and rerun frequency (by prompt set)
- False positive rate (manually confirmed non-regressions) and top drivers
- Override rate, average override duration, overrides without follow-up completion

Initial targets for limited rollout (go/no-go inputs; subject to final sign-off):
- Smoke gate completion p95 ≤ 30 minutes end-to-end (including queue)
- Full gate completion p95 ≤ 3 hours end-to-end
- Flake rate ≤ 5% of runs requiring >1 rerun
- Override rate ≤ 2% of applied changes (excluding pre-approved internal testing)

---

## Rollout plan
### Phase 0 — Internal staging (2026-01)
- Enable gating in staging for synthetic + internal test routes.
- Run shadow gate runs for common Optimize recommendations (do not block apply yet).
- Validate baseline selection, artifact links, and failure reason clarity.

### Phase 1 — Internal beta (2026-01 to 2026-02)
- Turn on enforcement (block apply) for select internal workloads.
- Add oncall runbook and dashboard.
- Measure flake rate and tune thresholds/prompt sets.

### Phase 2 — Limited production rollout (2026-02)
- Hosted API only.
- Start with:
  - a subset of routes (low/medium criticality)
  - a subset of accounts (opt-in + a small default cohort)
- Enforce gating for all in-scope diff types.
- Require full gates by default for quant profile changes.

### Phase 3 — GA (2026-03+)
- Expand to broader route coverage.
- Enable per-tier policy defaults (enterprise stricter).
- Begin Dedicated default integration plan; Private manual workflow surfaced in Console.

Rollback strategy:
- Feature flag gating enforcement per account/route.
- If gating system instability causes widespread blocking (infra), allow temporary “monitor-only” mode, but keep audit logging and require explicit approval for switching modes.

---

## Dependencies
- **Eval Harness:** trigger API, artifact storage, rerun aggregation, flake score.
- **Optimize service:** config diff schema, gate state machine persistence, apply-time enforcement.
- **Rollout controller:** promotion gating + optional rollback triggers.
- **Console:** UX surfaces for status/results/override request.
- **Observability pack:** metrics/traces + dashboards and alerts.
- **Policy/RBAC:** override approver roles and audit log export.

---

## Risks & mitigations
- **Eval flakiness blocks legitimate savings.** Mitigation: flake scoring, rerun policy, quarantine workflow; keep smoke gates high-signal.
- **Gate latency slows down developer workflow.** Mitigation: tiered gates; smoke-first; dedicated eval capacity pools; p95 targets tracked.
- **Baseline drift causes inconsistent comparisons.** Mitigation: baseline pinning per gate run; rerun on baseline change.
- **Overuse of overrides erodes protections.** Mitigation: strict RBAC, time bounds, audit review, override rate alerts.
- **Config diff invalid combinations.** Mitigation: static validation before expensive evals; clear rejection reasons.

---

## Acceptance criteria (MVP)
1) For each in-scope config diff type (batch, cache, quant), Optimize can initiate a gate run and persist results.
2) Apply is blocked server-side when gates are failing or missing; clear reason codes returned.
3) Console/Optimize shows gate status, key deltas, and artifact links.
4) Rollout controller blocks canary promotion when gates fail.
5) Override flow exists, is RBAC-restricted, time-bound, and emits audit events.
6) Observability dashboards show gate funnel and failure/flake breakdown; alerts exist for failure spikes and sustained infra timeouts.
7) End-to-end integration test covers: propose diff → run gates → pass/fail → apply allowed/blocked → override → apply allowed.

---

## Open questions
- Final default thresholds and minimum sample sizes per gate type (Applied ML to finalize in thresholds doc).
- Which batching/caching knobs qualify as “risky” and should require full gates by default (Runtime to provide initial classification).
- Online canary quality signals: which are reliable enough to block promotion vs advisory only (SRE + Applied ML alignment).

---

## Appendix: terminology
- **Gate Run:** A single evaluation execution for a proposed config diff compared against a pinned baseline.
- **Smoke Gate:** Fast, high-signal eval subset intended to catch obvious regressions.
- **Full Gate:** Broader eval suite intended to provide high confidence for production rollout.
- **Override:** Time-bound exception allowing apply despite failed/missing gates, with audit trail and follow-up.

- Quantization profile changes impacting long-context or rare-token behavior
- Batching policy changes changing effective scheduling / latency distribution and causing timeouts, truncation, or downstream tool-calling failures
- Prefix/KV caching settings changing behavior under partial prefix matches or cache eviction edge cases

We need **consistent, measurable, enforceable** guardrails that:
1) block known-bad changes before they reach full traffic,
2) remain fast enough to preserve Optimize’s “one-click value,” and
3) provide a complete audit trail for regulated / enterprise workflows.

---

## Goals
- **Prevent silent quality regressions** caused by cost optimizations by introducing required quality gates.
- **Define consistent pass/fail criteria** using offline evals and (where applicable) online canary signals.
- **Enforce gating at the right boundaries** (defense-in-depth): block Apply and block rollout promotion.
- **Make safe-by-default the fastest path**: provide tiered gates and clear guidance.
- **Traceability and auditability**: record what changed, why, what ran, results, and who approved/overrode.

---

## Non-goals (initial release)
- Fully automated gating for Redwood Private / on-prem deployments (we will support manual workflows + documentation only).
- Overhauling model onboarding or prompt set authoring processes (we integrate with existing eval harness and curated prompt sets).
- Building a new online experimentation system (we integrate with existing Rollouts canary workflows and telemetry).

---

## Personas & primary users
- **Application Engineer / ML Engineer (self-serve):** wants one-click apply for cost savings without breaking their app.
- **Platform Engineer (enterprise):** wants predictable guardrails, audit logs, and strong defaults.
- **Applied ML / Evals owner:** wants rigorous metrics, variance handling, and prompt set coverage control.
- **SRE / Oncall:** needs operational visibility and low-noise alerting (flake detection, timeouts, failure spikes).

---

## User stories
### US1 — One-click apply (Interactive)
**As an engineer**, when Optimize suggests a config change, I can click “Apply,” and Redwood will:
1) automatically run required quality gates for the proposed diff,
2) show pass/fail + key deltas and artifact links,
3) block apply if gates fail (or require an override with justification).

**Success looks like:** I can safely adopt a recommendation within a predictable time budget, with clear confidence signals.

### US2 — Auto-apply (Policy-driven)
**As a platform team**, I can opt into “auto-apply” for certain recommendation types and routes, but only if:
- required gates pass, and
- a canary rollout policy is attached (where applicable), and
- auto-rollback triggers exist.

**Success looks like:** cost savings accrue continuously without quality regressions and with an auditable trail.

### US3 — Gate failure triage
**As an oncall or eval owner**, when a gate fails I can quickly determine whether it is:
- a true regression,
- an eval flake / insufficient sample, or
- an infra / capacity issue (timeouts, region GPU scarcity),
with direct links to artifacts and recommended next steps.

### US4 — Override with accountability
**As an authorized approver**, I can approve a time-bound override when business needs require it, with:
- reason code + free-text justification,
- expiration date,
- required follow-up task (e.g., rerun full gate, create bug ticket), and
- audit log export compatibility.

---

## Supported config change types (in-scope)
Optimize recommendations that propose a diff in one or more of:
1) **Batching policy updates**
   - max batch size, continuous batching toggles/limits, queueing thresholds
2) **Prefix/KV caching settings**
   - prefix cache enablement, cache size/eviction tuning, KV reuse policies
3) **Quantization profile changes**
   - profile selection per model family and GPU SKU (e.g., FP16 → INT8, or INT8 variant A → variant B)

Out of scope for gating MVP:
- Prompt/template changes, tool definitions changes, safety policy changes
- Switching to a different base model family (handled via existing model rollouts + model eval policy)

---

## Product requirements
### R1 — Gating must be mandatory before production apply
- Any supported config change must create a **Gate Run** record.
- The Optimize “Apply” action must be **blocked** unless the gate run is **Passed** or a valid **Override Granted** exists.
- Enforcement must be **server-side** (not only UI), with clear machine-readable failure reasons.

### R2 — Tiered gates: Smoke vs Full
We will support at least two tiers:
- **Smoke gate (fast):** small prompt subset, high-signal checks; aims to catch obvious regressions quickly.
- **Full gate:** larger coverage across prompt sets and metrics, with stronger variance controls.

Default behavior by change type (MVP defaults; configurable in v1):
- Batching + cache diffs: Smoke gate required; Full gate required for enterprise tiers and/or risky knob changes.
- Quantization profile diffs: Smoke gate + Full gate required by default.

### R3 — Pass/fail criteria and variance handling
- Gate results must report:
  - baseline config used,
  - proposed config diff,
  - metric deltas vs baseline,
  - confidence/variance signals (per eval methodology),
  - flake score / rerun count.
- Pass/fail decisions must use thresholds defined in: `quality-gate-metrics-and-thresholds-v1`.

### R4 — Baseline selection rules
- Default baseline is the **current production config** for the same route/model/version.
- Quantization baseline must also match **hardware SKU** (GPU type) and supported execution path.
- Baseline must be immutable for the duration of a gate run; if baseline changes mid-run, rerun is required.

### R5 — Rollout coupling (canary & promotion)
- A config change cannot be promoted from canary to 100% if gates are failing.
- Rollout controller must:
  - halt promotion on gate failure,
  - optionally trigger rollback to baseline for configured routes,
  - notify owners via configured channels.

### R6 — Overrides (exception policy)
- Override capability must be restricted (RBAC), time-bound, and audited.
- Override must require:
  - owner (requester), approver, reason code, text justification,
  - expiration (default 72 hours; max allowed per policy),
  - follow-up task link (ticket).

### R7 — Console/Optimize UX requirements
- Optimize recommendation view must display:
  - gate status (Not started / Running / Passed / Failed / Overridden / Expired),
  - top-level summary (1–3 key metrics),
  - artifact links (eval report, prompts, traces),
  - clear call-to-action (Run gates / View results / Request override).
- Apply button behavior:
  - enabled only when Pass or Override Granted,
  - disabled with tooltip + blocking reasons otherwise.

### R8 — Observability & audit trail
- Gate-run events must be emitted to:
  - audit log (enterprise exportable),
  - metrics/tracing for operational monitoring.
- Required minimal audit events:
  - gate_started, gate_completed, gate_passed, gate_failed,
  - override_requested, override_granted, override_denied, override_expired,
  - apply_blocked_due_to_gate.

---

## MVP vs v1 scope
### MVP (Limited production rollout target: 2026-02)
- Supported diffs: batching, caching, quant profile
- Gate tiers: smoke + full
- Mandatory gating enforcement at Apply boundary
- Rollout promotion blocked on gate failure
- Minimal UI surfaces in Console/Optimize for status + artifact links
- Overrides enabled for restricted approvers with expiration + audit events
- Basic flake handling: rerun policy + flake score surfaced

### v1 (GA target: 2026-03+)
- Per-route / per-tier configurable gate policy (e.g., enterprise requires full gates)
- Better guidance and guardrails: “risk level” classification of diffs (static validation)
- Quarantine workflow for flaky prompt sets + admin tooling
- Expanded online canary quality signals (where reliable) and tighter coupling with rollback triggers
- Stronger reporting: cost saved vs quality protected dashboards

---

## Detailed functional requirements
### Gate policy selection
- Inputs to policy selection (MVP):
  - change type (batch/cache/quant)
  - account tier (self-serve vs enterprise)
  - route criticality flag (optional, default false)
- Outputs:
  - required gate tier(s)
  - timeout budget per tier
  - retry/rerun limits

### Gate state model
Gate run state must include:
- NotStarted → Pending → Running → (Passed | Failed | Canceled)
- OverrideRequested → (OverrideGranted | OverrideDenied)
- OverrideGranted → (Expired)

### Execution constraints
- Smoke gate target runtime: **≤ 20 minutes p95** (excluding queue time)
- Full gate target runtime: **≤ 120 minutes p95**
- Hard timeouts must be enforced; timeouts are treated as failures with clear reason codes (distinguish infra vs quality).

### Failure reason codes (user-facing + API)
At minimum:
- QUALITY_REGRESSION
- INSUFFICIENT_CONFIDENCE / HIGH_VARIANCE
- EVAL_FLAKE_DETECTED
- EVAL_TIMEOUT
- EVAL_INFRA_ERROR
- BASELINE_NOT_AVAILABLE
- CONFIG_DIFF_INVALID / UNSUPPORTED_COMBINATION

---

## Success metrics
### Outcome metrics (business & user)
- **Cost saved (realized):** incremental $ savings / token or per route after gated changes ship (tracked weekly).
- **Quality protected:**
  - # and % of proposed changes blocked due to quality regression
  - reduction in customer-reported quality incidents attributable to Optimize changes
- **Adoption:** % of Optimize recommendations that complete gating and ship (by change type)

### Guardrail health metrics (operational)
- Gate pass rate (smoke / full) by model family and change type
- Gate execution latency p50/p95 (queue + run time)
- Flake rate and rerun frequency (by prompt set)
- False positive rate (manually confirmed non-regressions) and top drivers
- Override rate, average override duration, overrides without follow-up completion

Initial targets for limited rollout (go/no-go inputs; subject to final sign-off):
- Smoke gate completion p95 ≤ 30 minutes end-to-end (including queue)
- Full gate completion p95 ≤ 3 hours end-to-end
- Flake rate ≤ 5% of runs requiring >1 rerun
- Override rate ≤ 2% of applied changes (excluding pre-approved internal testing)

---

## Rollout plan
### Phase 0 — Internal staging (2026-01)
- Enable gating in staging for synthetic + internal test routes.
- Run shadow gate runs for common Optimize recommendations (do not block apply yet).
- Validate baseline selection, artifact links, and failure reason clarity.

### Phase 1 — Internal beta (2026-01 to 2026-02)
- Turn on enforcement (block apply) for select internal workloads.
- Add oncall runbook and dashboard.
- Measure flake rate and tune thresholds/prompt sets.

### Phase 2 — Limited production rollout (2026-02)
- Hosted API only.
- Start with:
  - a subset of routes (low/medium criticality)
  - a subset of accounts (opt-in + a small default cohort)
- Enforce gating for all in-scope diff types.
- Require full gates by default for quant profile changes.

### Phase 3 — GA (2026-03+)
- Expand to broader route coverage.
- Enable per-tier policy defaults (enterprise stricter).
- Begin Dedicated default integration plan; Private manual workflow surfaced in Console.

Rollback strategy:
- Feature flag gating enforcement per account/route.
- If gating system instability causes widespread blocking (infra), allow temporary “monitor-only” mode, but keep audit logging and require explicit approval for switching modes.

---

## Dependencies
- **Eval Harness:** trigger API, artifact storage, rerun aggregation, flake score.
- **Optimize service:** config diff schema, gate state machine persistence, apply-time enforcement.
- **Rollout controller:** promotion gating + optional rollback triggers.
- **Console:** UX surfaces for status/results/override request.
- **Observability pack:** metrics/traces + dashboards and alerts.
- **Policy/RBAC:** override approver roles and audit log export.

---

## Risks & mitigations
- **Eval flakiness blocks legitimate savings.** Mitigation: flake scoring, rerun policy, quarantine workflow; keep smoke gates high-signal.
- **Gate latency slows down developer workflow.** Mitigation: tiered gates; smoke-first; dedicated eval capacity pools; p95 targets tracked.
- **Baseline drift causes inconsistent comparisons.** Mitigation: baseline pinning per gate run; rerun on baseline change.
- **Overuse of overrides erodes protections.** Mitigation: strict RBAC, time bounds, audit review, override rate alerts.
- **Config diff invalid combinations.** Mitigation: static validation before expensive evals; clear rejection reasons.

---

## Acceptance criteria (MVP)
1) For each in-scope config diff type (batch, cache, quant), Optimize can initiate a gate run and persist results.
2) Apply is blocked server-side when gates are failing or missing; clear reason codes returned.
3) Console/Optimize shows gate status, key deltas, and artifact links.
4) Rollout controller blocks canary promotion when gates fail.
5) Override flow exists, is RBAC-restricted, time-bound, and emits audit events.
6) Observability dashboards show gate funnel and failure/flake breakdown; alerts exist for failure spikes and sustained infra timeouts.
7) End-to-end integration test covers: propose diff → run gates → pass/fail → apply allowed/blocked → override → apply allowed.

---

## Open questions
- Final default thresholds and minimum sample sizes per gate type (Applied ML to finalize in thresholds doc).
- Which batching/caching knobs qualify as “risky” and should require full gates by default (Runtime to provide initial classification).
- Online canary quality signals: which are reliable enough to block promotion vs advisory only (SRE + Applied ML alignment).

---

## Appendix: terminology
- **Gate Run:** A single evaluation execution for a proposed config diff compared against a pinned baseline.
- **Smoke Gate:** Fast, high-signal eval subset intended to catch obvious regressions.
- **Full Gate:** Broader eval suite intended to provide high confidence for production rollout.
- **Override:** Time-bound exception allowing apply despite failed/missing gates, with audit trail and follow-up.

source.title: Marketplace SKU Catalog v2 (AWS + GCP) — Hosted, Dedicated, Private, Add-ons
source.author: Soojin Lee
source.content:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s **Marketplace SKU Catalog v2** for AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace.

The catalog is designed to:
- Map cleanly to Redwood’s internal billing meters and entitlements (no “special marketplace-only plans” in product).
- Support enterprise procurement patterns (usage-based, commits, reserved capacity, and Private licensing).
- Reduce buyer confusion by aligning names across clouds: **Hosted** vs **Dedicated** vs **Private**, plus add-ons.

This is the source of truth for:
- SKU naming conventions
- Inclusions/exclusions
- Marketplace billing dimensions → internal meters
- Entitlement semantics used by provisioning and metering

---

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Hosted API (usage-based) SKUs
- Dedicated capacity (reserved) SKUs
- Private deployment (license) SKUs
- Compliance + observability add-ons sold via marketplace

**Out of scope (handled via private offers / order forms as needed):**
- Custom model onboarding fees
- One-time professional services
- Complex multi-year ramp schedules (documented in private offer templates)

---

## SKU naming conventions
We need names that work in:
- AWS Marketplace product/offer UIs (title length limits, readability)
- GCP Marketplace listings (SKU + plan naming constraints)
- Internal billing catalog (stable identifiers)

### 1) Human-readable SKU names (customer-facing)
Format:
- **Redwood Inference <Product> — <Plan>**

Examples:
- “Redwood Inference Hosted — Pay-as-you-go”
- “Redwood Inference Dedicated — Reserved GPU Capacity”
- “Redwood Inference Private — VPC/On‑prem License”

Rules:
- Use **Hosted / Dedicated / Private** as top-level taxonomy.
- Avoid hardware jargon in the SKU name (hardware is a capacity attribute in Dedicated entitlements).
- Avoid absolute claims (e.g., “HIPAA compliant”) in any SKU name.

### 2) Marketplace SKU codes (cross-cloud)
Internal canonical code used across systems:
- **RI-MP-<PRODUCT>-<PLAN>-V2**

Where:
- PRODUCT ∈ {HOSTED, DEDICATED, PRIVATE, ADDON}
- PLAN is short and stable (PAYG, COMMIT, RESERVED, LICENSE, COMPLIANCE, RETENTION, AUDIT)

Examples:
- RI-MP-HOSTED-PAYG-V2
- RI-MP-DEDICATED-RESERVED-V2
- RI-MP-PRIVATE-LICENSE-V2
- RI-MP-ADDON-COMPLIANCE-V2

Notes:
- Cloud-specific SKU identifiers (AWS “dimensions”, GCP “billing dimensions/SKUs”) map to these canonical codes.
- v2 codes must not be reused once deprecated.

---

## Product packaging definitions (v2)
### Hosted (Redwood API)
**Intended buyer:** PLG teams + procurement-led usage-based purchases.

**Included:**
- Hosted inference endpoints (chat/text generation, embeddings, reranking where enabled)
- Streaming responses
- Basic usage analytics in Console
- Standard support (best-effort, business hours) unless upgraded via Support policy (not a marketplace SKU in v2)

**Excluded (unless separately contracted):**
- Dedicated data isolation / dedicated GPU pools
- Customer-managed keys (CMK) at-rest for hosted (Private offers/Private deployment only)
- Custom SLAs beyond standard platform targets

### Dedicated (Redwood Dedicated)
**Intended buyer:** teams needing predictable throughput, isolation, and SLA-backed performance.

**Included:**
- Dedicated GPU pool (reserved capacity) tied to an org and region
- Dedicated autoscaling policy within reserved capacity; burst capacity only if contracted
- Console observability + per-model dashboards

**Excluded:**
- On-prem delivery
- Air-gapped environments

### Private (Redwood Private)
**Intended buyer:** regulated enterprises and platform teams requiring VPC/on-prem options and customer-managed networking.

**Included:**
- Private control plane option (VPC) and/or on-prem package (as applicable to the purchased SKU)
- Audit logging features (base) and integration guidance
- Key management integration options (KMS/HSM integrations depend on deployment mode)

**Excluded:**
- Hardware procurement for on-prem (unless separately contracted)
- Customer-specific compliance attestations

---

## Billing meter taxonomy (internal)
All marketplace billing must map to the following internal meters.

### Hosted meters
- **meter.hosted.tokens.prompt**: prompt/input tokens (counted per request)
- **meter.hosted.tokens.completion**: completion/output tokens (counted per request)
- **meter.hosted.embeddings.units**: embedding units (1 unit = 1,000 tokens embedded, rounded up)
- **meter.hosted.rerank.requests**: rerank requests (per call)

### Dedicated meters
- **meter.dedicated.gpu_hour**: GPU-hours consumed within a dedicated pool (by hardware class)
- **meter.dedicated.reservation.month**: reserved capacity commitment (monthly)

### Private meters
- **meter.private.platform.month**: platform license fee (monthly)
- **meter.private.nodes.month**: optional node-based licensing if applicable (monthly)

### Add-on meters
- **meter.addon.compliance.month**: compliance bundle subscription (monthly)
- **meter.addon.audit_export.month**: audit log export feature (monthly)
- **meter.addon.retention_ext.month**: extended retention (monthly)

Metering principles:
- Marketplace dimension → internal meter mapping must be **1:1** wherever possible.
- If a marketplace requires multiple dimensions (e.g., prompt vs completion), both must be present and reconciled daily.
- Rounding rules must be consistent across clouds and align to internal invoice logic.

---

## SKU catalog (v2)
The tables below define the canonical v2 SKUs. Cloud-specific identifiers are recorded in the AWS/GCP offer structure docs; this doc defines the canonical catalog and mapping expectations.

### A) Hosted SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Hosted — Pay-as-you-go | RI-MP-HOSTED-PAYG-V2 | Usage-based | Prompt Tokens (1K), Completion Tokens (1K), Embeddings Units, Rerank Requests | meter.hosted.tokens.prompt; meter.hosted.tokens.completion; meter.hosted.embeddings.units; meter.hosted.rerank.requests | Hosted API access; standard rate limits per org tier | Default marketplace entry SKU; no minimum commit. |
| Redwood Inference Hosted — Committed Spend | RI-MP-HOSTED-COMMIT-V2 | Contract (commit drawdown) | Commit Amount ($) and/or Token Unit bundle (per marketplace constraints) | Internal ledger draws down against hosted meters above | Hosted API access; higher default quotas; invoice via marketplace | Use for customers requesting predictable monthly commit through marketplace. Structure differs by AWS vs GCP constraints; canonical code stays stable. |

Hosted inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- Includes Redwood model catalog access as exposed in Hosted API.
- Does **not** include dedicated capacity; customers must purchase Dedicated for isolation and guaranteed throughput.


### B) Dedicated SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Dedicated — Reserved GPU Capacity | RI-MP-DEDICATED-RESERVED-V2 | Contract (monthly reservation) | Reserved Capacity (monthly) + Optional GPU-hours overage (if allowed) | meter.dedicated.reservation.month (+ meter.dedicated.gpu_hour for overage where applicable) | Dedicated pool entitlement (region, hardware class, size); SLA profile flag | Hardware class (e.g., L4/A10/A100/H100) is an entitlement attribute; do not encode into SKU name unless a marketplace requires separate dimensions. |
| Redwood Inference Dedicated — Usage Overages (Add’l GPU-hours) | RI-MP-DEDICATED-OVERAGE-V2 | Usage-based (only when paired with reserved) | GPU-hours | meter.dedicated.gpu_hour | Allows burst/overage billing when enabled on the org | This SKU is optional and should not be public unless a partner portal requires it; typically enabled via private offer. |

Dedicated inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- “Reserved” implies capacity allocation + predictable throughput targets; exact SLO language is governed by the contract/offer.
- Dedicated does not imply Private networking; for VPC/on-prem requirements, use Private.


### C) Private SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Private — VPC/On‑prem License | RI-MP-PRIVATE-LICENSE-V2 | Contract (monthly/annual) | Platform License (monthly/annual) | meter.private.platform.month | Private deployment entitlement; private control plane flags; support routing tag | Deployment mode (VPC vs on-prem) is captured as an entitlement attribute and provisioning workflow selection. |
| Redwood Inference Private — Node-Based License (Optional) | RI-MP-PRIVATE-NODES-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Licensed Nodes (count/month) | meter.private.nodes.month | Node-count cap for licensed clusters | Only used when customer licensing requires node-based constraints (primarily on-prem). Prefer platform-only license when possible. |

Private inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- “Private” includes customer-managed networking and elevated security controls, but does not automatically grant specific regulatory certifications.
- Audit logs are available in all tiers; advanced export/retention features are add-ons (below).


### D) Add-on SKUs (attachable)
Add-ons are intended to be attachable to Hosted, Dedicated, or Private (subject to deployment compatibility). If a marketplace cannot express “attachable,” we list them as separate purchasable plans and enforce compatibility via entitlement checks.

| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Inclusions | Exclusions/notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compliance Bundle (Marketplace) | RI-MP-ADDON-COMPLIANCE-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Compliance Bundle (monthly) | meter.addon.compliance.month | compliance_bundle=true | Security documentation bundle access workflow, compliance feature flags (where applicable), and faster security review path | Does not confer customer compliance; evidence access may be gated and requires approved contacts. |
| Audit Log Export | RI-MP-ADDON-AUDIT-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Audit Export (monthly) | meter.addon.audit_export.month | audit_export=true | Export audit logs to customer SIEM/storage destinations supported by deployment mode | Availability varies by deployment type; enforce via entitlement compatibility matrix. |
| Extended Retention | RI-MP-ADDON-RETENTION-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Retention Extension (monthly) | meter.addon.retention_ext.month | retention_extended=true | Increased retention for logs/telemetry per policy | Does not override legal hold requirements; retention maxima depend on product constraints and published policy. |

Compatibility matrix (enforced by entitlement service):
- Compliance Bundle: Hosted ✅ / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅
- Audit Log Export: Hosted ⚠️ (limited) / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅
- Extended Retention: Hosted ✅ / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅

(⚠️ = supported only for specific data types or regions; refer to the Security Packet and Data Handling doc.)

---

## Marketplace → internal mapping rules
### 1) Hosted token mapping
- Prompt tokens billed to **meter.hosted.tokens.prompt**.
- Completion tokens billed to **meter.hosted.tokens.completion**.
- Marketplace “per 1K tokens” dimensions are computed as: `ceil(tokens / 1000)` for billing units *only if required by marketplace*; internal raw tokens are still stored for analytics and reconciliation.

### 2) Embeddings units
- 1 embedding unit = **1,000 tokens embedded**, rounded up per request.
- Internal records store raw token count + computed units for marketplace reconciliation.

### 3) Dedicated reservation + overage
- The reservation dimension maps to **meter.dedicated.reservation.month**.
- If overage is enabled, GPU-hour usage maps to **meter.dedicated.gpu_hour** with a hardware_class attribute (e.g., h100, a100, l4).

### 4) Private license
- Platform license maps to **meter.private.platform.month**.
- If node-based licensing is enabled, node count maps to **meter.private.nodes.month**.

### 5) Add-ons
- Add-on SKUs are billed monthly and create entitlement flags consumed by:
  - Console UI toggles
  - API/feature gating
  - Security docs access workflow (where applicable)

---

## Inclusions/exclusions (standardized language blocks)
These blocks are used in the SKU descriptions, listing copy, and order form references.

### Hosted — inclusions
- Hosted API endpoints for supported inference tasks.
- Model version pinning and rollouts (where available in Hosted).
- Standard observability in Redwood Console.

### Hosted — exclusions
- Dedicated GPU pools, capacity reservations, or strict per-tenant isolation.
- Private networking (VPC peering) and on-prem distribution.

### Dedicated — inclusions
- Dedicated capacity allocation with reserved sizing.
- Dedicated throughput predictability and isolation.

### Dedicated — exclusions
- On-prem delivery.
- Customer-managed network perimeter (use Private).

### Private — inclusions
- Private deployment options (VPC and/or on-prem package as contracted).
- Enterprise security controls and configuration options.

### Private — exclusions
- Any statement of customer compliance certification.
- Customer hardware procurement.

---

## Backwards compatibility and migration
Legacy marketplace listings and SKUs will be maintained in a limited-support state during transition.

Plan:
1) Publish v2 SKUs in partner portals (AWS/GCP) alongside legacy.
2) New subscriptions default to v2.
3) Existing customers remain on legacy SKUs until:
   - renewal, or
   - customer-requested migration, or
   - required by marketplace deprecation timelines.
4) Ensure metering reconciliation supports both v1 and v2 identifiers during the overlap window.

Guardrails:
- Do not change billing semantics for an existing subscription without explicit customer approval.
- Canonical codes are immutable once launched.

---

## Open items / decisions needed (for final publish)
- Confirm whether Hosted Commit is represented as a pure “$ commit drawdown” vs bundled “token units” for each marketplace (depends on portal constraints).
- Confirm whether Dedicated Overages SKU is public or private-offer-only.
- Confirm final entitlement attribute schema for hardware_class + region for Dedicated pools.

Owners:
- SKU semantics + naming: Soojin Lee
- Meter mapping + catalog config: Cole Summers
- RevRec/terms validation: Emily Stone, Michael Grant
- Security add-on wording compatibility: Naomi Feldman

title: Marketplace SKU Catalog v2 (AWS + GCP) — Hosted, Dedicated, Private, Add-ons
preview:
## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s **Marketplace SKU Catalog v2** for AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace.

The catalog is designed to:
- Map cleanly to Redwood’s internal billing meters and entitlements (no “special marketplace-only plans” in product).
- Support enterprise procurement patterns (usage-based, commits, reserved capacity, and Private licensing).
- Reduce buyer confusion by aligning names across clouds: **Hosted** vs **Dedicated** vs **Private**, plus add-ons.

This is the source of truth for:
- SKU naming conventions
- Inclusions/exclusions
- Marketplace billing dimensions → internal meters
- Entitlement semantics used by provisioning and metering

---

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Hosted API (usage-based) SKUs
- Dedicated capacity (reserved) SKUs
- Private deployment (license) SKUs
- Compliance + observability add-ons sold via marketplace

**Out of scope (handled via private offers / order forms as needed):**
- Custom model onboarding fees
- One-time professional services
- Complex multi-year ramp schedules (documented in private offer templates)

---

## SKU naming conventions
We need names that work in:
- AWS Marketplace product/offer UIs (title length limits, readability)
- GCP Marketplace listings (SKU + plan naming constraints)
- Internal billing catalog (stable identifiers)

### 1) Human-readable SKU names (customer-facing)
Format:
- **Redwood Inference <Product> — <Plan>**

Examples:
- “Redwood Inference Hosted — Pay-as-you-go”
- “Redwood Inference Dedicated — Reserved GPU Capacity”
- “Redwood Inference Private — VPC/On‑prem License”

Rules:
- Use **Hosted / Dedicated / Private** as top-level taxonomy.
- Avoid hardware jargon in the SKU name (hardware is a capacity attribute in Dedicated entitlements).
- Avoid absolute claims (e.g., “HIPAA compliant”) in any SKU name.

### 2) Marketplace SKU codes (cross-cloud)
Internal canonical code used across systems:
- **RI-MP-<PRODUCT>-<PLAN>-V2**

Where:
- PRODUCT ∈ {HOSTED, DEDICATED, PRIVATE, ADDON}
- PLAN is short and stable (PAYG, COMMIT, RESERVED, LICENSE, COMPLIANCE, RETENTION, AUDIT)

Examples:
- RI-MP-HOSTED-PAYG-V2
- RI-MP-DEDICATED-RESERVED-V2
- RI-MP-PRIVATE-LICENSE-V2
- RI-MP-ADDON-COMPLIANCE-V2

Notes:
- Cloud-specific SKU identifiers (AWS “dimensions”, GCP “billing dimensions/SKUs”) map to these canonical codes.
- v2 codes must not be reused once deprecated.

---

## Product packaging definitions (v2)
### Hosted (Redwood API)
**Intended buyer:** PLG teams + procurement-led usage-based purchases.

**Included:**
- Hosted inference endpoints (chat/text generation, embeddings, reranking where enabled)
- Streaming responses
- Basic usage analytics in Console
- Standard support (best-effort, business hours) unless upgraded via Support policy (not a marketplace SKU in v2)

**Excluded (unless separately contracted):**
- Dedicated data isolation / dedicated GPU pools
- Customer-managed keys (CMK) at-rest for hosted (Private offers/Private deployment only)
- Custom SLAs beyond standard platform targets

### Dedicated (Redwood Dedicated)
**Intended buyer:** teams needing predictable throughput, isolation, and SLA-backed performance.

**Included:**
- Dedicated GPU pool (reserved capacity) tied to an org and region
- Dedicated autoscaling policy within reserved capacity; burst capacity only if contracted
- Console observability + per-model dashboards

**Excluded:**
- On-prem delivery
- Air-gapped environments

### Private (Redwood Private)
**Intended buyer:** regulated enterprises and platform teams requiring VPC/on-prem options and customer-managed networking.

**Included:**
- Private control plane option (VPC) and/or on-prem package (as applicable to the purchased SKU)
- Audit logging features (base) and integration guidance
- Key management integration options (KMS/HSM integrations depend on deployment mode)

**Excluded:**
- Hardware procurement for on-prem (unless separately contracted)
- Customer-specific compliance attestations

---

## Billing meter taxonomy (internal)
All marketplace billing must map to the following internal meters.

### Hosted meters
- **meter.hosted.tokens.prompt**: prompt/input tokens (counted per request)
- **meter.hosted.tokens.completion**: completion/output tokens (counted per request)
- **meter.hosted.embeddings.units**: embedding units (1 unit = 1,000 tokens embedded, rounded up)
- **meter.hosted.rerank.requests**: rerank requests (per call)

### Dedicated meters
- **meter.dedicated.gpu_hour**: GPU-hours consumed within a dedicated pool (by hardware class)
- **meter.dedicated.reservation.month**: reserved capacity commitment (monthly)

### Private meters
- **meter.private.platform.month**: platform license fee (monthly)
- **meter.private.nodes.month**: optional node-based licensing if applicable (monthly)

### Add-on meters
- **meter.addon.compliance.month**: compliance bundle subscription (monthly)
- **meter.addon.audit_export.month**: audit log export feature (monthly)
- **meter.addon.retention_ext.month**: extended retention (monthly)

Metering principles:
- Marketplace dimension → internal meter mapping must be **1:1** wherever possible.
- If a marketplace requires multiple dimensions (e.g., prompt vs completion), both must be present and reconciled daily.
- Rounding rules must be consistent across clouds and align to internal invoice logic.

---

## SKU catalog (v2)
The tables below define the canonical v2 SKUs. Cloud-specific identifiers are recorded in the AWS/GCP offer structure docs; this doc defines the canonical catalog and mapping expectations.

### A) Hosted SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Hosted — Pay-as-you-go | RI-MP-HOSTED-PAYG-V2 | Usage-based | Prompt Tokens (1K), Completion Tokens (1K), Embeddings Units, Rerank Requests | meter.hosted.tokens.prompt; meter.hosted.tokens.completion; meter.hosted.embeddings.units; meter.hosted.rerank.requests | Hosted API access; standard rate limits per org tier | Default marketplace entry SKU; no minimum commit. |
| Redwood Inference Hosted — Committed Spend | RI-MP-HOSTED-COMMIT-V2 | Contract (commit drawdown) | Commit Amount ($) and/or Token Unit bundle (per marketplace constraints) | Internal ledger draws down against hosted meters above | Hosted API access; higher default quotas; invoice via marketplace | Use for customers requesting predictable monthly commit through marketplace. Structure differs by AWS vs GCP constraints; canonical code stays stable. |

Hosted inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- Includes Redwood model catalog access as exposed in Hosted API.
- Does **not** include dedicated capacity; customers must purchase Dedicated for isolation and guaranteed throughput.


### B) Dedicated SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Dedicated — Reserved GPU Capacity | RI-MP-DEDICATED-RESERVED-V2 | Contract (monthly reservation) | Reserved Capacity (monthly) + Optional GPU-hours overage (if allowed) | meter.dedicated.reservation.month (+ meter.dedicated.gpu_hour for overage where applicable) | Dedicated pool entitlement (region, hardware class, size); SLA profile flag | Hardware class (e.g., L4/A10/A100/H100) is an entitlement attribute; do not encode into SKU name unless a marketplace requires separate dimensions. |
| Redwood Inference Dedicated — Usage Overages (Add’l GPU-hours) | RI-MP-DEDICATED-OVERAGE-V2 | Usage-based (only when paired with reserved) | GPU-hours | meter.dedicated.gpu_hour | Allows burst/overage billing when enabled on the org | This SKU is optional and should not be public unless a partner portal requires it; typically enabled via private offer. |

Dedicated inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- “Reserved” implies capacity allocation + predictable throughput targets; exact SLO language is governed by the contract/offer.
- Dedicated does not imply Private networking; for VPC/on-prem requirements, use Private.


### C) Private SKUs
| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Redwood Inference Private — VPC/On‑prem License | RI-MP-PRIVATE-LICENSE-V2 | Contract (monthly/annual) | Platform License (monthly/annual) | meter.private.platform.month | Private deployment entitlement; private control plane flags; support routing tag | Deployment mode (VPC vs on-prem) is captured as an entitlement attribute and provisioning workflow selection. |
| Redwood Inference Private — Node-Based License (Optional) | RI-MP-PRIVATE-NODES-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Licensed Nodes (count/month) | meter.private.nodes.month | Node-count cap for licensed clusters | Only used when customer licensing requires node-based constraints (primarily on-prem). Prefer platform-only license when possible. |

Private inclusions/exclusions clarifications:
- “Private” includes customer-managed networking and elevated security controls, but does not automatically grant specific regulatory certifications.
- Audit logs are available in all tiers; advanced export/retention features are add-ons (below).


### D) Add-on SKUs (attachable)
Add-ons are intended to be attachable to Hosted, Dedicated, or Private (subject to deployment compatibility). If a marketplace cannot express “attachable,” we list them as separate purchasable plans and enforce compatibility via entitlement checks.

| Customer-facing SKU name | Canonical code | Marketplace billing model | Marketplace dimensions (conceptual) | Internal meter mapping | Entitlements granted | Inclusions | Exclusions/notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compliance Bundle (Marketplace) | RI-MP-ADDON-COMPLIANCE-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Compliance Bundle (monthly) | meter.addon.compliance.month | compliance_bundle=true | Security documentation bundle access workflow, compliance feature flags (where applicable), and faster security review path | Does not confer customer compliance; evidence access may be gated and requires approved contacts. |
| Audit Log Export | RI-MP-ADDON-AUDIT-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Audit Export (monthly) | meter.addon.audit_export.month | audit_export=true | Export audit logs to customer SIEM/storage destinations supported by deployment mode | Availability varies by deployment type; enforce via entitlement compatibility matrix. |
| Extended Retention | RI-MP-ADDON-RETENTION-V2 | Contract (monthly) | Retention Extension (monthly) | meter.addon.retention_ext.month | retention_extended=true | Increased retention for logs/telemetry per policy | Does not override legal hold requirements; retention maxima depend on product constraints and published policy. |

Compatibility matrix (enforced by entitlement service):
- Compliance Bundle: Hosted ✅ / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅
- Audit Log Export: Hosted ⚠️ (limited) / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅
- Extended Retention: Hosted ✅ / Dedicated ✅ / Private ✅

(⚠️ = supported only for specific data types or regions; refer to the Security Packet and Data Handling doc.)

---

## Marketplace → internal mapping rules
### 1) Hosted token mapping
- Prompt tokens billed to **meter.hosted.tokens.prompt**.
- Completion tokens billed to **meter.hosted.tokens.completion**.
- Marketplace “per 1K tokens” dimensions are computed as: `ceil(tokens / 1000)` for billing units *only if required by marketplace*; internal raw tokens are still stored for analytics and reconciliation.

### 2) Embeddings units
- 1 embedding unit = **1,000 tokens embedded**, rounded up per request.
- Internal records store raw token count + computed units for marketplace reconciliation.

### 3) Dedicated reservation + overage
- The reservation dimension maps to **meter.dedicated.reservation.month**.
- If overage is enabled, GPU-hour usage maps to **meter.dedicated.gpu_hour** with a hardware_class attribute (e.g., h100, a100, l4).

### 4) Private license
- Platform license maps to **meter.private.platform.month**.
- If node-based licensing is enabled, node count maps to **meter.private.nodes.month**.

### 5) Add-ons
- Add-on SKUs are billed monthly and create entitlement flags consumed by:
  - Console UI toggles
  - API/feature gating
  - Security docs access workflow (where applicable)

---

## Inclusions/exclusions (standardized language blocks)
These blocks are used in the SKU descriptions, listing copy, and order form references.

### Hosted — inclusions
- Hosted API endpoints for supported inference tasks.
- Model version pinning and rollouts (where available in Hosted).
- Standard observability in Redwood Console.

### Hosted — exclusions
- Dedicated GPU pools, capacity reservations, or strict per-tenant isolation.
- Private networking (VPC peering) and on-prem distribution.

### Dedicated — inclusions
- Dedicated capacity allocation with reserved sizing.
- Dedicated throughput predictability and isolation.

### Dedicated — exclusions
- On-prem delivery.
- Customer-managed network perimeter (use Private).

### Private — inclusions
- Private deployment options (VPC and/or on-prem package as contracted).
- Enterprise security controls and configuration options.

### Private — exclusions
- Any statement of customer compliance certification.
- Customer hardware procurement.

---

## Backwards compatibility and migration
Legacy marketplace listings and SKUs will be maintained in a limited-support state during transition.

Plan:
1) Publish v2 SKUs in partner portals (AWS/GCP) alongside legacy.
2) New subscriptions default to v2.
3) Existing customers remain on legacy SKUs until:
   - renewal, or
   - customer-requested migration, or
   - required by marketplace deprecation timelines.
4) Ensure metering reconciliation supports both v1 and v2 identifiers during the overlap window.

Guardrails:
- Do not change billing semantics for an existing subscription without explicit customer approval.
- Canonical codes are immutable once launched.

---

## Open items / decisions needed (for final publish)
- Confirm whether Hosted Commit is represented as a pure “$ commit drawdown” vs bundled “token units” for each marketplace (depends on portal constraints).
- Confirm whether Dedicated Overages SKU is public or private-offer-only.
- Confirm final entitlement attribute schema for hardware_class + region for Dedicated pools.

Owners:
- SKU semantics + naming: Soojin Lee
- Meter mapping + catalog config: Cole Summers
- RevRec/terms validation: Emily Stone, Michael Grant
- Security add-on wording compatibility: Naomi Feldman

## Overview
This document defines Redwood Inference’s **Marketplace SKU Catalog v2** for AWS Marketplace and GCP Marketplace.

The catalog is designed to:
- Map cleanly to Redwood’s internal billing meters and entitlements (no “special marketplace-only plans” in product).
- Support enterprise procurement patterns (usage-based, commits, reserved capacity, and Private licensing).
- Reduce buyer confusion by aligning names across clouds: **Hosted** vs **Dedicated** vs **Private**, plus add-ons.

This is the source of truth for:
- SKU naming conventions
- Inclusions/exclusions
- Marketplace billing dimensions → internal meters
- Entitlement semantics used by provisioning and metering

---

## Scope
**In scope:**
- Hosted API (usage-based) SKUs
- Dedicated capacity (reserved) SKUs
- Private deployment (license) SKUs
- Compliance + observability add-ons sold via marketplace

**Out of scope (handled via private offers / order forms as needed):**
- Custom model onboarding fees
- One-time professional services
- Complex multi-year ramp schedules (documented in private offer templates)

---

## SKU naming conventions
We need names that work in:
- AWS Marketplace product/offer UIs (title length limits, readability)
- GCP Marketplace listings (SKU + plan naming constraints)
- Internal billing catalog (stable identifiers)

- “Redwood Inference Dedicated — Reserved GPU Capacity”
- “Redwood Inference Private — VPC/On‑prem License”
