Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Telemetry gaps cause disputed credits. Mitigation: 30-day staging telemetry verification window and manual reconciliation workflow.
- Risk: Overly aggressive SLOs increase infra cost. Mitigation: SLO calibration experiments in pilot and automated suggestion engine in Optimize.
- Risk: Complexity for customers to interpret credits. Mitigation: clear invoice line items, monthly compliance CSV, and customer success onboarding sessions.

Open questions
- Should host-level per-token discounts be applied inside bundles for extremely high utilization (>80%)? (Finance input required)
- How to support multi-tenant Dedicated pools where multiple customers share a pool? Need an isolation/measurement model.

Appendix A — Example monthly compliance CSV columns
- bundle_id, month, pool_id, model, region, total_requests, pct_meeting_p95, pct_meeting_p99, computed_credit_percent, credit_amount_usd

Appendix B — Sample contract clause (billing remedy)
- \"Redwood will use observable telemetry to compute monthly SLO compliance. If the Bundle fails to meet the agreed p95 latency target, Customer will receive a credit applied as a reduction to the next invoice as described in the Financial Remedies Model.\"

Next steps
- Align pricing bands with finance and legal by 2025-11-30.
- Begin staging telemetry instrumentation and billing pipeline prototypes by 2025-12-15.
- Confirm pilot customers and schedule kickoff by 2026-01-15.

References
- Packaged tiers & credit conversion guidelines (internal)
- Redwood Optimize product overview (internal)
- Dedicated capacity reference architecture (internal)
text:
Overview

Problem statement:
Redwood customers pay for capacity and tokens, but many enterprise teams ask for predictable cost behavior tied to operational outcomes (latency SLOs, error budgets, and availability). Current unit-based pricing does not capture the value of: (1) guaranteed latency/availability for critical routes, (2) bundled tooling (Optimize features, caching, and rollout controls), and (3) simpler accounting for reserved capacity + burst. This PRD proposes an SLO-backed pricing model and bundle constructs that map directly to customer SLAs and internal cost/accounting needs.

Objective (what success looks like)
- Launch 3 new bundled SKUs that combine reserved capacity, SLO guarantees, and optional Optimize features by Q2 2026.
- Achieve 20% uplift in MRR from existing Dedicated customers migrating to SLO Bundles within 12 months of GA.
- Reduce billing disputes for latency incidents by 60% in pilot customers (tracked via billing-support ticket tags).

Non-goals
- This PRD does not change hosted API per-token public pricing. It targets Redwood Dedicated and Private customers and reserved-capacity commitments.
- It does not include fine-grained per-prompt quality/loss adjustment billing (that is future work).

Target customers
- Enterprise product teams requiring contractual latency SLAs (e.g., e-commerce search, realtime assistants).
- Platform teams buying Dedicated pools who want simplified internal chargebacks.
- High-volume customers seeking operational credits for Optimize and cache features.

Success metrics
1. Commercial: number of customers on SLO Bundles, conversion rate from existing Dedicated contracts, ARR growth attributable to bundles.
2. Operational: mean time to billing dispute triage, percent of requests meeting SLOs per billing cycle, utilization of bundled Optimize credits.
3. Product: number of rollout features enabled via bundles, NPS lift for customers on bundles.

User stories
- As a Platform lead, I want a contract that guarantees 95th percentile token latency <= 120ms for a dedicated pool so I can commit internal SLAs to product teams.
- As a Finance lead, I want a single invoice line for reserved capacity + Optimize credits to simplify chargeback.
- As an SRE, I want automatic fallback policies and documented incident remedies included in the contract so customers know how Redwood will remediate SLO slippage.

Requirements
Functional requirements:
1) SKU model: create three SKUs — Base (SLO-Lite), Performance (SLO-Standard), Enterprise (SLO-Premium).
2) Each SKU must expose a clear set of SLOs (latency p95 & p99, availability, token throughput) and corresponding financial remedies (credits).
3) Bundles include a configurable number of Optimize credits (tokens of Optimize actions: auto-batching suggestions, model-quantization assessments, and caching reserved units).
4) Contracts must enumerate: model families covered, maximum sequence length, region(s), and supported fallback variants.
5) Billing system must issue pro-rated credits automatically when SLO breaches are validated.
6) Console UI: new Billing > Bundles page to create and manage bundles, view SLO compliance charts, and export monthly compliance reports.

Non-functional requirements:
- Telemetry: system must capture per-request latency, model variant, routing policy, and pool id for all requests covered by bundles (retention 12 months).
- Reporting latency: SLO compliance dashboard must refresh hourly and allow export of monthly CSVs for billing reconciliation.
- Security: contract metadata and bundle assignments must respect RBAC and be available to customer-admins only.

Pricing constructs and examples
Principles:
- Bundle price = reserved-capacity fee + SLO premium + amortized Optimize credits.
- SLO premium scales with the aggressiveness of latency targets and criticality (SLO-Premium > SLO-Standard > SLO-Lite).
- Clear, auditable metrics must tie SLO breaches to financial remedies.

Example pricing table (illustrative; final commercial pricing TBD):

| SKU | Pool GPU Equivalent | Monthly base | SLO p95 (tokens/ms) | Optimize credits / month | Expected effective per-1M tokens |
|-----|---------------------|--------------:|--------------------:|--------------------------:|-------------------------------:|
| SLO-Lite | 1 x A100 | $18,000 | p95 <= 220ms | 1,000 | $850 |
| SLO-Standard | 2 x A100 | $32,000 | p95 <= 120ms | 5,000 | $720 |
| SLO-Premium | 4 x A100 | $60,000 | p95 <= 60ms | 20,000 | $640 |

Financial remedies model
- For each calendar month, compute SLO compliance window and percent of covered requests meeting the SLO. If compliance < 99.9% for a given p95 target, issue tiered credits: 0.5% invoice credit for compliance in [98.0%,99.9%), 2% for [95.0%,97.99%), and 5% for <95%. Credits are applied automatically and visible in the Bundle invoice line item.

Telemetry and validation plan
Required events and attributes (minimum):
- request.id, timestamp, pool_id, model.name, model.variant, seq_len, tokens_generated, latency_ms, route_policy, response_status, cache_hit (bool).
- Aggregations: hourly p50/p95/p99 by pool_id + model.variant + route_policy.
Validation flow:
1) Billing runs nightly to compute hourly aggregates;
2) For each bundle contract, compute monthly compliance and generate preliminary credit amount;
3) SRE/finance review automated preliminary credits (3 business days) before finalizing;
4) Customer receives notification and invoice adjustment at month end.

Console & API changes
- API: POST /v1/bundles to create bundle, GET /v1/bundles/{id}/compliance returns monthly compliance CSV link. Example payload snippet:

{
  \"bundle_name\": \"Acme-Performance-2026\",
  \"pool_id\": \"pool-xyz-123\",
  \"sku\": \"SLO-Standard\",
  \"models\": [\"redwood-rlm-v2\", \"llama-3-sim\"],
  \"regions\": [\"us-west1\"],
  \"start_date\": \"2026-01-01\"
}

- UI: New Bundles management page under Billing. Key screens: Bundle detail, SLO compliance chart (hourly p95 heatmap), Optimize credit consumption, Invoice preview.

Rollout plan
Phase 0 — Design & agreements (Q4 2025)
- Finalize SKUs and credit mechanics with finance and legal. Prototype compliance pipeline in staging.
- Obtain pilot commitments from 3 existing Dedicated customers.

Phase 1 — Pilot (Q1 2026)
- Launch pilots with 3 customers (one per SKU). Validate telemetry, credit calculations, and customer reporting.
- Collect pricing feedback and refine commercial remedies.

Phase 2 — GA (Q2 2026)
- GA launch to new Dedicated contracts and Private deployments. Update sales collateral and renewals playbooks.
- Migrate existing high-value Dedicated customers via opt-in upgrade offers.

Phase 3 — Expansion (H2 2026)
- Add per-route SLO attachments, support for multi-region SLA stitching, and small-batch spot pooling.

Migration and customer communications
- Customers on legacy Dedicated will receive tailored transition offers: price-match for first 6 months or pro-rated credits.
- Sales playbook includes: comparison worksheet, step-by-step migration checklist, and a red-team Q&A for legal.

Ownership and stakeholders
- Product: Maya Chen (PRD owner)
- Eng/Runtime: Ethan Park (implementation lead)
- Eng/Billing: Diego Alvarez (billing integration)
- Finance: Priya Natarajan (pricing & legal prep)
- SRE: Arjun Rao (telemetry & validation)

Risks and mitigations
- Risk: Telemetry gaps cause disputed credits. Mitigation: 30-day staging telemetry verification window and manual reconciliation workflow.
- Risk: Overly aggressive SLOs increase infra cost. Mitigation: SLO calibration experiments in pilot and automated suggestion engine in Optimize.
- Risk: Complexity for customers to interpret credits. Mitigation: clear invoice line items, monthly compliance CSV, and customer success onboarding sessions.

Open questions
- Should host-level per-token discounts be applied inside bundles for extremely high utilization (>80%)? (Finance input required)
- How to support multi-tenant Dedicated pools where multiple customers share a pool? Need an isolation/measurement model.

Appendix A — Example monthly compliance CSV columns
- bundle_id, month, pool_id, model, region, total_requests, pct_meeting_p95, pct_meeting_p99, computed_credit_percent, credit_amount_usd

Appendix B — Sample contract clause (billing remedy)
- \"Redwood will use observable telemetry to compute monthly SLO compliance. If the Bundle fails to meet the agreed p95 latency target, Customer will receive a credit applied as a reduction to the next invoice as described in the Financial Remedies Model.\"

Next steps
- Align pricing bands with finance and legal by 2025-11-30.
- Begin staging telemetry instrumentation and billing pipeline prototypes by 2025-12-15.
- Confirm pilot customers and schedule kickoff by 2026-01-15.

References
- Packaged tiers & credit conversion guidelines (internal)
- Redwood Optimize product overview (internal)
- Dedicated capacity reference architecture (internal)

source.title: Design Spec: Optimization Config Versioning + Safe Apply/Rollback
source.author: Benji Okafor
source.body:
## Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end design for **Optimization Config versioning** and **safe apply/rollback workflows** across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Optimization Configs control runtime optimization behavior such as:
- batching heuristics
- caching toggles and parameters
- quantization profile selection

The design introduces immutable config versions, scoped rollouts with gating signals, and one-click rollback with strong auditability.

## Goals
1. **Immutable version history** for all optimization changes (who/what/when, diffable).
2. **Safe apply** via staged rollout (tenant/model/region scope) with gates:
   - SLO gates (5xx + p95/p99 latency)
   - saturation/capacity signals (GPU utilization, queueing)
   - eval/quality gates for quantization changes
3. **One-click rollback** (Console + API), with well-defined semantics in a distributed runtime.
4. **Idempotent runtime config applier** and protections against apply/rollback race conditions.
5. **Works across deployment modes**:
   - Hosted + Dedicated (managed by Redwood control plane)
   - Private (same version model + operator-friendly pin/rollback integration)

## Non-goals (initial release)
- Fully automatic rollback for all customers without human confirmation.
- Cross-subsystem transactional rollback (e.g., simultaneous rollback of unrelated routing policies).
- Replacing existing general “deployment” rollouts; this system is specific to optimization configs.

## Terminology
- **Optimization Config (logical)**: The conceptual configuration for a scope (e.g., tenant A + model X + region us-east).
- **Version**: An immutable snapshot of the Optimization Config payload plus metadata.
- **Active version**: The version currently targeted for a scope.
- **Rollout**: A staged transition from one version to another, with monitoring and gate evaluation.
- **Promotion**: Marking a version as “known-good” for a broader scope or as a recommended baseline.
- **Scope**: A tuple defining where the config applies.

## High-level architecture
Components:
1. **Optimization Config Service (control plane)**
   - Stores immutable versions
   - Computes diffs
   - Validates schema + semantics
   - Orchestrates rollouts and rollbacks
   - Emits audit events
2. **Canary/Rollout Controller**
   - Progresses staged rollout
   - Evaluates gating signals
   - Pauses, fails, or completes rollout
3. **Serving Runtime (data plane)**
   - Fetches active config version for a scope
   - Applies config idempotently on workers
   - Exposes metrics tagged with config_version
4. **Console UX**
   - Version history + diff view
   - Safe apply flow and progress view
   - Rollback button with blast radius summary + confirmation

### Data/control flow (Hosted/Dedicated)
1. Operator creates a new version (draft) in Console/API.
2. Service runs validation (schema + semantic).
3. Operator starts safe apply: creates a Rollout from current active version → target version.
4. Rollout Controller stages traffic exposure (canary percent or shard selection).
5. Gates read metrics and eval results. If pass, rollout progresses; otherwise pauses/fails.
6. When complete, the service sets the scope’s Active pointer to the target version.
7. Rollback sets Active pointer back to prior version and triggers the same apply pipeline in reverse (with additional safeguards).

### Data/control flow (Private)
- Versions exist in the Private control plane, but operators may also **pin** a version through installer/Helm/Terraform values.
- Rollback is implemented as a controlled update of the pinned version (or active pointer), plus a verification checklist.

## Requirements and invariants
### Version immutability
- Version payloads are immutable once created.
- Metadata fields like labels and “promotion status” are mutable, but the content hash (payload) is not.

### Deterministic application
- A given worker applies config version V deterministically.
- The runtime must be safe under retries: applying V multiple times must converge to the same state.

### Eventually consistent, but never “unknown”
- During rollout, workers may temporarily run a mix of versions.
- The system must always be able to report:
  - the **desired/active** version for a scope
  - the **observed** versions on workers (best-effort)
  - the **rollout state** and last gate evaluation

### Rollback safety
- Rollback must be safe even when a rollout is mid-flight.
- Race condition policy: a scope has a single monotonic “generation” such that apply/rollback operations cannot interleave silently.

## Scope model
A scope defines where a config applies.

**Scope key fields** (v1):
- tenant_id (required)
- model_id (required)
- region (optional; defaults to “all regions for tenant/model”)
- deployment_mode (Hosted | Dedicated | Private) (required)

Notes:
- Dedicated is treated like Hosted from a control plane perspective, but may have additional isolation keys (e.g., dedicated_pool_id). We keep that as an optional field in v1.
- Private may use customer-provided identifiers; we still store tenant_id/model_id as Redwood-level logical identifiers.

## Data model
This section describes the logical entities and suggested storage schema. Exact DDL lives in ENG-4821 and corresponding migrations.

### Entity: OptimizationConfigVersion
Represents an immutable configuration snapshot.

Fields:
- version_id: string (ULID)
- scope_key: string (canonicalized tuple; see below)
- payload_json: string (the full config)
- payload_sha256: string
- parent_version_id: string (optional; used to build a chain)
- created_by: string (user/service principal)
- created_at: timestamp
- change_reason: string (operator-provided)
- source: string (console|api|automation)
- risk_level: string (low|medium|high; computed + overrideable)
- annotations: string (freeform; optional)

Canonical scope_key format:
- "tenant:{tenant_id}|model:{model_id}|region:{region_or_all}|mode:{deployment_mode}|pool:{pool_id_or_none}"

### Entity: OptimizationConfigPointer (Active)
Represents the currently active (desired) version for a scope.

Fields:
- scope_key: string (PK)
- active_version_id: string
- generation: int (monotonic; increments on every apply/rollback)
- updated_by: string
- updated_at: timestamp

Invariant:
- (scope_key, generation) strictly increases with each pointer change.

### Entity: OptimizationConfigRollout
Represents an apply operation (including rollback).

Fields:
- rollout_id: string (ULID)
- scope_key: string
- from_version_id: string
- to_version_id: string
- type: string (apply|rollback)
- status: string (queued|running|paused|failed|completed|canceled)
- canary_strategy: string (percent|shard)
- canary_steps: string (serialized list; e.g., "1,5,25,50,100")
- step_duration_seconds: int
- gateset_id: string (references default/override gate rules)
- started_at: timestamp
- completed_at: timestamp (nullable)
- created_by: string
- created_at: timestamp
- failure_reason: string (nullable)

### Entity: GateEvaluationRecord
Stores the rollout’s gate evaluations for observability and audit.

Fields:
- rollout_id: string
- step: string (e.g., "5%")
- evaluated_at: timestamp
- result: string (pass|fail|inconclusive)
- details: string (human-readable summary + links)

### Config payload schema (v1)
Payload is a JSON object with top-level sections:
- batching
- caching
- quantization
- misc

Example (illustrative):
- batching.max_batch_tokens
- batching.max_concurrent_sequences
- caching.enabled
- caching.prefix_cache.max_entries
- quantization.profile_id
- quantization.fallback_profile_id

Schema validation rules:
- strict JSON schema validation
- unknown fields rejected by default (forward-compatible via explicit schema version)

Semantic validation examples:
- batching.max_batch_tokens must be within safe bounds per model family
- caching.enabled requires runtime capability flag for the model/region
- quantization.profile_id must exist and be compatible with model_id

## Versioning semantics
### Creating a version
- A new version is created for a specific scope_key.
- parent_version_id defaults to the current active version for the scope (if any).
- A version can be created even if it is never applied (e.g., abandoned experiments).

### Promotion
Promotion is metadata indicating broader confidence.

We support two promotion concepts:
1. **Known-good label** for a scope: "known_good" tag for quick selection.
2. **Recommended baseline** for a model family: implemented as a separate pointer type in a future phase.

For v1, we implement only (1) as metadata (no extra pointer), to avoid complexity.

## API surface
APIs are provided via the Optimization Config Service. Some endpoints are internal-only at first; customer-facing equivalents may be added in later iterations.

### Authentication and authorization
- All endpoints require auth.
- RBAC checks map to permissions:
  - optimization_config.version.create
  - optimization_config.version.validate
  - optimization_config.rollout.start
  - optimization_config.rollout.pause/resume/cancel
  - optimization_config.rollback.execute
  - optimization_config.version.promote (metadata)

See: RBAC policy bundle updates and PM-902.

### Endpoints (v1)
**Versions**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/versions
  - create a new version for a scope
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions?scope_key=... (paginated)
  - list versions
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}
  - fetch version details
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}/diff?base_version_id=...
  - server-side diff (base defaults to parent_version_id)
- POST /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}/validate
  - runs schema + semantic checks; returns errors/warnings

**Active pointer**
- GET /v1/optimization-config/active?scope_key=...
  - returns active_version_id + generation

**Rollouts / safe apply**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts
  - create rollout (apply) for scope from active → target
- GET /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}
  - rollout status + last gate result
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:pause
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:resume
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:cancel

**Rollback**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollbacks
  - creates a rollout of type=rollback with to_version_id = specified prior version
  - server enforces "rollback target must be an existing version for the same scope_key"

### Response semantics and idempotency
- All create endpoints accept optional idempotency_key.
- Rollout creation is idempotent by (scope_key, to_version_id, type, created_by) within a short window to prevent accidental duplicates.

### Errors and edge cases
- 409 Conflict if:
  - a rollout is already running for the scope and the operation would overlap
  - generation has moved (stale client)
- 422 Unprocessable Entity for validation failures
- 403 Forbidden for RBAC failures

## Console workflows
Console screens covered by DES-211/212/213.

### 1) Version history (timeline)
For a selected scope (tenant/model/region):
- show active version
- list prior versions with:
  - created_by, created_at
  - change_reason
  - status tags: "validated", "applied", "failed rollout", "known-good"

### 2) Diff view
- server-side diff with field-level highlighting
- risky fields emphasized (quantization.profile_id, batching.* ceilings)
- show computed “risk_level” with rationale

### 3) Safe apply
Flow:
1. Select a version (validated required for high-risk changes; optional for low-risk)
2. Configure rollout:
   - canary strategy (percent default)
   - steps (defaults: 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
   - step duration (default 10 minutes)
   - gateset (default recommended)
3. Start rollout
4. Monitor progress:
   - gate results per step
   - links to dashboard panels
5. Completion: version becomes active for scope

### 4) Rollback
Rollback entry points:
- from a specific version details page (“Rollback to this version”)
- from the active version card (“Rollback to previous active”)

Confirmation modal includes:
- blast radius summary (scope)
- from_version → to_version
- warning if rollback crosses quantization profile boundaries
- “type to confirm” for high-risk scopes

After rollback starts:
- Console shows rollout state (rollback is just a rollout type)
- links to rollback runbook and dashboards

## Interaction with runtime/apply pipeline
This design assumes the runtime already has a config apply pipeline that can be adapted for version fetching and idempotent application.

### Runtime responsibilities
- Periodically fetch (or subscribe to) active pointer for each scope:
  - active_version_id
  - generation
- Fetch the corresponding version payload
- Apply it using an idempotent applier
- Emit metrics tagged by:
  - tenant_id, model_id, region
  - config_version_id
  - rollout_id (when applicable)

### Fetch model
We support two modes:
1. Pull-based:
   - workers poll active pointer (fast path cached; low TTL)
2. Push-assisted:
   - control plane emits an event "optimization_config.changed" to notify runtime to refresh

v1 uses pull-based with push-assisted as an optimization (eventing is already planned in PR-20063).

### Apply checkpoints and idempotency
To avoid mixed/partial state on a worker, apply is modeled as a small transaction:
1. Validate payload compatibility with runtime build (capabilities)
2. Apply batching params
3. Apply caching params
4. Apply quantization selection
5. Commit applied_version_id + generation to a local state file / in-memory register

If any step fails:
- worker reports apply_failure metric including failing component
- worker remains on previous applied version (or enters safe fallback mode depending on component)

### Handling apply/rollback races
We use the pointer generation field:
- When a worker observes (active_version_id, generation), it applies only if generation is newer than its last applied generation.
- If it is in the middle of applying generation N and sees generation N+1, it aborts and restarts from latest desired state.

Control plane policy:
- Only one rollout can be RUNNING per scope_key.
- Rollback requests against an in-flight rollout:
  - allowed only if requester has rollback permission
  - rollout controller cancels/pauses the in-flight rollout and starts a rollback rollout with a new generation

## Rollout controller and gating
### Rollout progression
For percent-based canary:
- Controller selects traffic exposure increments by adjusting the request router policy for that scope.
- At each step:
  - wait for step_duration
  - evaluate gates over the step window (with a warmup exclusion window; default first 2 minutes ignored)
  - if pass → proceed
  - if fail → pause and require operator action (v1)

For shard-based canary:
- Controller selects a fixed set of worker shards or sub-regions (Dedicated pools) for exposure.

### Default gates (v1)
Values are defaults; ADR defines final thresholds.

SLO gates (per scope):
- 5xx rate: must not exceed baseline + 0.20% absolute for two consecutive evaluation windows
- p95 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.15
- p99 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.20

Saturation gates:
- GPU utilization: must not exceed 92% sustained at canary step (to avoid queueing collapse)
- queue_delay_p95: must not exceed 2x baseline

Quantization quality gates:
- If quantization.profile_id changes, require eval harness signal:
  - regression_score <= threshold for selected prompt sets
  - inconclusive results pause rollout

### Gate evaluation inputs
- Metrics from the runtime/observability stack tagged with config_version_id.
- Optional perf-canary job results for controlled synthetic load.
- Eval harness results for quantization changes.

### Baselines
Baseline is computed as:
- current active version performance during the last 24 hours
- adjusted by time-of-day seasonality where available

v1 baseline uses a simpler approach:
- median of last 6 hours for the same scope and traffic class

## Audit logging
Every lifecycle action emits an audit event with:
- action (create|validate|apply_start|apply_pause|apply_resume|apply_complete|rollback_start|rollback_complete|promote)
- actor identity (user/service)
- scope_key
- version_id (target)
- from_version_id (when applicable)
- rollout_id
- request_id
- timestamp

Event naming and required fields are defined in the audit log specification page.

## Failure modes and mitigation
1. **Validation misses a semantic incompatibility**
   - Mitigation: runtime capability checks; rollout gates detect regressions; rollback is fast.
2. **Rollout controller stuck (no progression)**
   - Mitigation: watchdog that fails rollout if no state change for N minutes; operator can cancel.
3. **Mixed worker state persists**
   - Mitigation: generation-based convergence; worker reports last_applied_version/generation; controller shows convergence metric.
4. **Metrics tagging missing config_version**
   - Mitigation: enforce tag presence in runtime instrumentation; gate evaluation marks “inconclusive” if tags absent.
5. **Rollback makes things worse**
   - Mitigation: rollback is itself gated and can be paused; operators can rollback again to earlier known-good.

## Backward compatibility and migration
Existing (pre-versioning) optimization configs will be migrated as follows:
- For each existing scope, create an initial OptimizationConfigVersion v0 with:
  - created_by = "migration"
  - change_reason = "bootstrap from pre-versioning config"
- Set active pointer to v0.

During migration window:
- old config store remains read-only
- runtime reads active pointer only

## Observability
Required dashboards and metrics:
- Rollout progress (step, percent, status)
- Error rate and latency split by config_version
- Apply failures (by component: batching/caching/quantization)
- Convergence: % of workers reporting desired generation
- Audit annotations on charts (apply/rollback events)

## Security considerations
- RBAC enforcement at API boundary (no direct DB writes).
- Rollback requires elevated permission for production tenants.
- All events are auditable and exportable to SIEM.
- Private deployments must support local audit retention and optional export.

## Implementation plan (summary)
- Control plane: Version + Pointer tables, CRUD, diff, validation.
- Rollout controller: staged policy changes + gate hooks.
- Runtime: generation-aware fetch, idempotent apply, metrics tags.
- Console: history, diff, safe apply wizard, rollback flow.
- E2E: validate→canary→promote→rollback, with failure injection.

## Open questions
1. Should we allow **cross-scope promotions** in v1 (e.g., promote from canary region to all regions)? Current plan: not in v1.
2. How do we represent Dedicated pool scoping cleanly without ballooning scope_key variants?
3. What is the best default baseline window for gates in highly bursty workloads?
4. Quantization eval noise: when to “pause” vs “fail” on inconclusive signals?

## Appendix A: Example end-to-end sequence
1. Operator creates version V123 for tenant T1, model M1, region us-east.
2. Runs validation; receives warnings (risk=medium) but passes.
3. Starts rollout R55 from V120 → V123 with steps 1/5/25/50/100.
4. At 25% step, p95 latency exceeds threshold; rollout pauses.
5. Operator clicks Rollback to V120; rollback rollout R56 starts.
6. Active pointer updated to V120 with generation increment.
7. Workers converge; dashboards show recovery; audit events recorded for both pause and rollback.

title: Design Spec: Optimization Config Versioning + Safe Apply/Rollback
preview:
## Summary
This document specifies the end-to-end design for **Optimization Config versioning** and **safe apply/rollback workflows** across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Optimization Configs control runtime optimization behavior such as:
- batching heuristics
- caching toggles and parameters
- quantization profile selection

The design introduces immutable config versions, scoped rollouts with gating signals, and one-click rollback with strong auditability.

## Goals
1. **Immutable version history** for all optimization changes (who/what/when, diffable).
2. **Safe apply** via staged rollout (tenant/model/region scope) with gates:
   - SLO gates (5xx + p95/p99 latency)
   - saturation/capacity signals (GPU utilization, queueing)
   - eval/quality gates for quantization changes
3. **One-click rollback** (Console + API), with well-defined semantics in a distributed runtime.
4. **Idempotent runtime config applier** and protections against apply/rollback race conditions.
5. **Works across deployment modes**:
   - Hosted + Dedicated (managed by Redwood control plane)
   - Private (same version model + operator-friendly pin/rollback integration)

## Non-goals (initial release)
- Fully automatic rollback for all customers without human confirmation.
- Cross-subsystem transactional rollback (e.g., simultaneous rollback of unrelated routing policies).
- Replacing existing general “deployment” rollouts; this system is specific to optimization configs.

## Terminology
- **Optimization Config (logical)**: The conceptual configuration for a scope (e.g., tenant A + model X + region us-east).
- **Version**: An immutable snapshot of the Optimization Config payload plus metadata.
- **Active version**: The version currently targeted for a scope.
- **Rollout**: A staged transition from one version to another, with monitoring and gate evaluation.
- **Promotion**: Marking a version as “known-good” for a broader scope or as a recommended baseline.
- **Scope**: A tuple defining where the config applies.

## High-level architecture
Components:
1. **Optimization Config Service (control plane)**
   - Stores immutable versions
   - Computes diffs
   - Validates schema + semantics
   - Orchestrates rollouts and rollbacks
   - Emits audit events
2. **Canary/Rollout Controller**
   - Progresses staged rollout
   - Evaluates gating signals
   - Pauses, fails, or completes rollout
3. **Serving Runtime (data plane)**
   - Fetches active config version for a scope
   - Applies config idempotently on workers
   - Exposes metrics tagged with config_version
4. **Console UX**
   - Version history + diff view
   - Safe apply flow and progress view
   - Rollback button with blast radius summary + confirmation

### Data/control flow (Hosted/Dedicated)
1. Operator creates a new version (draft) in Console/API.
2. Service runs validation (schema + semantic).
3. Operator starts safe apply: creates a Rollout from current active version → target version.
4. Rollout Controller stages traffic exposure (canary percent or shard selection).
5. Gates read metrics and eval results. If pass, rollout progresses; otherwise pauses/fails.
6. When complete, the service sets the scope’s Active pointer to the target version.
7. Rollback sets Active pointer back to prior version and triggers the same apply pipeline in reverse (with additional safeguards).

### Data/control flow (Private)
- Versions exist in the Private control plane, but operators may also **pin** a version through installer/Helm/Terraform values.
- Rollback is implemented as a controlled update of the pinned version (or active pointer), plus a verification checklist.

## Requirements and invariants
### Version immutability
- Version payloads are immutable once created.
- Metadata fields like labels and “promotion status” are mutable, but the content hash (payload) is not.

### Deterministic application
- A given worker applies config version V deterministically.
- The runtime must be safe under retries: applying V multiple times must converge to the same state.

### Eventually consistent, but never “unknown”
- During rollout, workers may temporarily run a mix of versions.
- The system must always be able to report:
  - the **desired/active** version for a scope
  - the **observed** versions on workers (best-effort)
  - the **rollout state** and last gate evaluation

### Rollback safety
- Rollback must be safe even when a rollout is mid-flight.
- Race condition policy: a scope has a single monotonic “generation” such that apply/rollback operations cannot interleave silently.

## Scope model
A scope defines where a config applies.

**Scope key fields** (v1):
- tenant_id (required)
- model_id (required)
- region (optional; defaults to “all regions for tenant/model”)
- deployment_mode (Hosted | Dedicated | Private) (required)

Notes:
- Dedicated is treated like Hosted from a control plane perspective, but may have additional isolation keys (e.g., dedicated_pool_id). We keep that as an optional field in v1.
- Private may use customer-provided identifiers; we still store tenant_id/model_id as Redwood-level logical identifiers.

## Data model
This section describes the logical entities and suggested storage schema. Exact DDL lives in ENG-4821 and corresponding migrations.

### Entity: OptimizationConfigVersion
Represents an immutable configuration snapshot.

Fields:
- version_id: string (ULID)
- scope_key: string (canonicalized tuple; see below)
- payload_json: string (the full config)
- payload_sha256: string
- parent_version_id: string (optional; used to build a chain)
- created_by: string (user/service principal)
- created_at: timestamp
- change_reason: string (operator-provided)
- source: string (console|api|automation)
- risk_level: string (low|medium|high; computed + overrideable)
- annotations: string (freeform; optional)

Canonical scope_key format:
- "tenant:{tenant_id}|model:{model_id}|region:{region_or_all}|mode:{deployment_mode}|pool:{pool_id_or_none}"

### Entity: OptimizationConfigPointer (Active)
Represents the currently active (desired) version for a scope.

Fields:
- scope_key: string (PK)
- active_version_id: string
- generation: int (monotonic; increments on every apply/rollback)
- updated_by: string
- updated_at: timestamp

Invariant:
- (scope_key, generation) strictly increases with each pointer change.

### Entity: OptimizationConfigRollout
Represents an apply operation (including rollback).

Fields:
- rollout_id: string (ULID)
- scope_key: string
- from_version_id: string
- to_version_id: string
- type: string (apply|rollback)
- status: string (queued|running|paused|failed|completed|canceled)
- canary_strategy: string (percent|shard)
- canary_steps: string (serialized list; e.g., "1,5,25,50,100")
- step_duration_seconds: int
- gateset_id: string (references default/override gate rules)
- started_at: timestamp
- completed_at: timestamp (nullable)
- created_by: string
- created_at: timestamp
- failure_reason: string (nullable)

### Entity: GateEvaluationRecord
Stores the rollout’s gate evaluations for observability and audit.

Fields:
- rollout_id: string
- step: string (e.g., "5%")
- evaluated_at: timestamp
- result: string (pass|fail|inconclusive)
- details: string (human-readable summary + links)

### Config payload schema (v1)
Payload is a JSON object with top-level sections:
- batching
- caching
- quantization
- misc

Example (illustrative):
- batching.max_batch_tokens
- batching.max_concurrent_sequences
- caching.enabled
- caching.prefix_cache.max_entries
- quantization.profile_id
- quantization.fallback_profile_id

Schema validation rules:
- strict JSON schema validation
- unknown fields rejected by default (forward-compatible via explicit schema version)

Semantic validation examples:
- batching.max_batch_tokens must be within safe bounds per model family
- caching.enabled requires runtime capability flag for the model/region
- quantization.profile_id must exist and be compatible with model_id

## Versioning semantics
### Creating a version
- A new version is created for a specific scope_key.
- parent_version_id defaults to the current active version for the scope (if any).
- A version can be created even if it is never applied (e.g., abandoned experiments).

### Promotion
Promotion is metadata indicating broader confidence.

We support two promotion concepts:
1. **Known-good label** for a scope: "known_good" tag for quick selection.
2. **Recommended baseline** for a model family: implemented as a separate pointer type in a future phase.

For v1, we implement only (1) as metadata (no extra pointer), to avoid complexity.

## API surface
APIs are provided via the Optimization Config Service. Some endpoints are internal-only at first; customer-facing equivalents may be added in later iterations.

### Authentication and authorization
- All endpoints require auth.
- RBAC checks map to permissions:
  - optimization_config.version.create
  - optimization_config.version.validate
  - optimization_config.rollout.start
  - optimization_config.rollout.pause/resume/cancel
  - optimization_config.rollback.execute
  - optimization_config.version.promote (metadata)

See: RBAC policy bundle updates and PM-902.

### Endpoints (v1)
**Versions**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/versions
  - create a new version for a scope
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions?scope_key=... (paginated)
  - list versions
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}
  - fetch version details
- GET /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}/diff?base_version_id=...
  - server-side diff (base defaults to parent_version_id)
- POST /v1/optimization-config/versions/{version_id}/validate
  - runs schema + semantic checks; returns errors/warnings

**Active pointer**
- GET /v1/optimization-config/active?scope_key=...
  - returns active_version_id + generation

**Rollouts / safe apply**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts
  - create rollout (apply) for scope from active → target
- GET /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}
  - rollout status + last gate result
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:pause
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:resume
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:cancel

**Rollback**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollbacks
  - creates a rollout of type=rollback with to_version_id = specified prior version
  - server enforces "rollback target must be an existing version for the same scope_key"

### Response semantics and idempotency
- All create endpoints accept optional idempotency_key.
- Rollout creation is idempotent by (scope_key, to_version_id, type, created_by) within a short window to prevent accidental duplicates.

### Errors and edge cases
- 409 Conflict if:
  - a rollout is already running for the scope and the operation would overlap
  - generation has moved (stale client)
- 422 Unprocessable Entity for validation failures
- 403 Forbidden for RBAC failures

## Console workflows
Console screens covered by DES-211/212/213.

### 1) Version history (timeline)
For a selected scope (tenant/model/region):
- show active version
- list prior versions with:
  - created_by, created_at
  - change_reason
  - status tags: "validated", "applied", "failed rollout", "known-good"

### 2) Diff view
- server-side diff with field-level highlighting
- risky fields emphasized (quantization.profile_id, batching.* ceilings)
- show computed “risk_level” with rationale

### 3) Safe apply
Flow:
1. Select a version (validated required for high-risk changes; optional for low-risk)
2. Configure rollout:
   - canary strategy (percent default)
   - steps (defaults: 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
   - step duration (default 10 minutes)
   - gateset (default recommended)
3. Start rollout
4. Monitor progress:
   - gate results per step
   - links to dashboard panels
5. Completion: version becomes active for scope

### 4) Rollback
Rollback entry points:
- from a specific version details page (“Rollback to this version”)
- from the active version card (“Rollback to previous active”)

Confirmation modal includes:
- blast radius summary (scope)
- from_version → to_version
- warning if rollback crosses quantization profile boundaries
- “type to confirm” for high-risk scopes

After rollback starts:
- Console shows rollout state (rollback is just a rollout type)
- links to rollback runbook and dashboards

## Interaction with runtime/apply pipeline
This design assumes the runtime already has a config apply pipeline that can be adapted for version fetching and idempotent application.

### Runtime responsibilities
- Periodically fetch (or subscribe to) active pointer for each scope:
  - active_version_id
  - generation
- Fetch the corresponding version payload
- Apply it using an idempotent applier
- Emit metrics tagged by:
  - tenant_id, model_id, region
  - config_version_id
  - rollout_id (when applicable)

### Fetch model
We support two modes:
1. Pull-based:
   - workers poll active pointer (fast path cached; low TTL)
2. Push-assisted:
   - control plane emits an event "optimization_config.changed" to notify runtime to refresh

v1 uses pull-based with push-assisted as an optimization (eventing is already planned in PR-20063).

### Apply checkpoints and idempotency
To avoid mixed/partial state on a worker, apply is modeled as a small transaction:
1. Validate payload compatibility with runtime build (capabilities)
2. Apply batching params
3. Apply caching params
4. Apply quantization selection
5. Commit applied_version_id + generation to a local state file / in-memory register

If any step fails:
- worker reports apply_failure metric including failing component
- worker remains on previous applied version (or enters safe fallback mode depending on component)

### Handling apply/rollback races
We use the pointer generation field:
- When a worker observes (active_version_id, generation), it applies only if generation is newer than its last applied generation.
- If it is in the middle of applying generation N and sees generation N+1, it aborts and restarts from latest desired state.

Control plane policy:
- Only one rollout can be RUNNING per scope_key.
- Rollback requests against an in-flight rollout:
  - allowed only if requester has rollback permission
  - rollout controller cancels/pauses the in-flight rollout and starts a rollback rollout with a new generation

## Rollout controller and gating
### Rollout progression
For percent-based canary:
- Controller selects traffic exposure increments by adjusting the request router policy for that scope.
- At each step:
  - wait for step_duration
  - evaluate gates over the step window (with a warmup exclusion window; default first 2 minutes ignored)
  - if pass → proceed
  - if fail → pause and require operator action (v1)

For shard-based canary:
- Controller selects a fixed set of worker shards or sub-regions (Dedicated pools) for exposure.

### Default gates (v1)
Values are defaults; ADR defines final thresholds.

SLO gates (per scope):
- 5xx rate: must not exceed baseline + 0.20% absolute for two consecutive evaluation windows
- p95 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.15
- p99 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.20

Saturation gates:
- GPU utilization: must not exceed 92% sustained at canary step (to avoid queueing collapse)
- queue_delay_p95: must not exceed 2x baseline

Quantization quality gates:
- If quantization.profile_id changes, require eval harness signal:
  - regression_score <= threshold for selected prompt sets
  - inconclusive results pause rollout

### Gate evaluation inputs
- Metrics from the runtime/observability stack tagged with config_version_id.
- Optional perf-canary job results for controlled synthetic load.
- Eval harness results for quantization changes.

### Baselines
Baseline is computed as:
- current active version performance during the last 24 hours
- adjusted by time-of-day seasonality where available

v1 baseline uses a simpler approach:
- median of last 6 hours for the same scope and traffic class

## Audit logging
Every lifecycle action emits an audit event with:
- action (create|validate|apply_start|apply_pause|apply_resume|apply_complete|rollback_start|rollback_complete|promote)
- actor identity (user/service)
- scope_key
- version_id (target)
- from_version_id (when applicable)
- rollout_id
- request_id
- timestamp

Event naming and required fields are defined in the audit log specification page.

## Failure modes and mitigation
1. **Validation misses a semantic incompatibility**
   - Mitigation: runtime capability checks; rollout gates detect regressions; rollback is fast.
2. **Rollout controller stuck (no progression)**
   - Mitigation: watchdog that fails rollout if no state change for N minutes; operator can cancel.
3. **Mixed worker state persists**
   - Mitigation: generation-based convergence; worker reports last_applied_version/generation; controller shows convergence metric.
4. **Metrics tagging missing config_version**
   - Mitigation: enforce tag presence in runtime instrumentation; gate evaluation marks “inconclusive” if tags absent.
5. **Rollback makes things worse**
   - Mitigation: rollback is itself gated and can be paused; operators can rollback again to earlier known-good.

## Backward compatibility and migration
Existing (pre-versioning) optimization configs will be migrated as follows:
- For each existing scope, create an initial OptimizationConfigVersion v0 with:
  - created_by = "migration"
  - change_reason = "bootstrap from pre-versioning config"
- Set active pointer to v0.

During migration window:
- old config store remains read-only
- runtime reads active pointer only

## Observability
Required dashboards and metrics:
- Rollout progress (step, percent, status)
- Error rate and latency split by config_version
- Apply failures (by component: batching/caching/quantization)
- Convergence: % of workers reporting desired generation
- Audit annotations on charts (apply/rollback events)

## Security considerations
- RBAC enforcement at API boundary (no direct DB writes).
- Rollback requires elevated permission for production tenants.
- All events are auditable and exportable to SIEM.
- Private deployments must support local audit retention and optional export.

## Implementation plan (summary)
- Control plane: Version + Pointer tables, CRUD, diff, validation.
- Rollout controller: staged policy changes + gate hooks.
- Runtime: generation-aware fetch, idempotent apply, metrics tags.
- Console: history, diff, safe apply wizard, rollback flow.
- E2E: validate→canary→promote→rollback, with failure injection.

## Open questions
1. Should we allow **cross-scope promotions** in v1 (e.g., promote from canary region to all regions)? Current plan: not in v1.
2. How do we represent Dedicated pool scoping cleanly without ballooning scope_key variants?
3. What is the best default baseline window for gates in highly bursty workloads?
4. Quantization eval noise: when to “pause” vs “fail” on inconclusive signals?

## Appendix A: Example end-to-end sequence
1. Operator creates version V123 for tenant T1, model M1, region us-east.
2. Runs validation; receives warnings (risk=medium) but passes.
3. Starts rollout R55 from V120 → V123 with steps 1/5/25/50/100.
4. At 25% step, p95 latency exceeds threshold; rollout pauses.
5. Operator clicks Rollback to V120; rollback rollout R56 starts.
6. Active pointer updated to V120 with generation increment.
7. Workers converge; dashboards show recovery; audit events recorded for both pause and rollback.

- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts
  - create rollout (apply) for scope from active → target
- GET /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}
  - rollout status + last gate result
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:pause
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:resume
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollouts/{rollout_id}:cancel

**Rollback**
- POST /v1/optimization-config/rollbacks
  - creates a rollout of type=rollback with to_version_id = specified prior version
  - server enforces "rollback target must be an existing version for the same scope_key"

### Response semantics and idempotency
- All create endpoints accept optional idempotency_key.
- Rollout creation is idempotent by (scope_key, to_version_id, type, created_by) within a short window to prevent accidental duplicates.

### Errors and edge cases
- 409 Conflict if:
  - a rollout is already running for the scope and the operation would overlap
  - generation has moved (stale client)
- 422 Unprocessable Entity for validation failures
- 403 Forbidden for RBAC failures

## Console workflows
Console screens covered by DES-211/212/213.

### 1) Version history (timeline)
For a selected scope (tenant/model/region):
- show active version
- list prior versions with:
  - created_by, created_at
  - change_reason
  - status tags: "validated", "applied", "failed rollout", "known-good"

### 2) Diff view
- server-side diff with field-level highlighting
- risky fields emphasized (quantization.profile_id, batching.* ceilings)
- show computed “risk_level” with rationale

### 3) Safe apply
Flow:
1. Select a version (validated required for high-risk changes; optional for low-risk)
2. Configure rollout:
   - canary strategy (percent default)
   - steps (defaults: 1% → 5% → 25% → 50% → 100%)
   - step duration (default 10 minutes)
   - gateset (default recommended)
3. Start rollout
4. Monitor progress:
   - gate results per step
   - links to dashboard panels
5. Completion: version becomes active for scope

### 4) Rollback
Rollback entry points:
- from a specific version details page (“Rollback to this version”)
- from the active version card (“Rollback to previous active”)

Confirmation modal includes:
- blast radius summary (scope)
- from_version → to_version
- warning if rollback crosses quantization profile boundaries
- “type to confirm” for high-risk scopes

After rollback starts:
- Console shows rollout state (rollback is just a rollout type)
- links to rollback runbook and dashboards

## Interaction with runtime/apply pipeline
This design assumes the runtime already has a config apply pipeline that can be adapted for version fetching and idempotent application.

### Runtime responsibilities
- Periodically fetch (or subscribe to) active pointer for each scope:
  - active_version_id
  - generation
- Fetch the corresponding version payload
- Apply it using an idempotent applier
- Emit metrics tagged by:
  - tenant_id, model_id, region
  - config_version_id
  - rollout_id (when applicable)

### Fetch model
We support two modes:
1. Pull-based:
   - workers poll active pointer (fast path cached; low TTL)
2. Push-assisted:
   - control plane emits an event "optimization_config.changed" to notify runtime to refresh

v1 uses pull-based with push-assisted as an optimization (eventing is already planned in PR-20063).

### Apply checkpoints and idempotency
To avoid mixed/partial state on a worker, apply is modeled as a small transaction:
1. Validate payload compatibility with runtime build (capabilities)
2. Apply batching params
3. Apply caching params
4. Apply quantization selection
5. Commit applied_version_id + generation to a local state file / in-memory register

If any step fails:
- worker reports apply_failure metric including failing component
- worker remains on previous applied version (or enters safe fallback mode depending on component)

### Handling apply/rollback races
We use the pointer generation field:
- When a worker observes (active_version_id, generation), it applies only if generation is newer than its last applied generation.
- If it is in the middle of applying generation N and sees generation N+1, it aborts and restarts from latest desired state.

Control plane policy:
- Only one rollout can be RUNNING per scope_key.
- Rollback requests against an in-flight rollout:
  - allowed only if requester has rollback permission
  - rollout controller cancels/pauses the in-flight rollout and starts a rollback rollout with a new generation

## Rollout controller and gating
### Rollout progression
For percent-based canary:
- Controller selects traffic exposure increments by adjusting the request router policy for that scope.
- At each step:
  - wait for step_duration
  - evaluate gates over the step window (with a warmup exclusion window; default first 2 minutes ignored)
  - if pass → proceed
  - if fail → pause and require operator action (v1)

For shard-based canary:
- Controller selects a fixed set of worker shards or sub-regions (Dedicated pools) for exposure.

### Default gates (v1)
Values are defaults; ADR defines final thresholds.

SLO gates (per scope):
- 5xx rate: must not exceed baseline + 0.20% absolute for two consecutive evaluation windows
- p95 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.15
- p99 latency: must not exceed baseline * 1.20

Saturation gates:
- GPU utilization: must not exceed 92% sustained at canary step (to avoid queueing collapse)
- queue_delay_p95: must not exceed 2x baseline

Quantization quality gates:
- If quantization.profile_id changes, require eval harness signal:
  - regression_score <= threshold for selected prompt sets
  - inconclusive results pause rollout

### Gate evaluation inputs
- Metrics from the runtime/observability stack tagged with config_version_id.
- Optional perf-canary job results for controlled synthetic load.
- Eval harness results for quantization changes.

### Baselines
Baseline is computed as:
- current active version performance during the last 24 hours
- adjusted by time-of-day seasonality where available

v1 baseline uses a simpler approach:
- median of last 6 hours for the same scope and traffic class

## Audit logging
Every lifecycle action emits an audit event with:
- action (create|validate|apply_start|apply_pause|apply_resume|apply_complete|rollback_start|rollback_complete|promote)
- actor identity (user/service)
- scope_key
- version_id (target)
- from_version_id (when applicable)
- rollout_id
- request_id
- timestamp

Event naming and required fields are defined in the audit log specification page.

## Failure modes and mitigation
1. **Validation misses a semantic incompatibility**
   - Mitigation: runtime capability checks; rollout gates detect regressions; rollback is fast.
2. **Rollout controller stuck (no progression)**
   - Mitigation: watchdog that fails rollout if no state change for N minutes; operator can cancel.
3. **Mixed worker state persists**
   - Mitigation: generation-based convergence; worker reports last_applied_version/generation; controller shows convergence metric.
4. **Metrics tagging missing config_version**
   - Mitigation: enforce tag presence in runtime instrumentation; gate evaluation marks “inconclusive” if tags absent.
5. **Rollback makes things worse**
   - Mitigation: rollback is itself gated and can be paused; operators can rollback again to earlier known-good.

## Backward compatibility and migration
Existing (pre-versioning) optimization configs will be migrated as follows:
- For each existing scope, create an initial OptimizationConfigVersion v0 with:
  - created_by = "migration"
  - change_reason = "bootstrap from pre-versioning config"
- Set active pointer to v0.

During migration window:
- old config store remains read-only
- runtime reads active pointer only

## Observability
Required dashboards and metrics:
- Rollout progress (step, percent, status)
- Error rate and latency split by config_version
- Apply failures (by component: batching/caching/quantization)
- Convergence: % of workers reporting desired generation
- Audit annotations on charts (apply/rollback events)

## Security considerations
- RBAC enforcement at API boundary (no direct DB writes).
- Rollback requires elevated permission for production tenants.
- All events are auditable and exportable to SIEM.
- Private deployments must support local audit retention and optional export.

## Implementation plan (summary)
- Control plane: Version + Pointer tables, CRUD, diff, validation.
- Rollout controller: staged policy changes + gate hooks.
- Runtime: generation-aware fetch, idempotent apply, metrics tags.
- Console: history, diff, safe apply wizard, rollback flow.
- E2E: validate→canary→promote→rollback, with failure injection.

## Open questions
1. Should we allow **cross-scope promotions** in v1 (e.g., promote from canary region to all regions)? Current plan: not in v1.
2. How do we represent Dedicated pool scoping cleanly without ballooning scope_key variants?
3. What is the best default baseline window for gates in highly bursty workloads?
4. Quantization eval noise: when to “pause” vs “fail” on inconclusive signals?

## Appendix A: Example end-to-end sequence
1. Operator creates version V123 for tenant T1, model M1, region us-east.
2. Runs validation; receives warnings (risk=medium) but passes.
3. Starts rollout R55 from V120 → V123 with steps 1/5/25/50/100.
4. At 25% step, p95 latency exceeds threshold; rollout pauses.
5. Operator clicks Rollback to V120; rollback rollout R56 starts.
6. Active pointer updated to V120 with generation increment.
7. Workers converge; dashboards show recovery; audit events recorded for both pause and rollback.

source.title: Console UX Spec: Model Change History & Release Notes (Rollouts/A-B)
source.author: Kenji Nakamura
source.content:
h1. Console UX Spec: Model Change History & Release Notes (Rollouts/A-B)

h2. Summary
This document specifies the Console user experience for (1) per-model change history and (2) customer-visible release notes as they appear during rollouts (canary and A/B). It defines the information architecture, primary screens, filtering behavior (deployment/region/workspace/time), RBAC handling for public vs internal fields, and key interaction details.

This spec is intentionally product+design focused. API contracts and schema details are referenced but not re-defined here.

h2. Goals
* Provide a clear, trustworthy timeline of model/version changes that customers can use to answer: “What changed?”, “When did it change for my deployment/region?”, “How risky is it?”, and “How do I roll back?”
* Make release notes discoverable in-context of rollouts (canary/A-B) so users can validate changes before broad promotion.
* Enable Support/CS and internal admins to see richer internal notes and audit context without risk of leaking internal-only data.
* Support deployment-mode differences (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) while presenting a consistent mental model.

h2. Non-goals (v1)
* Customer notifications/subscriptions (email/webhook) beyond existing internal webhook plumbing.
* Full rewrite/backfill of all historical release notes (we will display limited backfilled items and focus on forward generation).
* Explaining model quality changes with statistically strong claims inside the Console (we will link to supporting methodology where available, and keep language bounded).

h2. Primary personas & key jobs-to-be-done
* Workspace Admin (customer):
** Understand why outputs changed and correlate to a model update.
** Decide whether to pin to a prior version or adjust rollout policy.
* ML/Platform Engineer (customer):
** Compare variants/quantization profiles and check compatibility/runtime requirements.
** Validate changes by region/deployment and confirm availability.
* Support/CS (internal):
** Answer escalations with exact change timeline and internal context (who/what/why).
** Export/share a safe customer-visible view.
* Release Engineering (internal):
** Confirm release note coverage before promotion; spot missing/invalid metadata.

h2. Definitions (UI terms)
* Model: a named serving target in the catalog (e.g., “rw-chat-8b”).
* Version: semver-like model artifact/version line item (e.g., 2.3.1).
* Variant: model “shape” under a version (instruction-tuned vs base; context length variants).
* Profile: quantization/runtime profile (e.g., fp16, int8, gptq), sometimes exposed as “Performance profiles”.
* Change event: an atomic change that produces a changelog entry (metadata edited, version promoted, deprecation announced, rollback executed).
* Release note entry: a customer-visible rendering of one or more change events, categorized (breaking, security, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation) with severity and guidance.
* Rollout: policy-driven promotion of a model version/variant/profile by cohort, region, or deployment.

h2. Information architecture
We introduce two primary Console surfaces (plus an embedded panel):

1) Model details → “History” tab
* Per-model timeline of release note entries and change events (customer-visible by default).
* Filters for deployment mode, region, time range, and “Show only my workspace impact”.

2) Console-wide “Release notes” feed
* A global feed for the workspace, scoped to models the workspace is entitled to.
* Focused on customer-visible release note entries (not every internal event).

3) Rollout view embedded “What’s changing?” panel (new)
* In rollouts (canary/A-B), show the release note entry for the candidate version(s) with a diff-like summary and “View full notes”.

Navigation placement:
* Left nav: “Models” (existing) → select a model → tabs: Overview | Deployments | History | Settings
* Left nav: “Release notes” (new, under Observability/Operations depending on IA constraints; decision pending)
* Rollouts: within existing Rollouts UI, add a right-side panel (or top section on narrow screens) for release notes and guidance.

h2. Screen 1: Model Details → History tab
h3. Purpose
Give users a reliable timeline of changes for a specific model, with “what changed for me” filtering and clear distinctions between:
* “Release notes” (curated, customer-visible)
* “Change events” (more granular, may include operational events; restricted)

h3. Layout
Top: Model header
* Model name + short description
* Current default version (per selected deployment/region)
* Chips: Hosted / Dedicated / Private availability (read-only indicators)

Below: Filters row
* Time range: Last 7 days | 30 days | 90 days | Custom
* Deployment mode: Hosted | Dedicated | Private (multi-select; defaults based on workspace entitlements)
* Region: multi-select, populated from the workspace’s enabled regions + “All regions”
* Impact scope toggle:
** “Show all changes” (default)
** “Show changes that affected my workspace” (filters to events that intersect with workspace entitlements + active deployments)
* Category filter: All | Breaking | Security | Performance | Quality | Bugfix | Deprecation
* Search (optional in v1.1): keyword search across title/body (public fields only)

Main content: Timeline list
Each item is a “Release note entry card” (customer-visible) with optional “Related events” expandable section.

Right rail (optional):
* “Pinned versions” quick link
* “Rollback guidance” quick link
* “Compatibility” quick link

h3. Release note entry card spec
Fields shown (customer-visible):
* Category badge + severity (e.g., Breaking—High)
* Title (one line)
* Date/time (localized) + “Effective in: [regions] / [deployment modes]”
* Summary (2–4 lines, truncated with “Read more”)
* Key changes bullets (up to 5)
* Guidance links:
** “Migration/compatibility notes”
** “Rollback guidance”
** “Known limitations” (only if present)
* Affected versions:
** “From: vX.Y.Z → To: vA.B.C” (or “New version available: vA.B.C”)

Expand/collapse behavior:
* Default collapsed; expand reveals full body + structured sections (if present):
** What changed
** Who is affected
** Required action (if any)
** Rollback
** Deprecation timeline (if any)
** Quality notes (bounded language)

“Related events” (collapsed by default):
* Shows a small list of event IDs and timestamps (e.g., “version promoted”, “metadata updated”) for internal roles only.

h3. “Effective in” computation rules (display)
Because availability differs by deployment mode/region, every entry must communicate where it applies.

Display rules:
* If entry applies to all selected regions/modes: show “Effective in: All selected regions”.
* If subset: show chips with “+N more” (hover/expand to full list).
* If event hasn’t reached selected region yet (scheduled/rolling): show “Rolling out” status with ETA (if provided) or “No ETA”.

Status indicators (small pill on card):
* “Available” (fully rolled out for the selected scope)
* “Rolling out” (partially rolled out)
* “Scheduled” (future effective start)
* “Reverted” (rollback occurred; link to rollback entry)

h3. Empty and edge states
* No entries in time range: show empty state with suggested actions:
** Expand time range
** Remove region filter
** View global Release notes feed
* Selected scope includes Private but workspace has no Private entitlement: hide Private filter option and show tooltip “Not enabled for this workspace”.
* Model is deprecated: show banner at top with deprecation date and replacement model(s), linking to relevant entry.

h2. Screen 2: Console-wide Release notes feed
h3. Purpose
Provide a single place where customers can see changes across models, with sensible defaults for relevance and minimal noise.

h3. Default behavior
* Default scope: “Changes that affected my workspace” ON.
* Default time range: last 30 days.
* Default categories: all.

h3. Layout
Header:
* “Release notes”
* Toggle: “Affected my workspace” vs “All available to workspace”

Filters bar:
* Time range
* Deployment mode
* Region
* Category
* Model selector (multi-select with typeahead)

List:
* Same card component as History tab.
* Each card includes model name + link to model details.

h3. Pagination and performance constraints
* Infinite scroll or pagination (decision pending; recommendation: pagination with stable URLs).
* Target: first contentful list within 1.5s p95 on typical broadband; filter changes within 800ms p95 (excluding backend latency).

h2. Screen 3: Rollouts UI embedded “What’s changing?” panel
h3. Purpose
When a user configures or monitors a rollout (canary or A/B), they should see a clear explanation of what they are rolling out.

h3. Entry points
* Rollout creation flow (step: Select candidate version/variant/profile)
* Rollout detail page (active rollout)
* Rollout evaluation view (A/B metrics page)

h3. Panel contents
Top:
* Candidate: version/variant/profile
* Baseline: current default (or currently pinned) version

Then:
* “Release notes summary”:
** Category/severity
** 2–3 key change bullets
** Known risks/limitations (if present)
** “View full release notes” (opens drawer)

Drawer (full notes):
* Same expanded view as History card, plus:
** “Applied to this rollout scope” explanation (computed): region(s), cohort(s), traffic split.
** “Safety rails” callout if rollout has fallback policies configured.

h3. Canary vs A/B nuances
* Canary:
** Show “Expected impact window” (if policy schedules are configured).
** Emphasize rollback and fallback info.
* A/B:
** Show “Comparison context”: baseline vs candidate.
** If eval signal references exist, show a bounded callout: “Quality signals available” with link to internal methodology page (customer-safe) or “Not available”.

h2. Filtering & scoping behavior (all screens)
h3. Deployment mode filter
* Options only include modes the workspace can access.
* Dedicated regions may be a subset of Hosted; reflect that in the region filter (disable unavailable combinations).

h3. Region filter
* Populated based on workspace configuration and entitlements.
* If a user selects regions with no changes, show partial empty state but keep list for other regions.

h3. “Affected my workspace” vs “All changes”
Definition: “Affected my workspace” includes entries where the effective scope intersects:
* the workspace’s enabled regions, and
* the workspace’s allowed deployment modes, and
* any model/version currently used by at least one of:
** active deployments (Hosted usage, Dedicated pool binding, Private bundle installed), or
** pinned versions configured in workspace settings.

Note: For Hosted customers without explicit “deployments”, we approximate “used by workspace” using last-30-days usage telemetry for that model endpoint. (If telemetry unavailable, fallback to entitlements-only with disclaimer.)

h2. Public vs internal fields (RBAC & redaction)
This UX depends on strict enforcement of “public/customer-visible” vs “internal” release note content.

h3. Roles
* Standard customer roles: Viewer, Developer, Workspace Admin
* Internal roles: Support, Release Engineering, Security/Compliance, Super Admin

h3. Display rules
* Customer roles:
** Only customer-visible release note entries render.
** No internal-only fields, event IDs, actor identity, or internal links.
* Internal roles:
** Default view still shows customer-visible rendering.
** A toggle appears: “Show internal details” (off by default). When enabled:
*** show internal notes section
*** show event IDs
*** show actor + source (control plane vs registry)
*** show audit log link-out

h3. Copy/visibility safeguards
* Any internal-only content should be visually separated (e.g., grey background section) and labeled “Internal only”.
* Internal toggle state must not persist across sessions for non-admin internal roles unless explicitly re-enabled (to reduce accidental screen-sharing leaks).
* When “internal details” is enabled, show a persistent banner: “Internal details visible—do not share externally.”

h2. Content guidelines (UI constraints)
While editorial rules live elsewhere, the Console must enforce a few UI-bound constraints:
* Titles must be <= 80 characters recommended; truncate with tooltip.
* Summary must be <= 280 characters recommended; truncate with “Read more”.
* Categories are single-select per entry (primary), with optional tags (secondary) shown as small chips.
* Severity language:
** Low / Medium / High
** Breaking changes default to High unless overridden by template logic.

h2. Accessibility & internationalization
* All filters keyboard navigable; focus order matches visual order.
* Badge colors meet contrast requirements; do not rely on color alone (include text label).
* Dates/times localized; ensure “Effective in” lists are screen-reader friendly.
* Long model/version strings must wrap gracefully; avoid horizontal scrolling in cards.

h2. Telemetry & success metrics (instrumentation)
Events to instrument:
* release_notes_feed_viewed
* release_notes_filter_changed (include filter types; no PII)
* release_note_card_expanded
* release_note_rollback_link_clicked
* rollout_whats_changing_opened
* internal_details_toggled (internal roles only)

UX success indicators (paired with PRD metrics):
* Reduction in support tickets “missing release notes for promotion”.
* Increase in self-serve resolution: clicks on rollback guidance + decrease in “output drift” escalations needing engineering intervention.
* Time-to-answer for Support (internal): median time to locate last change affecting a model drops.

h2. API/data dependencies (for alignment)
The UX assumes:
* A Release Notes API supports:
** list release notes (global feed) with filters: model_id(s), deployment_mode(s), region(s), time_range, category
** per-model history feed (same underlying data)
** a “scope evaluation” endpoint or server-side filtering for “affected my workspace”
* Each entry includes:
** id, title, category, severity, summary, body, created_at, effective_scope (regions, modes), status (available/rolling/scheduled/reverted)
** version pointers: from_version (optional), to_version
** links: rollback_guide_url (optional), migration_url (optional)
** visibility flags: public/internal
* Rollout detail page can request release notes for the candidate version(s) directly.

h2. Error handling
* If release notes service is unavailable:
** Show non-blocking inline error with retry.
** Rollouts panel should degrade gracefully (show “Release notes temporarily unavailable” and keep rollout controls usable).
* If filters result in an invalid combination (e.g., region not supported for Dedicated):
** Show disabled states and a short explanation; do not silently drop filters.

h2. Security & privacy considerations
* Never show internal-only fields to customer roles (hard enforcement server-side + defense-in-depth client-side).
* Avoid exposing specific internal incident references, customer names, or vendor/hardware specifics in customer-visible notes.
* For “affected my workspace” computed using usage telemetry, do not expose raw usage counts in this UX (only a boolean relevance filter).

h2. Open questions
1) IA placement: Should “Release notes” live under Models, Observability, or a top-level Operations section?
2) Do we need a “Compare versions” view (diff) in v1.1, or is the card expansion sufficient?
3) For Private deployments, do we show:
* installed bundle versions (if connected) vs last exported notes only (offline)?
4) How should we present “Rolling out” when regional rollout is stochastic (capacity-driven) and no ETA is available?
5) Should internal roles have the ability to copy a “customer-safe link” that forces public rendering regardless of viewer role?

h2. Milestones (UX deliverables)
* M1 (Design-ready): Components + basic flows for History tab and Rollouts panel (this doc).
* M2 (Beta): Global Release notes feed + full filtering and workspace-impact toggle.
* M3 (GA): Polished empty states, improved scope messaging, and internal-details toggle.

h2. Appendix: Text-only wireframe notes
*History tab*
* Row 1: [Model name] [Availability chips] [Current default version pill]
* Row 2: [Time range dropdown] [Deployment multi-select] [Region multi-select] [Impact toggle] [Category dropdown]
* List: cards with left badge (category) + title; right: date; below: summary; footer: links.

*Rollout panel*
* Header: “What’s changing?”
* Baseline vs Candidate
* Bullets: key changes
* CTA: “View full release notes” (drawer)

Change log (doc):
* 2025-10-04: Initial draft (Kenji)
* 2025-10-11: Added rollout panel details + RBAC toggle behavior (Kenji)
* 2025-10-18: Updated filtering rules for ‘affected my workspace’ + empty states (Kenji)

title: Console UX Spec: Model Change History & Release Notes (Rollouts/A-B)

2) Console-wide “Release notes” feed

3) Rollout view embedded “What’s changing?” panel (new)

Navigation placement:
