* Left nav: “Release notes” (new, under Observability/Operations depending on IA constraints; decision pending)
* Rollouts: within existing Rollouts UI, add a right-side panel (or top section on narrow screens) for release notes and guidance.

h2. Screen 1: Model Details → History tab
h3. Purpose
Give users a reliable timeline of changes for a specific model, with “what changed for me” filtering and clear distinctions between:
* “Release notes” (curated, customer-visible)
* “Change events” (more granular, may include operational events; restricted)

h3. Layout
Top: Model header
* Model name + short description
* Current default version (per selected deployment/region)
* Chips: Hosted / Dedicated / Private availability (read-only indicators)

Below: Filters row
* Time range: Last 7 days | 30 days | 90 days | Custom
* Deployment mode: Hosted | Dedicated | Private (multi-select; defaults based on workspace entitlements)
* Region: multi-select, populated from the workspace’s enabled regions + “All regions”
* Impact scope toggle:
** “Show all changes” (default)
** “Show changes that affected my workspace” (filters to events that intersect with workspace entitlements + active deployments)
* Category filter: All | Breaking | Security | Performance | Quality | Bugfix | Deprecation
* Search (optional in v1.1): keyword search across title/body (public fields only)

Main content: Timeline list
Each item is a “Release note entry card” (customer-visible) with optional “Related events” expandable section.

Right rail (optional):
* “Pinned versions” quick link
* “Rollback guidance” quick link
* “Compatibility” quick link

h3. Release note entry card spec
Fields shown (customer-visible):
* Category badge + severity (e.g., Breaking—High)
* Title (one line)
* Date/time (localized) + “Effective in: [regions] / [deployment modes]”
* Summary (2–4 lines, truncated with “Read more”)
* Key changes bullets (up to 5)
* Guidance links:
** “Migration/compatibility notes”
** “Rollback guidance”
** “Known limitations” (only if present)
* Affected versions:
** “From: vX.Y.Z → To: vA.B.C” (or “New version available: vA.B.C”)

Expand/collapse behavior:
* Default collapsed; expand reveals full body + structured sections (if present):
** What changed
** Who is affected
** Required action (if any)
** Rollback
** Deprecation timeline (if any)
** Quality notes (bounded language)

“Related events” (collapsed by default):
* Shows a small list of event IDs and timestamps (e.g., “version promoted”, “metadata updated”) for internal roles only.

h3. “Effective in” computation rules (display)
Because availability differs by deployment mode/region, every entry must communicate where it applies.

Display rules:
* If entry applies to all selected regions/modes: show “Effective in: All selected regions”.
* If subset: show chips with “+N more” (hover/expand to full list).
* If event hasn’t reached selected region yet (scheduled/rolling): show “Rolling out” status with ETA (if provided) or “No ETA”.

Status indicators (small pill on card):
* “Available” (fully rolled out for the selected scope)
* “Rolling out” (partially rolled out)
* “Scheduled” (future effective start)
* “Reverted” (rollback occurred; link to rollback entry)

h3. Empty and edge states
* No entries in time range: show empty state with suggested actions:
** Expand time range
** Remove region filter
** View global Release notes feed
* Selected scope includes Private but workspace has no Private entitlement: hide Private filter option and show tooltip “Not enabled for this workspace”.
* Model is deprecated: show banner at top with deprecation date and replacement model(s), linking to relevant entry.

h2. Screen 2: Console-wide Release notes feed
h3. Purpose
Provide a single place where customers can see changes across models, with sensible defaults for relevance and minimal noise.

h3. Default behavior
* Default scope: “Changes that affected my workspace” ON.
* Default time range: last 30 days.
* Default categories: all.

h3. Layout
Header:
* “Release notes”
* Toggle: “Affected my workspace” vs “All available to workspace”

Filters bar:
* Time range
* Deployment mode
* Region
* Category
* Model selector (multi-select with typeahead)

List:
* Same card component as History tab.
* Each card includes model name + link to model details.

h3. Pagination and performance constraints
* Infinite scroll or pagination (decision pending; recommendation: pagination with stable URLs).
* Target: first contentful list within 1.5s p95 on typical broadband; filter changes within 800ms p95 (excluding backend latency).

h2. Screen 3: Rollouts UI embedded “What’s changing?” panel
h3. Purpose
When a user configures or monitors a rollout (canary or A/B), they should see a clear explanation of what they are rolling out.

h3. Entry points
* Rollout creation flow (step: Select candidate version/variant/profile)
* Rollout detail page (active rollout)
* Rollout evaluation view (A/B metrics page)

h3. Panel contents
Top:
* Candidate: version/variant/profile
* Baseline: current default (or currently pinned) version

Then:
* “Release notes summary”:
** Category/severity
** 2–3 key change bullets
** Known risks/limitations (if present)
** “View full release notes” (opens drawer)

Drawer (full notes):
* Same expanded view as History card, plus:
** “Applied to this rollout scope” explanation (computed): region(s), cohort(s), traffic split.
** “Safety rails” callout if rollout has fallback policies configured.

h3. Canary vs A/B nuances
* Canary:
** Show “Expected impact window” (if policy schedules are configured).
** Emphasize rollback and fallback info.
* A/B:
** Show “Comparison context”: baseline vs candidate.
** If eval signal references exist, show a bounded callout: “Quality signals available” with link to internal methodology page (customer-safe) or “Not available”.

h2. Filtering & scoping behavior (all screens)
h3. Deployment mode filter
* Options only include modes the workspace can access.
* Dedicated regions may be a subset of Hosted; reflect that in the region filter (disable unavailable combinations).

h3. Region filter
* Populated based on workspace configuration and entitlements.
* If a user selects regions with no changes, show partial empty state but keep list for other regions.

h3. “Affected my workspace” vs “All changes”
Definition: “Affected my workspace” includes entries where the effective scope intersects:
* the workspace’s enabled regions, and
* the workspace’s allowed deployment modes, and
* any model/version currently used by at least one of:
** active deployments (Hosted usage, Dedicated pool binding, Private bundle installed), or
** pinned versions configured in workspace settings.

Note: For Hosted customers without explicit “deployments”, we approximate “used by workspace” using last-30-days usage telemetry for that model endpoint. (If telemetry unavailable, fallback to entitlements-only with disclaimer.)

h2. Public vs internal fields (RBAC & redaction)
This UX depends on strict enforcement of “public/customer-visible” vs “internal” release note content.

h3. Roles
* Standard customer roles: Viewer, Developer, Workspace Admin
* Internal roles: Support, Release Engineering, Security/Compliance, Super Admin

h3. Display rules
* Customer roles:
** Only customer-visible release note entries render.
** No internal-only fields, event IDs, actor identity, or internal links.
* Internal roles:
** Default view still shows customer-visible rendering.
** A toggle appears: “Show internal details” (off by default). When enabled:
*** show internal notes section
*** show event IDs
*** show actor + source (control plane vs registry)
*** show audit log link-out

h3. Copy/visibility safeguards
* Any internal-only content should be visually separated (e.g., grey background section) and labeled “Internal only”.
* Internal toggle state must not persist across sessions for non-admin internal roles unless explicitly re-enabled (to reduce accidental screen-sharing leaks).
* When “internal details” is enabled, show a persistent banner: “Internal details visible—do not share externally.”

h2. Content guidelines (UI constraints)
While editorial rules live elsewhere, the Console must enforce a few UI-bound constraints:
* Titles must be <= 80 characters recommended; truncate with tooltip.
* Summary must be <= 280 characters recommended; truncate with “Read more”.
* Categories are single-select per entry (primary), with optional tags (secondary) shown as small chips.
* Severity language:
** Low / Medium / High
** Breaking changes default to High unless overridden by template logic.

h2. Accessibility & internationalization
* All filters keyboard navigable; focus order matches visual order.
* Badge colors meet contrast requirements; do not rely on color alone (include text label).
* Dates/times localized; ensure “Effective in” lists are screen-reader friendly.
* Long model/version strings must wrap gracefully; avoid horizontal scrolling in cards.

h2. Telemetry & success metrics (instrumentation)
Events to instrument:
* release_notes_feed_viewed
* release_notes_filter_changed (include filter types; no PII)
* release_note_card_expanded
* release_note_rollback_link_clicked
* rollout_whats_changing_opened
* internal_details_toggled (internal roles only)

UX success indicators (paired with PRD metrics):
* Reduction in support tickets “missing release notes for promotion”.
* Increase in self-serve resolution: clicks on rollback guidance + decrease in “output drift” escalations needing engineering intervention.
* Time-to-answer for Support (internal): median time to locate last change affecting a model drops.

h2. API/data dependencies (for alignment)
The UX assumes:
* A Release Notes API supports:
** list release notes (global feed) with filters: model_id(s), deployment_mode(s), region(s), time_range, category
** per-model history feed (same underlying data)
** a “scope evaluation” endpoint or server-side filtering for “affected my workspace”
* Each entry includes:
** id, title, category, severity, summary, body, created_at, effective_scope (regions, modes), status (available/rolling/scheduled/reverted)
** version pointers: from_version (optional), to_version
** links: rollback_guide_url (optional), migration_url (optional)
** visibility flags: public/internal
* Rollout detail page can request release notes for the candidate version(s) directly.

h2. Error handling
* If release notes service is unavailable:
** Show non-blocking inline error with retry.
** Rollouts panel should degrade gracefully (show “Release notes temporarily unavailable” and keep rollout controls usable).
* If filters result in an invalid combination (e.g., region not supported for Dedicated):
** Show disabled states and a short explanation; do not silently drop filters.

h2. Security & privacy considerations
* Never show internal-only fields to customer roles (hard enforcement server-side + defense-in-depth client-side).
* Avoid exposing specific internal incident references, customer names, or vendor/hardware specifics in customer-visible notes.
* For “affected my workspace” computed using usage telemetry, do not expose raw usage counts in this UX (only a boolean relevance filter).

h2. Open questions
1) IA placement: Should “Release notes” live under Models, Observability, or a top-level Operations section?
2) Do we need a “Compare versions” view (diff) in v1.1, or is the card expansion sufficient?
3) For Private deployments, do we show:
* installed bundle versions (if connected) vs last exported notes only (offline)?
4) How should we present “Rolling out” when regional rollout is stochastic (capacity-driven) and no ETA is available?
5) Should internal roles have the ability to copy a “customer-safe link” that forces public rendering regardless of viewer role?

h2. Milestones (UX deliverables)
* M1 (Design-ready): Components + basic flows for History tab and Rollouts panel (this doc).
* M2 (Beta): Global Release notes feed + full filtering and workspace-impact toggle.
* M3 (GA): Polished empty states, improved scope messaging, and internal-details toggle.

h2. Appendix: Text-only wireframe notes
*History tab*
* Row 1: [Model name] [Availability chips] [Current default version pill]
* Row 2: [Time range dropdown] [Deployment multi-select] [Region multi-select] [Impact toggle] [Category dropdown]
* List: cards with left badge (category) + title; right: date; below: summary; footer: links.

*Rollout panel*
* Header: “What’s changing?”
* Baseline vs Candidate
* Bullets: key changes
* CTA: “View full release notes” (drawer)

Change log (doc):
* 2025-10-04: Initial draft (Kenji)
* 2025-10-11: Added rollout panel details + RBAC toggle behavior (Kenji)
* 2025-10-18: Updated filtering rules for ‘affected my workspace’ + empty states (Kenji)

h1. Console UX Spec: Model Change History & Release Notes (Rollouts/A-B)

h2. Summary
This document specifies the Console user experience for (1) per-model change history and (2) customer-visible release notes as they appear during rollouts (canary and A/B). It defines the information architecture, primary screens, filtering behavior (deployment/region/workspace/time), RBAC handling for public vs internal fields, and key interaction details.

This spec is intentionally product+design focused. API contracts and schema details are referenced but not re-defined here.

h2. Goals
* Provide a clear, trustworthy timeline of model/version changes that customers can use to answer: “What changed?”, “When did it change for my deployment/region?”, “How risky is it?”, and “How do I roll back?”
* Make release notes discoverable in-context of rollouts (canary/A-B) so users can validate changes before broad promotion.
* Enable Support/CS and internal admins to see richer internal notes and audit context without risk of leaking internal-only data.
* Support deployment-mode differences (Hosted, Dedicated, Private) while presenting a consistent mental model.

h2. Non-goals (v1)
* Customer notifications/subscriptions (email/webhook) beyond existing internal webhook plumbing.
* Full rewrite/backfill of all historical release notes (we will display limited backfilled items and focus on forward generation).
* Explaining model quality changes with statistically strong claims inside the Console (we will link to supporting methodology where available, and keep language bounded).

h2. Primary personas & key jobs-to-be-done
* Workspace Admin (customer):
** Understand why outputs changed and correlate to a model update.
** Decide whether to pin to a prior version or adjust rollout policy.
* ML/Platform Engineer (customer):
** Compare variants/quantization profiles and check compatibility/runtime requirements.
** Validate changes by region/deployment and confirm availability.
* Support/CS (internal):
** Answer escalations with exact change timeline and internal context (who/what/why).
** Export/share a safe customer-visible view.
* Release Engineering (internal):
** Confirm release note coverage before promotion; spot missing/invalid metadata.

h2. Definitions (UI terms)
* Model: a named serving target in the catalog (e.g., “rw-chat-8b”).
* Version: semver-like model artifact/version line item (e.g., 2.3.1).
* Variant: model “shape” under a version (instruction-tuned vs base; context length variants).
* Profile: quantization/runtime profile (e.g., fp16, int8, gptq), sometimes exposed as “Performance profiles”.
* Change event: an atomic change that produces a changelog entry (metadata edited, version promoted, deprecation announced, rollback executed).
* Release note entry: a customer-visible rendering of one or more change events, categorized (breaking, security, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation) with severity and guidance.
* Rollout: policy-driven promotion of a model version/variant/profile by cohort, region, or deployment.

h2. Information architecture
We introduce two primary Console surfaces (plus an embedded panel):

1) Model details → “History” tab
* Per-model timeline of release note entries and change events (customer-visible by default).
* Filters for deployment mode, region, time range, and “Show only my workspace impact”.

2) Console-wide “Release notes” feed
* A global feed for the workspace, scoped to models the workspace is entitled to.
* Focused on customer-visible release note entries (not every internal event).

3) Rollout view embedded “What’s changing?” panel (new)
* In rollouts (canary/A-B), show the release note entry for the candidate version(s) with a diff-like summary and “View full notes”.

Navigation placement:
* Left nav: “Models” (existing) → select a model → tabs: Overview | Deployments | History | Settings
* Left nav: “Release notes” (new, under Observability/Operations depending on IA constraints; decision pending)
* Rollouts: within existing Rollouts UI, add a right-side panel (or top section on narrow screens) for release notes and guidance.

* “Change events” (more granular, may include operational events; restricted)

source.title: PRD: Model Metadata Schema v1 + Automated Changelog Generator
source.author: Marissa Cole
source.content:
h1. Overview
Redwood Inference is expanding Unified Rollouts (canary + A/B testing) and model lifecycle tooling. Today, customers and internal teams cannot reliably answer: *what changed, why it changed, how risky it is, and how to roll back*. Model/version change details are spread across the model registry, runtime configuration, PR descriptions, and ad-hoc release notes.

This PRD defines:
* A single, standardized *Model Metadata Schema v1* for all served models/variants/quantization profiles.
* An automated *changelog generator* that produces consistent, customer-visible release notes (Console + docs) and internal audit/change events.

h2. Problem Statement
h3. Current pain
* *Inconsistent customer visibility:* model updates can be promoted with incomplete or missing release notes (customer-facing “silent changes”).
* *No single source of truth:* model identity, versioning, compatibility constraints, and deployment availability are stored in multiple systems.
* *Support friction:* when customers report output drift, support cannot quickly correlate “what changed” with model promotions and rollout phases.
* *Enterprise procurement blockers:* regulated customers require change-management evidence: audit logs, retention, and clear deprecation timelines.
* *Risky rollouts:* without structured metadata (compatibility + risk), rollout policy engine cannot enforce safe defaults.

h3. Why now
* Rollouts/A-B testing is becoming a platform primitive. Release notes must be accurate per model version and per deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).
* We are increasing the frequency of model onboarding, quantization profile additions, and runtime upgrades.

h2. Goals
1) *Define and adopt Model Metadata Schema v1* for all served models/variants/quantization profiles, including:
   * semantic versioning
   * compatibility constraints
   * tokenizer/runtime requirements
   * safety/quality notes
   * deprecations
   * rollout eligibility
   * per-deployment availability (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) and region constraints
2) *Generate customer-visible release notes automatically* from metadata + change events with consistent language and categorization (security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation) and links to guidance.
3) *Provide internal changelog/audit events* to support enterprise change-management expectations and support workflows (who changed what, when, where).

h2. Non-Goals (Out of Scope for v1)
* End-to-end customer subscription notifications (email/webhook) beyond basic internal webhook plumbing.
* Rewriting all historical release notes. We will do forward-looking generation with a small backfill for high-traffic models.
* Building a full evaluation platform. We will *attach* eval references/signals where available; we will not define new eval methodologies here.
* Replacing runtime configuration management. We will reference runtime requirements but not redesign runtime config.

h2. Users / Personas
* *Customer developer (Hosted/Dedicated):* wants to know what changed before/after a rollout; needs to evaluate risk and mitigate regressions.
* *Enterprise platform owner (Dedicated/Private):* requires change history, deprecation timelines, and upgrade bundle release notes.
* *Support / Customer Success:* needs per-model, per-account visibility into recent promotions and known issues.
* *Release Engineering:* needs consistent capture of release note inputs and CI enforcement.
* *Security & Compliance:* requires auditable, retained change events and the ability to export to SIEM.

h2. User Stories
h3. Customer-facing
1) As a customer, I can view a model’s release notes for the exact version I’m pinned to, including breaking changes and rollback guidance.
2) As a customer, I can see if an update impacts my deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and region availability.
3) As a customer, I can understand if a change is a performance improvement, quality change, bug fix, security update, or deprecation.

h3. Support / CS
4) As support, I can quickly retrieve the change history for a specific model (including promotions and rollbacks) to investigate output drift.
5) As support, I can distinguish *public release notes* from *internal-only notes* (e.g., incident references, internal ticket links).
6) As CS/TAM, I can export a customer-readable change summary for an enterprise account during a live escalation.

h3. Release Engineering / Platform
7) As release engineering, I can require structured release note inputs on promotion and block promotion if required fields are missing.
8) As engineering, I can validate metadata schema and redaction rules in CI and preview the generated release notes artifact.
9) As platform/policy owner, I can use structured metadata (risk + compatibility) to gate rollout eligibility (e.g., “no breaking changes allowed for default Hosted unless explicitly approved”).

h3. Security / Compliance
10) As security/compliance, I can query audit events that prove who changed model metadata, who promoted a version, and where it was published.
11) As security/compliance, I can export audit logs to SIEM and meet retention expectations for enterprise contracts.

h2. Requirements
h3. Functional requirements
A) *Model Metadata Schema v1*
* Canonical identifiers for:
  * model family, variant, and quantization profile
  * semantic version (major/minor/patch) and build identifiers where applicable
* Compatibility constraints:
  * tokenizer requirements and versions
  * runtime engine minimum version (and optional maximum for known incompatibilities)
  * tool-calling / structured output capability flags (if exposed)
* Deployment availability matrix:
  * Hosted/Dedicated/Private flags
  * region constraints (where applicable)
  * “eligible for rollout” vs “restricted” plus reason
* Change annotations:
  * category taxonomy: security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation
  * severity level (guidelines from the style guide)
  * customer guidance links (migration / rollback / known issues)
* Deprecations:
  * deprecation status, announcement date, removal date (where known)
  * replacement guidance
* Public vs internal fields:
  * explicit field-level classification to prevent accidental leakage
  * templates must only use public fields for customer-visible notes

B) *Eventing + Changelog generation*
* Emit structured events for:
  * model metadata create/update
  * model version promotion / rollback
  * deprecation announcements / status transitions
* Generate release note artifacts:
  * per model version (customer-visible)
  * internal changelog entries with internal fields and references
* Deterministic generation:
  * given the same metadata + events, the rendered output must be stable (idempotent)
* Categorization + templates:
  * consistent phrasing per category/severity
  * include “What changed / Impact / Recommended action / Rollback” sections where applicable

C) *Product surfaces*
* Console:
  * per-model change history (filter by deployment mode, region)
  * release notes feed for recent promotions
  * RBAC: internal-only notes visible only to approved roles
* Docs:
  * public release notes pages generated from artifacts
  * stable URLs per model/version
  * RSS/JSON feed (if supported by docs pipeline) for external consumption

D) *Workflow + CI enforcement*
* Schema validation tooling for:
  * metadata schema conformance
  * redaction rules
  * required fields per change category (e.g., breaking change requires migration/rollback guidance)
* Release note preview artifact in CI for reviewers (human approval step)
* Promotion gate: block promotion if required metadata or release note inputs are missing

h3. Non-functional requirements
* Reliability:
  * changelog generator should tolerate event replays (at-least-once delivery)
  * no customer-visible release notes should be partially published
* Security:
  * strict separation of public vs internal fields
  * audit log integrity and access controls
* Performance:
  * release notes endpoints must support pagination; history queries should be fast enough for support workflows
* Backwards compatibility:
  * pinned model versions must remain resolvable
  * Private offline bundles must include the relevant release notes artifacts

h2. MVP Definition (v1)
The MVP focuses on *forward-looking* correctness, customer trust, and internal supportability.

MVP includes:
1) Model Metadata Schema v1 defined and adopted for the top N models and all new onboardings.
2) Event emission for metadata change + version promotion/rollback.
3) Changelog generation pipeline producing:
   * customer-visible release notes entries (public)
   * internal entries (with internal-only context)
4) Console:
   * read-only release notes feed (recent promotions)
   * per-model change history page with basic filtering
   * RBAC enforced; internal fields never displayed to standard roles
5) Docs:
   * public release notes pages generated from artifacts
6) CI:
   * schema validation + redaction checks
   * release note preview artifact

MVP explicitly does not require:
* full historical backfill
* customer notification subscriptions
* per-account personalized change feeds (beyond being able to filter by deployment and region)

h2. Success Metrics
h3. Adoption / coverage
* >= 95% of model version promotions have generated customer-visible release notes within 30 minutes of promotion.
* 100% of new model onboardings use Schema v1.
* Top N (target: 20) customer-used models migrated to Schema v1 within the project window.

h3. Quality / trust
* Reduce support tickets tagged “silent model change / missing release notes” by 50% within 60 days post-GA.
* 0 P0 incidents of internal-only content leaking into customer-visible release notes.

h3. Operational outcomes
* Mean time to identify “what changed” during output drift escalations reduced by 60% (baseline: support self-report + sampled cases).
* Promotion gate compliance: >= 98% of promotions pass on first attempt after initial rollout of CI checks (measures usability of workflow).

h2. Release Notes Taxonomy (Customer-visible)
We will standardize categories and required sections.

Required categories (v1):
* *Security* (may be vague by necessity; redaction rules apply)
* *Breaking change* (requires migration/rollback guidance)
* *Performance* (latency/throughput/cost; must avoid overstating)
* *Quality* (must include evidence references or careful language; no claims without statistical support)
* *Bug fix*
* *Deprecation* (requires timeline when known)

Each entry template should support:
* Summary (1–2 sentences)
* Impact (who is affected)
* Recommended action (if any)
* Rollback guidance (if applicable)
* Links (docs/guide)

h2. Public vs Internal Data Handling
Principles:
* Public release notes must be derivable from *explicitly public* fields only.
* Internal notes may include:
  * internal ticket IDs
  * incident references
  * infrastructure details not appropriate for customers
  * customer-specific context

Enforcement:
* Field-level classification in schema and generator.
* CI test cases for redaction.
* Console RBAC for internal views.

h2. Dependencies
* Model registry schema changes and migration support (Quentin Harris, Michael Nguyen).
* Control plane event emission (Ethan Park).
* Console backend endpoints + RBAC integration (Chloe Martin, Jada Williams).
* Docs build pipeline changes for generated release notes (Elliot Price, Wei Chen).
* Audit logging requirements (Naomi Feldman, Marcus Lin).
* Evals signal references (Jonas Weber, Sana Farid) for safe public summaries.

h2. Risks and Mitigations
* *Risk: leaking internal-only information.*
  * Mitigation: strict schema classification + generator allowlist + CI tests + regression tests in Console.
* *Risk: release notes claim quality improvements without rigor.*
  * Mitigation: require evidence links or “directional” language; partner review for quality claims.
* *Risk: inconsistent availability across Hosted/Dedicated/Private causing confusion.*
  * Mitigation: per-deployment availability fields + Console filtering; Private export requirements.
* *Risk: migration/backfill churn blocks progress.*
  * Mitigation: phased migration (top models first), defaults for unknowns, and clear “unknown/unspecified” handling.

h2. Phased Rollout Plan (10–12 weeks)
h3. Phase 0: Discovery + design (Weeks 1–3)
* Inventory existing metadata sources and gaps.
* Draft Schema v1 and publishing rules.
* Align on “source of truth” and public/internal strategy.
* Draft templates + taxonomy + severity guidelines.
Deliverables:
* Finalized schema spec + examples
* ADRs approved
* Initial Console UX mocks (read-only)

h3. Phase 1: Implementation (Weeks 4–8)
* Model registry storage changes + migration tooling.
* Event emission for metadata changes and promotions.
* Changelog generator implementation (service or library) + templating.
* CI validation + release note preview artifacts.
Deliverables:
* Schema validation in CI
* Generator producing artifacts in staging
* Initial internal-only query endpoints (support/compliance)

h3. Phase 2: Integration + QA (Weeks 8–10)
* Console integration: release notes feed + per-model history.
* Docs integration: public pages generated from artifacts.
* End-to-end tests: edit metadata -> promote -> publish -> rollback.
Deliverables:
* RBAC verified (no internal fields for standard roles)
* Performance baseline for query endpoints

h3. Phase 3: Rollout + GA (Weeks 10–12)
* Dogfood internally.
* Beta with 1–2 enterprise accounts (Dedicated and/or Private).
* Enable promotion gates in production with an initial “warn-only” period, then enforce.
Deliverables:
* GA checklist complete
* Operational runbooks for publishing/rollback and support workflows

h2. Launch Criteria (GA)
Must-have:
* Schema v1 adopted for all new model entries.
* Release notes generated for >= 95% of promotions over a 2-week window.
* Console and docs surfaces live.
* Audit events emitted and queryable; retention configured per security guidance.
* Redaction tests passing; security sign-off.

Nice-to-have:
* Limited backfill for top models (last 30–60 days of promotions).

h2. Analytics / Instrumentation
Track:
* number of promotions, number with release notes, time-to-publish notes
* frequency of “warn-only” CI failures by type (missing fields, redaction)
* Console usage: views of release notes and change history
* Support usage: internal changelog queries and export actions

h2. Open Questions
1) What is the minimum required evidence to mark a change as “Quality” vs “Bug fix”? (Owner: Jonas Weber / Sana Farid)
2) How do we represent “runtime-only” changes that affect output (e.g., kernel changes) when the model weights are unchanged? (Owner: Quentin Harris / Ethan Park)
3) How do we express compatibility constraints for tool-calling/structured output across model families without overfitting to current APIs? (Owner: Paula Mendes)
4) What retention periods are required for audit events across Hosted vs Private? (Owner: Naomi Feldman)
5) What’s the default policy for “unknown” deprecation timelines (how to avoid misleading customers)? (Owner: Marissa Cole / Wei Chen)

h2. Stakeholders
* Product: Marissa Cole (lead), Paula Mendes
* Engineering: Dylan Brooks, Chloe Martin, Ethan Park
* Key ICs: Jada Williams, Quentin Harris, Michael Nguyen, Connor O'Brien
* Partners: Jonas Weber, Sana Farid, DevEx/Docs, Security & Compliance, Support/CS

h2. Appendix: Example Customer-visible Entry (Template)
*Title:* Redwood Model Catalog Update: <model-name> v<semver>
*Category:* Performance
*Summary:* Reduced median latency for short prompts under typical Hosted workloads.
*Impact:* No API changes. Output should be equivalent; performance may vary by region and traffic.
*Recommended action:* None. Customers with strict latency SLOs should validate in staging.
*Rollback:* Pin to <previous-version> in Console > Model Settings.
*Links:* Model pinning guide; Known issues.

(Exact language, evidence requirements, and severity wording governed by the Release Notes Taxonomy + Style Guide.)

h1. Overview
Redwood Inference is expanding Unified Rollouts (canary + A/B testing) and model lifecycle tooling. Today, customers and internal teams cannot reliably answer: *what changed, why it changed, how risky it is, and how to roll back*. Model/version change details are spread across the model registry, runtime configuration, PR descriptions, and ad-hoc release notes.

This PRD defines:
* A single, standardized *Model Metadata Schema v1* for all served models/variants/quantization profiles.
* An automated *changelog generator* that produces consistent, customer-visible release notes (Console + docs) and internal audit/change events.

h2. Problem Statement
h3. Current pain
* *Inconsistent customer visibility:* model updates can be promoted with incomplete or missing release notes (customer-facing “silent changes”).

* *Support friction:* when customers report output drift, support cannot quickly correlate “what changed” with model promotions and rollout phases.
* *Enterprise procurement blockers:* regulated customers require change-management evidence: audit logs, retention, and clear deprecation timelines.
* *Risky rollouts:* without structured metadata (compatibility + risk), rollout policy engine cannot enforce safe defaults.

h3. Why now
* Rollouts/A-B testing is becoming a platform primitive. Release notes must be accurate per model version and per deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private).

h2. Goals

   * semantic versioning
   * compatibility constraints
   * tokenizer/runtime requirements
   * safety/quality notes
   * deprecations
   * rollout eligibility
   * per-deployment availability (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) and region constraints
2) *Generate customer-visible release notes automatically* from metadata + change events with consistent language and categorization (security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation) and links to guidance.
3) *Provide internal changelog/audit events* to support enterprise change-management expectations and support workflows (who changed what, when, where).

h2. Non-Goals (Out of Scope for v1)
* End-to-end customer subscription notifications (email/webhook) beyond basic internal webhook plumbing.
* Rewriting all historical release notes. We will do forward-looking generation with a small backfill for high-traffic models.
* Building a full evaluation platform. We will *attach* eval references/signals where available; we will not define new eval methodologies here.
* Replacing runtime configuration management. We will reference runtime requirements but not redesign runtime config.

h2. Users / Personas
* *Customer developer (Hosted/Dedicated):* wants to know what changed before/after a rollout; needs to evaluate risk and mitigate regressions.
* *Enterprise platform owner (Dedicated/Private):* requires change history, deprecation timelines, and upgrade bundle release notes.
* *Support / Customer Success:* needs per-model, per-account visibility into recent promotions and known issues.
* *Release Engineering:* needs consistent capture of release note inputs and CI enforcement.
* *Security & Compliance:* requires auditable, retained change events and the ability to export to SIEM.

h2. User Stories
h3. Customer-facing
1) As a customer, I can view a model’s release notes for the exact version I’m pinned to, including breaking changes and rollback guidance.
2) As a customer, I can see if an update impacts my deployment mode (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) and region availability.
3) As a customer, I can understand if a change is a performance improvement, quality change, bug fix, security update, or deprecation.

h3. Support / CS
4) As support, I can quickly retrieve the change history for a specific model (including promotions and rollbacks) to investigate output drift.
5) As support, I can distinguish *public release notes* from *internal-only notes* (e.g., incident references, internal ticket links).
6) As CS/TAM, I can export a customer-readable change summary for an enterprise account during a live escalation.

h3. Release Engineering / Platform
7) As release engineering, I can require structured release note inputs on promotion and block promotion if required fields are missing.
8) As engineering, I can validate metadata schema and redaction rules in CI and preview the generated release notes artifact.
9) As platform/policy owner, I can use structured metadata (risk + compatibility) to gate rollout eligibility (e.g., “no breaking changes allowed for default Hosted unless explicitly approved”).

h3. Security / Compliance
10) As security/compliance, I can query audit events that prove who changed model metadata, who promoted a version, and where it was published.
11) As security/compliance, I can export audit logs to SIEM and meet retention expectations for enterprise contracts.

h2. Requirements
h3. Functional requirements
A) *Model Metadata Schema v1*
* Canonical identifiers for:
  * model family, variant, and quantization profile
  * semantic version (major/minor/patch) and build identifiers where applicable
* Compatibility constraints:
  * tokenizer requirements and versions
  * runtime engine minimum version (and optional maximum for known incompatibilities)
  * tool-calling / structured output capability flags (if exposed)
* Deployment availability matrix:
  * Hosted/Dedicated/Private flags
  * region constraints (where applicable)
  * “eligible for rollout” vs “restricted” plus reason
* Change annotations:
  * category taxonomy: security, breaking, perf, quality, bugfix, deprecation
  * severity level (guidelines from the style guide)
  * customer guidance links (migration / rollback / known issues)
* Deprecations:
  * deprecation status, announcement date, removal date (where known)
  * replacement guidance
* Public vs internal fields:
  * explicit field-level classification to prevent accidental leakage
  * templates must only use public fields for customer-visible notes

B) *Eventing + Changelog generation*
* Emit structured events for:
  * model metadata create/update
  * model version promotion / rollback
  * deprecation announcements / status transitions
* Generate release note artifacts:
  * per model version (customer-visible)
  * internal changelog entries with internal fields and references
* Deterministic generation:
  * given the same metadata + events, the rendered output must be stable (idempotent)
* Categorization + templates:
  * consistent phrasing per category/severity
  * include “What changed / Impact / Recommended action / Rollback” sections where applicable

C) *Product surfaces*
* Console:
  * per-model change history (filter by deployment mode, region)
  * release notes feed for recent promotions
  * RBAC: internal-only notes visible only to approved roles
* Docs:
  * public release notes pages generated from artifacts
  * stable URLs per model/version
  * RSS/JSON feed (if supported by docs pipeline) for external consumption

D) *Workflow + CI enforcement*
* Schema validation tooling for:
  * metadata schema conformance
  * redaction rules
  * required fields per change category (e.g., breaking change requires migration/rollback guidance)
* Release note preview artifact in CI for reviewers (human approval step)
* Promotion gate: block promotion if required metadata or release note inputs are missing

h3. Non-functional requirements
* Reliability:
  * changelog generator should tolerate event replays (at-least-once delivery)
  * no customer-visible release notes should be partially published
* Security:
  * strict separation of public vs internal fields
  * audit log integrity and access controls
* Performance:
  * release notes endpoints must support pagination; history queries should be fast enough for support workflows
* Backwards compatibility:
  * pinned model versions must remain resolvable
  * Private offline bundles must include the relevant release notes artifacts

h2. MVP Definition (v1)
The MVP focuses on *forward-looking* correctness, customer trust, and internal supportability.

MVP includes:
1) Model Metadata Schema v1 defined and adopted for the top N models and all new onboardings.
2) Event emission for metadata change + version promotion/rollback.
3) Changelog generation pipeline producing:
   * customer-visible release notes entries (public)
   * internal entries (with internal-only context)
4) Console:
   * read-only release notes feed (recent promotions)
   * per-model change history page with basic filtering
   * RBAC enforced; internal fields never displayed to standard roles
5) Docs:
   * public release notes pages generated from artifacts
6) CI:
   * schema validation + redaction checks
   * release note preview artifact

MVP explicitly does not require:
* full historical backfill
* customer notification subscriptions
* per-account personalized change feeds (beyond being able to filter by deployment and region)

h2. Success Metrics
h3. Adoption / coverage
* >= 95% of model version promotions have generated customer-visible release notes within 30 minutes of promotion.
* 100% of new model onboardings use Schema v1.
* Top N (target: 20) customer-used models migrated to Schema v1 within the project window.

h3. Quality / trust
* Reduce support tickets tagged “silent model change / missing release notes” by 50% within 60 days post-GA.
* 0 P0 incidents of internal-only content leaking into customer-visible release notes.

h3. Operational outcomes
* Mean time to identify “what changed” during output drift escalations reduced by 60% (baseline: support self-report + sampled cases).
* Promotion gate compliance: >= 98% of promotions pass on first attempt after initial rollout of CI checks (measures usability of workflow).

h2. Release Notes Taxonomy (Customer-visible)
We will standardize categories and required sections.

Required categories (v1):
* *Security* (may be vague by necessity; redaction rules apply)
* *Breaking change* (requires migration/rollback guidance)
* *Performance* (latency/throughput/cost; must avoid overstating)
* *Quality* (must include evidence references or careful language; no claims without statistical support)
* *Bug fix*
* *Deprecation* (requires timeline when known)

Each entry template should support:
* Summary (1–2 sentences)
* Impact (who is affected)
* Recommended action (if any)
* Rollback guidance (if applicable)
* Links (docs/guide)

h2. Public vs Internal Data Handling
Principles:
* Public release notes must be derivable from *explicitly public* fields only.
* Internal notes may include:
  * internal ticket IDs
  * incident references
  * infrastructure details not appropriate for customers
  * customer-specific context

Enforcement:
* Field-level classification in schema and generator.
* CI test cases for redaction.
* Console RBAC for internal views.

h2. Dependencies
* Model registry schema changes and migration support (Quentin Harris, Michael Nguyen).
* Control plane event emission (Ethan Park).
* Console backend endpoints + RBAC integration (Chloe Martin, Jada Williams).
* Docs build pipeline changes for generated release notes (Elliot Price, Wei Chen).
* Audit logging requirements (Naomi Feldman, Marcus Lin).
* Evals signal references (Jonas Weber, Sana Farid) for safe public summaries.

h2. Risks and Mitigations
* *Risk: leaking internal-only information.*
  * Mitigation: strict schema classification + generator allowlist + CI tests + regression tests in Console.
* *Risk: release notes claim quality improvements without rigor.*
  * Mitigation: require evidence links or “directional” language; partner review for quality claims.
* *Risk: inconsistent availability across Hosted/Dedicated/Private causing confusion.*
  * Mitigation: per-deployment availability fields + Console filtering; Private export requirements.
* *Risk: migration/backfill churn blocks progress.*
  * Mitigation: phased migration (top models first), defaults for unknowns, and clear “unknown/unspecified” handling.

h2. Phased Rollout Plan (10–12 weeks)
h3. Phase 0: Discovery + design (Weeks 1–3)
* Inventory existing metadata sources and gaps.
* Draft Schema v1 and publishing rules.
* Align on “source of truth” and public/internal strategy.
* Draft templates + taxonomy + severity guidelines.
Deliverables:
* Finalized schema spec + examples
* ADRs approved
* Initial Console UX mocks (read-only)

h3. Phase 1: Implementation (Weeks 4–8)
* Model registry storage changes + migration tooling.
* Event emission for metadata changes and promotions.
* Changelog generator implementation (service or library) + templating.
* CI validation + release note preview artifacts.
Deliverables:
* Schema validation in CI
* Generator producing artifacts in staging
* Initial internal-only query endpoints (support/compliance)

h3. Phase 2: Integration + QA (Weeks 8–10)
* Console integration: release notes feed + per-model history.
* Docs integration: public pages generated from artifacts.
* End-to-end tests: edit metadata -> promote -> publish -> rollback.
Deliverables:
* RBAC verified (no internal fields for standard roles)
* Performance baseline for query endpoints

h3. Phase 3: Rollout + GA (Weeks 10–12)
* Dogfood internally.
* Beta with 1–2 enterprise accounts (Dedicated and/or Private).
* Enable promotion gates in production with an initial “warn-only” period, then enforce.
Deliverables:
* GA checklist complete
* Operational runbooks for publishing/rollback and support workflows

h2. Launch Criteria (GA)
Must-have:
* Schema v1 adopted for all new model entries.
* Release notes generated for >= 95% of promotions over a 2-week window.
* Console and docs surfaces live.
* Audit events emitted and queryable; retention configured per security guidance.
* Redaction tests passing; security sign-off.

Nice-to-have:
* Limited backfill for top models (last 30–60 days of promotions).

h2. Analytics / Instrumentation
Track:
* number of promotions, number with release notes, time-to-publish notes
* frequency of “warn-only” CI failures by type (missing fields, redaction)
* Console usage: views of release notes and change history
* Support usage: internal changelog queries and export actions

h2. Open Questions
1) What is the minimum required evidence to mark a change as “Quality” vs “Bug fix”? (Owner: Jonas Weber / Sana Farid)
2) How do we represent “runtime-only” changes that affect output (e.g., kernel changes) when the model weights are unchanged? (Owner: Quentin Harris / Ethan Park)
3) How do we express compatibility constraints for tool-calling/structured output across model families without overfitting to current APIs? (Owner: Paula Mendes)
4) What retention periods are required for audit events across Hosted vs Private? (Owner: Naomi Feldman)
5) What’s the default policy for “unknown” deprecation timelines (how to avoid misleading customers)? (Owner: Marissa Cole / Wei Chen)

h2. Stakeholders
* Product: Marissa Cole (lead), Paula Mendes
* Engineering: Dylan Brooks, Chloe Martin, Ethan Park
* Key ICs: Jada Williams, Quentin Harris, Michael Nguyen, Connor O'Brien
* Partners: Jonas Weber, Sana Farid, DevEx/Docs, Security & Compliance, Support/CS

h2. Appendix: Example Customer-visible Entry (Template)
*Title:* Redwood Model Catalog Update: <model-name> v<semver>
*Category:* Performance
*Summary:* Reduced median latency for short prompts under typical Hosted workloads.
*Impact:* No API changes. Output should be equivalent; performance may vary by region and traffic.
*Recommended action:* None. Customers with strict latency SLOs should validate in staging.
*Rollback:* Pin to <previous-version> in Console > Model Settings.
*Links:* Model pinning guide; Known issues.

(Exact language, evidence requirements, and severity wording governed by the Release Notes Taxonomy + Style Guide.)

source.title: Design Spec: Model Version Rollouts (Canonical Plan Template)
source.author: Monica Patel
source.body:
h1. Overview
This page defines the canonical rollout plan template for introducing a new LLM model version behind an existing model name (alias) or introducing a new model endpoint that will receive production traffic. It is intended to be copied into a new rollout plan page per change.

h1. When this template is required
Use this template for any of the following:
* Changing the default version behind a model alias
* Introducing a new version that will receive >0% external customer traffic
* Changing routing policy or fallback behavior in a way that affects customer requests

h1. Rollout plan template (copy/paste)

h2. 1) Summary
* What is changing?
* Why now?
* Expected customer impact (best-case and worst-case)
* Who is on point during rollout (name + Slack channel)

h2. 2) Scope
* Environments: Hosted / Dedicated / Private
* Regions impacted
* Customer tiers or allowlists (if any)
* Request types: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank (as applicable)

h2. 3) Versioning and compatibility
* Model alias (if any)
* New version identifier (e.g., rw-llama-3-70b@2025-10-01)
* Backward compatibility notes (tool calling schema, output formatting, tokenization differences)
* Max context and max output token limits

h2. 4) Success criteria (must be explicit thresholds)
Define pass/fail thresholds for each category and the evaluation window. Minimum required categories:
* Reliability: 5xx rate, timeouts, admission rejects
* Latency: p50/p95 end-to-end and time-to-first-token for streaming
* Cost/efficiency: tokens/sec/GPU, cache hit rate, batch efficiency
* Quality: regression guardrail (eval set delta) and customer-reported issues

Include a table with thresholds (example):
||Metric||Window||Pass threshold||Fail threshold||Owner||
|p95 latency|30 min|<= +5% vs baseline|> +10% vs baseline|SRE oncall|
|5xx rate|30 min|<= +0.05% absolute|> +0.2% absolute|Platform oncall|
|Quality score (internal eval)|per run|>= baseline - 0.3|< baseline - 0.7|Applied ML|

h2. 5) Monitoring and dashboards
* Links to dashboards (by service/model/region)
* Alerts to enable (list)
* Trace/log queries for fast triage
* Who watches what at each ramp step

h2. 6) Rollout strategy
* Canary design: what % first, for how long, and what signals decide next step
* Ramp schedule (with timestamps)
* Controls: per-customer allowlist, per-region traffic caps, concurrency caps
* A/B test plan (if used): experiment id, assignment, evaluation criteria

h2. 7) Rollback and fallback
* Rollback trigger conditions (must map to thresholds)
* Rollback procedure reference (runbook link)
* Fallback policy (automatic): target version(s), ordering, and constraints
* What happens for in-flight requests and streaming responses

h2. 8) Customer communications
* Which customers must be notified (Dedicated/Private, contractually sensitive)
* Channel: email, support ticket, status page, in-product banner
* Timeline: before/at/after rollout
* Message content requirements: change summary, risk, opt-out/pinning path, support contact

h2. 9) Approvals
* Change class (per policy)
* Approvers and timestamps
* Approved change window

h2. 10) Post-rollout validation
* Final KPIs after full ramp
* Customer feedback summary
* Follow-up tasks and owners

h1. Notes
* The plan should be treated as the source of truth during an incident.
* If any section is not applicable, write N/A with a brief justification.

source.title: Release Notes: Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode
source.author: Connor O'Brien
source.content:
h1. Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode (GA)

*Release date:* 2025-09-12

This release adds customer-configurable autoscaling guardrails for *Redwood Dedicated* deployments (hard *min/max capacity bounds*) and an optional *Predictive* mode that pre-scales for known traffic ramps while preserving safety invariants and auditability.

h2. What changed

h3. 1) Customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard enforcement)

You can now set *hard* per-deployment capacity bounds:

* *Min capacity* (baseline): autoscaler will not scale below this value.
* *Max capacity* (ceiling): autoscaler will not scale above this value.

Bounds are enforced in the autoscaler loop via deterministic clamping:

* We compute *requested desired capacity* (from reactive + optional predictive inputs).
* We compute *bounded desired capacity* = clamp(requested_desired, min, max).
* We persist/emit a reasoned decision record (requested vs bounded and why).

h3. 2) Predictive mode (optional, bounded, with reactive override)

When enabled, Predictive mode biases scale decisions using forecasted demand signals (e.g., time-of-day patterns and historical workload characteristics). Predictive mode is designed as a *bias*, not a replacement:

* Reactive signals (queue depth / saturation) can override prediction when prediction is wrong.
* All outcomes are still constrained by min/max bounds.
* If forecasts are stale/unavailable, the autoscaler falls back to reactive behavior.

h3. 3) Safety, stability, and failure-mode handling

This release includes additional guardrails to reduce oscillation and prevent unsafe downscales:

* Scale cooldowns + dampening (anti-oscillation)
* Minimum step sizing for scale changes (where applicable)
* Safe handling of missing/invalid predictive inputs (no scale-to-zero behavior)
* Improved behavior and surfacing when *min capacity cannot be met* due to region capacity shortages

h3. 4) Observability + auditability

New metrics, logs, dashboards, and alerts make scaling behavior explainable and supportable:

* Desired vs bounded capacity time series
* Bound-hit counters (min-hit and max-hit)
* Predictive error metrics (prediction vs actual demand, forecast staleness)
* Scale event timeline with reasons (reactive vs predictive vs bounded)
* Audit events for config changes (who/when/what) and scaling decisions (requested, bounded, why)

h2. Availability

* *Applies to:* Redwood Dedicated deployments
* *Rollout stage:* GA for eligible Dedicated accounts
* *Default behavior:* No behavior change for existing Dedicated deployments unless the feature is enabled for the account and a scaling policy is configured.

h2. How to enable

h3. Prerequisites

* Account must be allowlisted / feature-flag enabled for autoscaling policy configuration (Dedicated).
* The deployment must be using the Dedicated autoscaler (standard for Dedicated fleets).

h3. Enable in Console

1. Navigate to *Dedicated* → select deployment → *Scaling policy*.
2. Configure:
   a. *Min capacity* (baseline)
   b. *Max capacity* (ceiling)
   c. Optional: *Cooldowns* (if exposed for your account)
3. Optional: toggle *Predictive mode*.
4. Save. The control plane validates constraints (e.g., min ≤ max) and records an audit log event.

h3. Enable via API / IaC

* API: Update the deployment scaling policy via the Control Plane scaling policy endpoints.
* Terraform: Set min/max and predictive mode in the Redwood Dedicated modules (where available for your account).

For exact field names and examples, refer to the design spec (see Links).

h2. Semantics and precedence (important)

* Bounds are *hard* and always applied to the final desired capacity.
* Predictive mode can increase/decrease the requested desired capacity, but cannot violate bounds.
* Reactive signals can override prediction when the system is saturating or queueing, but cannot violate bounds.
* All decisions are deterministic and recorded with both requested and bounded values.

For the normative specification, see: *ADR: Autoscaling bounds precedence and safety invariants*.

h2. Migration notes

h3. Existing Dedicated deployments

* No changes are applied automatically.
* If you set bounds, ensure the *min capacity* is consistent with your baseline SLO needs and regional capacity availability.
* If you enable Predictive mode, start with conservative bounds and review prediction error and bound-hit metrics during the first week.

h3. Recommended rollout for a customer deployment

1. Set *max capacity* first (budget/safety ceiling) while keeping min at current baseline.
2. Observe bound-hit rate and queue depth for 2–3 business days.
3. Adjust min upward only if you need stronger baseline latency protection.
4. Enable Predictive mode (optional) and validate:
   * p95 latency during ramps
   * queue depth trends
   * prediction staleness / error

h2. Operational impact and support guidance

h3. What on-call should expect

* New alerts for max-bound saturation (often correlated with queue buildup).
* New degraded state when min cannot be met due to regional shortage.
* New audit log entries for policy changes and autoscaler decisions.

h3. Common scenarios

* *Customer hits max bound and sees queueing:* This is expected behavior. The system will not exceed max. Work with CS/TAM to evaluate raising max, optimizing throughput (batching/quantization), or adding burst capacity options.
* *Customer expects predictive to “fix” max-bound queueing:* Predictive mode cannot violate max. It can only pre-scale within the ceiling.
* *Customer sees oscillation after enabling predictive:* Use cooldown/dampening defaults; validate forecast staleness; consider disabling predictive temporarily and returning to reactive-only.

h2. Known limitations (as of this release)

* Predictive mode effectiveness depends on sufficient history and stable ramp patterns.
* Timezone/schedule mismatches can reduce forecast quality; verify deployment region/timezone alignment in customer configuration.
* Predictive mode does not perform cross-region placement optimization (out of scope for this release).

h2. Links

* PRD: Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode
* Design spec: Autoscaling policy schema and Console UX
* ADR: Bounds precedence and safety invariants
* Dashboard spec: Autoscaling bounds observability dashboard
* SLO/alerting updates: Dedicated autoscaling indicators and paging thresholds
* Runbook: Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting

h2. Owners / escalation

* *Product (Dedicated):* Lauren Bishop
* *Fleet autoscaler:* Vanessa Ortiz / Keiko Yamamoto

title: Release Notes: Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode
preview:
h1. Dedicated Autoscaling Bounds + Predictive Mode (GA)

*Release date:* 2025-09-12

This release adds customer-configurable autoscaling guardrails for *Redwood Dedicated* deployments (hard *min/max capacity bounds*) and an optional *Predictive* mode that pre-scales for known traffic ramps while preserving safety invariants and auditability.

h2. What changed

h3. 1) Customer-configurable autoscaling bounds (hard enforcement)

You can now set *hard* per-deployment capacity bounds:

* *Min capacity* (baseline): autoscaler will not scale below this value.
* *Max capacity* (ceiling): autoscaler will not scale above this value.

Bounds are enforced in the autoscaler loop via deterministic clamping:

* We compute *requested desired capacity* (from reactive + optional predictive inputs).
* We compute *bounded desired capacity* = clamp(requested_desired, min, max).
* We persist/emit a reasoned decision record (requested vs bounded and why).

h3. 2) Predictive mode (optional, bounded, with reactive override)

When enabled, Predictive mode biases scale decisions using forecasted demand signals (e.g., time-of-day patterns and historical workload characteristics). Predictive mode is designed as a *bias*, not a replacement:

* Reactive signals (queue depth / saturation) can override prediction when prediction is wrong.
* All outcomes are still constrained by min/max bounds.
* If forecasts are stale/unavailable, the autoscaler falls back to reactive behavior.

h3. 3) Safety, stability, and failure-mode handling

This release includes additional guardrails to reduce oscillation and prevent unsafe downscales:

* Scale cooldowns + dampening (anti-oscillation)
* Minimum step sizing for scale changes (where applicable)
* Safe handling of missing/invalid predictive inputs (no scale-to-zero behavior)
* Improved behavior and surfacing when *min capacity cannot be met* due to region capacity shortages

h3. 4) Observability + auditability

New metrics, logs, dashboards, and alerts make scaling behavior explainable and supportable:

* Desired vs bounded capacity time series
* Bound-hit counters (min-hit and max-hit)
* Predictive error metrics (prediction vs actual demand, forecast staleness)
* Scale event timeline with reasons (reactive vs predictive vs bounded)
* Audit events for config changes (who/when/what) and scaling decisions (requested, bounded, why)

h2. Availability

* *Applies to:* Redwood Dedicated deployments
* *Rollout stage:* GA for eligible Dedicated accounts
* *Default behavior:* No behavior change for existing Dedicated deployments unless the feature is enabled for the account and a scaling policy is configured.

h2. How to enable

h3. Prerequisites

* Account must be allowlisted / feature-flag enabled for autoscaling policy configuration (Dedicated).
* The deployment must be using the Dedicated autoscaler (standard for Dedicated fleets).

h3. Enable in Console

1. Navigate to *Dedicated* → select deployment → *Scaling policy*.
2. Configure:
   a. *Min capacity* (baseline)
   b. *Max capacity* (ceiling)
   c. Optional: *Cooldowns* (if exposed for your account)
3. Optional: toggle *Predictive mode*.
4. Save. The control plane validates constraints (e.g., min ≤ max) and records an audit log event.

h3. Enable via API / IaC

* API: Update the deployment scaling policy via the Control Plane scaling policy endpoints.
* Terraform: Set min/max and predictive mode in the Redwood Dedicated modules (where available for your account).

For exact field names and examples, refer to the design spec (see Links).

h2. Semantics and precedence (important)

* Bounds are *hard* and always applied to the final desired capacity.
* Predictive mode can increase/decrease the requested desired capacity, but cannot violate bounds.
* Reactive signals can override prediction when the system is saturating or queueing, but cannot violate bounds.
* All decisions are deterministic and recorded with both requested and bounded values.

For the normative specification, see: *ADR: Autoscaling bounds precedence and safety invariants*.

h2. Migration notes

h3. Existing Dedicated deployments

* No changes are applied automatically.
* If you set bounds, ensure the *min capacity* is consistent with your baseline SLO needs and regional capacity availability.
* If you enable Predictive mode, start with conservative bounds and review prediction error and bound-hit metrics during the first week.

h3. Recommended rollout for a customer deployment

1. Set *max capacity* first (budget/safety ceiling) while keeping min at current baseline.
2. Observe bound-hit rate and queue depth for 2–3 business days.
3. Adjust min upward only if you need stronger baseline latency protection.
4. Enable Predictive mode (optional) and validate:
   * p95 latency during ramps
   * queue depth trends
   * prediction staleness / error

h2. Operational impact and support guidance

h3. What on-call should expect

* New alerts for max-bound saturation (often correlated with queue buildup).
* New degraded state when min cannot be met due to regional shortage.
* New audit log entries for policy changes and autoscaler decisions.

h3. Common scenarios

* *Customer hits max bound and sees queueing:* This is expected behavior. The system will not exceed max. Work with CS/TAM to evaluate raising max, optimizing throughput (batching/quantization), or adding burst capacity options.
* *Customer expects predictive to “fix” max-bound queueing:* Predictive mode cannot violate max. It can only pre-scale within the ceiling.
* *Customer sees oscillation after enabling predictive:* Use cooldown/dampening defaults; validate forecast staleness; consider disabling predictive temporarily and returning to reactive-only.

h2. Known limitations (as of this release)

* Predictive mode effectiveness depends on sufficient history and stable ramp patterns.
* Timezone/schedule mismatches can reduce forecast quality; verify deployment region/timezone alignment in customer configuration.
* Predictive mode does not perform cross-region placement optimization (out of scope for this release).

h2. Links

* PRD: Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode
* Design spec: Autoscaling policy schema and Console UX
* ADR: Bounds precedence and safety invariants
* Dashboard spec: Autoscaling bounds observability dashboard
* SLO/alerting updates: Dedicated autoscaling indicators and paging thresholds
* Runbook: Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting

h2. Owners / escalation

* *Product (Dedicated):* Lauren Bishop
* *Fleet autoscaler:* Vanessa Ortiz / Keiko Yamamoto
* *Control plane policy:* Ethan Park
* *SRE readiness:* Rafael Mendes
* *Observability:* Allison Grant / Sergio Costa

For customer escalations, coordinate via Customer Success (Rachel Kim / Dev Patel) and reference the dashboards + runbook above.
