source.title: Private Observability Minimum Pack — Product Requirements (v1)
source.author: Vivek Kulkarni
source.body:
## Summary
Redwood Private customers frequently ask “what’s the supported way to run observability?” Today, installs are inconsistent: some customers integrate Prometheus/Grafana and logs cleanly; others ship no dashboards, have incomplete scrapes, or produce unsafe/unstructured logs. This creates Support/SRE escalations and increases time-to-resolution.

The **Private observability minimum pack** defines and ships a **baseline, supported** observability bundle for Redwood Private installs (VPC and on-prem), packaged with the installer/Helm charts and validated in reference environments.

This page is the **v1 PRD**: personas, requirements, non-goals, and acceptance criteria.

---

## Goals
1. **Turnkey baseline observability** for Redwood Private installs with minimal customer configuration.
2. **Standardize** the minimum set of dashboards + alerts that Redwood Support/SRE will rely on during incidents.
3. Provide a **supported log pipeline** with safe defaults (structured logs + redaction guidance) and **export options** for common private environment sinks.
4. Ensure the pack is **upgrade-safe** (dashboards/alerts continue to work or have documented migrations) and **air-gap compatible**.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Building a “premium observability” offering (multi-cluster federation, long retention, customer-specific dashboards, advanced tracing controls, deep historical analytics).
- Replacing customer-standard tooling (e.g., “Redwood SIEM”). We integrate with it.
- Guaranteed cross-vendor parity with Hosted/Dedicated Redwood Console observability. v1 focuses on a supported minimum for Private.
- Full distributed tracing for all components in all environments. (Traces are “best-effort / optional” in v1; metrics + logs are the baseline.)

---

## Personas & primary use cases
### Persona A: Customer Platform/Infra team (primary)
**Who:** Engineers operating Kubernetes, observability, and security controls for the customer.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Install Redwood Private and know it is “healthy” without building custom dashboards.
- Integrate logs/metrics with existing monitoring/SIEM.
- Operate upgrades/rollbacks safely and verify monitoring still works.
- Avoid high-cardinality metrics, excessive resource usage, and unsafe logs.

### Persona B: Redwood Support & SRE (primary)
**Who:** Redwood Support engineers and on-call SREs assisting customers.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Diagnose common failures quickly (scrape failures, saturation, OOM/restarts, network policy issues).
- Have **consistent** dashboards and alert semantics across customer installs.
- Avoid alert noise and unclear “what do I do now?” next steps.

### Persona C: Solutions Engineering / Professional Services (secondary)
**Who:** SE/PS deploying Private in complex customer environments.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Reference architectures and sizing guidance.
- Clear “supported vs customizable” lines.
- Export recipes for Splunk/ELK/Loki/OTLP where applicable.

---

## Definitions
### What “minimum supported” means
A Private install is considered **minimum-pack supported** when:
- The customer deploys the observability components using Redwood-provided Helm/installer integration (or provides equivalent endpoints and wiring), and
- The default dashboards render data, and
- The baseline alert rules are installed (even if some are disabled by default per policy), and
- Logs are collected in a supported structured format with recommended redaction settings.

### Components in scope
**In-scope (v1):**
- Private control plane services (auth/RBAC integration points, config/policy service, upgrade controller)
- API gateway / ingress
- Routing/policy engine
- Serving runtime pods (inference)
- Model registry integration points
- Installer/upgrade controller

**Out of scope (v1):**
- Customer-managed apps that call Redwood API
- Customer model hosting outside Redwood runtime

---

## Supported deployment environments (v1)
The minimum pack must work (validated) in:
- **VPC Private** (customer-managed cluster)
- **On-prem / air-gapped** (offline image mirroring and artifact bundling)

Assumptions / compatibility targets:
- Kubernetes: supported Redwood Private versions (see Private release notes); validate at least two recent minor versions in CI/staging.
- Metrics: Prometheus-compatible scraping (Prometheus operator or standalone Prometheus).
- Dashboards: Grafana-compatible JSON dashboards.
- Logs: Kubernetes DaemonSet/sidecar-based collection where appropriate.

---

## Requirements

### R1. Packaging & installation (must-have)
**R1.1** Observability pack ships as a **versioned artifact bundle** (dashboards + alert rules + log collector config) that is included with:
- `redwood-helm-charts` (values + templates)
- `redwood-private-installer` (preflight + postinstall validation)

**R1.2** Air-gapped installs must be supported:
- All required images must be mirrorable
- All dashboards/rules/config must be packaged in the offline bundle
- Checksums/signing must be compatible with existing installer bundle verification

**R1.3** Defaults must be “safe” for typical customer clusters:
- Bounded metric cardinality
- Conservative resource requests/limits for observability components
- Log redaction defaults that avoid secrets/PII

**R1.4** Easy opt-out/override:
- Customers can disable specific exporters/collectors (where permissible) and still run Redwood
- Customers can point to existing Prometheus/Grafana/log sinks instead of installing bundled ones

### R2. Metrics requirements (must-have)
Metrics must cover golden signals across key planes:
- **Data plane / runtime:** request rate, error rate, latency (p50/p95/p99), token throughput, batching/queueing signals, saturation (GPU utilization, memory), OOM/restarts
- **Control plane:** API availability/latency, auth failures, policy/routing health, registry integration errors
- **Infra integration:** scrape health, TSDB ingestion health, exporter health

Constraints:
- Must follow Private metric naming and label standards (see standards page).
- Labels must be cardinality-safe for customer Prometheus. Avoid unbounded `tenant_id`, `request_id`, raw `prompt`, full model identifiers if high-cardinality. Prefer aggregated labels and allow explicit opt-in for more detailed labels.

### R3. Dashboards (must-have)
The pack must include a curated set of dashboards with consistent taxonomy, quick links to runbooks, and “top-of-page health summary” panels.

**Required dashboard set (v1):**
1. **Runtime Overview (Private)**
   - Latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate, request volume
   - Token throughput, generation speed
   - Batch size distribution / continuous batching effectiveness
   - Queue depth / backpressure signals
   - KV cache hit rate / utilization (where available)
   - GPU utilization + GPU memory utilization; CPU/memory basics per pod

2. **Control Plane Health (Private)**
   - API gateway availability and latency
   - Auth/RBAC integration failures (e.g., OIDC/JWT validation errors)
   - Routing/policy engine health and configuration reload errors
   - Model registry integration (sync failures, fetch errors)
   - Installer/upgrade controller health (reconcile errors)

3. **GPU / Node Capacity (Private)**
   - GPU saturation over time
   - GPU OOM events / throttling indicators
   - Node-level CPU/memory/disk pressure for nodes hosting runtime pods

4. **Kubernetes Workloads (Private)**
   - Pod restarts and crash loops for Redwood namespaces
   - Pending pods / scheduling failures (resource constraints)
   - Network policy / service discovery indicators (e.g., missing endpoints)

5. **Observability Pipeline Health (Private)**
   - Prometheus scrape success for Redwood targets
   - TSDB ingestion and storage pressure
   - Log collector health: dropped logs, queue/backpressure, retry errors

Dashboard design requirements:
- Dashboard variables must not cause high-cardinality queries by default.
- “Last 15m” view should be actionable for incidents; include longer windows for trend triage.
- Each dashboard includes a “What changed?” section: deploy version/installer version panels when feasible.

### R4. Alerting (must-have)
The pack must include Prometheus alert rules aligned to SLOs and “highest-impact failure modes.”

**Alert philosophy:**
- **Page-worthy (Sev2/Sev1)**: customer-facing availability/latency SLO burn, widespread errors, capacity exhaustion causing request failures.
- **Ticket-only (Sev3/Sev4)**: early warning signals (disk filling, scrape flakiness) with sufficient time to remediate.
- Alerts must include: summary, impact, likely causes, and a **first 3 steps** section + runbook link.

**Required alert families (v1):**
1. **Availability & error rate**
   - API request error rate above threshold (fast burn)
   - Elevated 5xx from gateway/runtime

2. **Latency SLO burn**
   - Multi-window burn rate for p95 latency where SLO guidance exists

3. **Saturation / resource pressure**
   - GPU sustained saturation above threshold (actionable)
   - GPU memory near capacity / OOM kill detected
   - Pod restart loops (CrashLoopBackoff) for runtime/control-plane
   - CPU/memory pressure for critical components (only if actionable; avoid noisy “>80% CPU” by default)

4. **Queueing/backpressure**
   - Queue depth sustained above threshold causing latency impact

5. **Observability failure**
   - Prometheus scrape failing for Redwood targets
   - Log collector dropping >X% logs or persistent send failures

**Noise controls:**
- Provide default thresholds tuned for typical Private footprints.
- Allow customer override via Helm values.
- Some alerts may be **disabled by default** if they are known to be noisy without customer-specific thresholds (must be documented and still shipped).

### R5. Logs: collection, structure, redaction, exports (must-have)
**R5.1 Structured logging**
- Redwood services must emit structured logs (JSON) with a consistent core schema.

Minimum required fields (where applicable):
- `timestamp`, `level`, `service`, `component`, `namespace`, `pod`, `node`
- `request_id` (generated ID; do not log full payload)
- `route` (bounded set or hashed/normalized per standards)
- `model` (bounded/normalized name; avoid per-request unique identifiers)
- `tenant` as a **non-reversible hash** if needed for debugging (must be optional / per standards)
- `error_code` / `grpc_code` / `http_status`
- `latency_ms` (for key request logs)

**R5.2 Redaction / safety**
- Default config must not log raw prompts, completions, authorization headers, tokens, cookies, or customer secrets.
- Provide documented guidance for safe debug escalation (e.g., short-lived debug modes with explicit customer approval).

**R5.3 Supported export sinks (v1)**
The pack must include validated configurations/examples for exporting logs from Private to:
- **Loki** (Grafana stack)
- **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
- **Splunk HEC**

Optional / best-effort (documented, not a hard requirement for GA):
- **OTLP logs endpoint** (if included, must be clearly labeled “experimental” for v1)

**R5.4 Backpressure/durability defaults**
- Collector config must define queue sizes, batching, retry policies, and an explicit drop behavior.
- Defaults prioritize **cluster safety** (do not OOM nodes) while making drops visible via metrics and alerts.

### R6. Validation, smoke tests, and supportability (must-have)
- Installer must include **preflight checks** for common breakages:
  - Storage class availability (PVC provisioning)
  - Network policies that would block scraping/collector egress
  - Minimal resource availability
- Post-install validation must verify:
  - Prometheus can scrape Redwood targets
  - Dashboards are installed and queryable
  - Baseline alerts are loaded
  - Log pipeline is running and can deliver to the configured sink

- Provide a smoke test harness suitable for CI and customer-like staging.

---

## Requirements table (condensed)
Priority levels: P0 = must-have for v1 GA; P1 = should-have; P2 = nice-to-have.

P0
- Bundle versioned dashboards + rules + log config into Helm/installer, including air-gap support.
- Runtime Overview dashboard and Control Plane Health dashboard.
- Baseline alert families for availability, latency burn, GPU OOM/restarts, scrape failures.
- Structured logs + redaction defaults + validated exports to Splunk HEC and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch and Loki.
- Installer preflight + postinstall validation + CI smoke tests.

P1
- Observability Pipeline Health dashboard with collector drop metrics.
- Optional OTLP export (best-effort; clearly labeled).
- Automated “runbook links required” linting for alert annotations.

P2
- Tracing end-to-end with sampling controls and exemplars.
- Multi-cluster federation and long retention guidance beyond sizing defaults.

---

## UX / Documentation requirements (must-have)
- Publish a customer-facing (or customer-shareable) install/config guide for enabling the pack.
- Provide clear instructions for:
  - Using customer-provided Prometheus/Grafana vs deploying bundled components
  - Exporting logs to each supported sink
  - Tuning retention, resources, and thresholds
- Provide Support-facing known issues + workarounds and day-2 runbooks.

---

## Acceptance criteria (GA)
A release is GA-ready when all items below are true:

### Installation & packaging
1. Observability artifacts (dashboards, rules, log collector configs) are versioned and shipped with the Private installer/Helm charts.
2. Air-gapped bundle contains all required images and artifacts; install completes without internet access.
3. Upgrade and rollback across at least one prior supported Private version does not break dashboards/alerts (or provides documented migrations).

### Dashboards
4. The required dashboards render data in reference environments (VPC + on-prem-like), with no empty panels due to missing metrics.
5. Dashboard variables and default queries do not generate unbounded cardinality or overload Prometheus in typical cluster sizes.

### Alerting
6. Baseline alerts load successfully and include: severity, summary, impact, and runbook link.
7. Alert noise is reviewed in staging: page-worthy alerts are not firing during normal steady-state, and any disabled-by-default alerts are documented with rationale.

### Logging
8. Logs are structured, include the minimum required fields, and do not include prohibited sensitive data by default.
9. Export configurations for Splunk HEC + Elasticsearch/OpenSearch + Loki are documented and validated in at least one reference environment each.
10. Log pipeline metrics exist for dropped logs/backpressure and are surfaced on a dashboard and (where appropriate) an alert.

### Validation & supportability
11. Installer preflight checks catch common misconfigurations (storage, network policies for scrapes/egress).
12. Postinstall validation provides a clear pass/fail signal for scrapes, dashboards, and alert loading.
13. CI smoke tests run on every release branch and include failure-injection for at least: scrape failure, runtime pod crash loop, and GPU OOM simulation (or closest feasible proxy).

---

## Dependencies
- Observability standards for metric naming/labels and alert severity taxonomy (Owner: SRE/Architecture).
- Runtime/control plane instrumentation changes to ensure required metrics exist and are cardinality-safe.
- Installer/Helm integration work for packaging + toggles + validations.
- Security review for logging schema, redaction defaults, and retention guidance.

---

## Risks & mitigations
1. **Metric cardinality explosion** (models/routes/tenants). Mitigation: enforce label constraints, provide recording rules and aggregation; default dashboards use aggregated views.
2. **Alert noise** in heterogeneous customer environments. Mitigation: multi-window burn alerts, conservative defaults, disable certain alerts by default with documentation.
3. **Air-gap packaging misses artifacts** (dashboards/rules/config). Mitigation: bundle manifest + automated bundle verification test in CI.
4. **Customer network policies block scrape/egress**. Mitigation: installer preflight checks and a documented minimal NetworkPolicy exception set.
5. **Security concerns over log content**. Mitigation: explicit prohibited fields list, redaction defaults, and documented debug escalation process.

---

## Open questions
1. Do we make OTLP export a v1 requirement or “documented best-effort”? (Current: best-effort / optional.)
2. Which alerts should be disabled by default for GA (if any) and what is the exact policy for enabling them?
3. Do we ship a bundled Prometheus/Grafana stack by default, or default to integrating with customer tooling (with an optional bundled deployment)?

---

## Owners
- Product: Vivek Kulkarni
- Program management: Talia Benson
- Telemetry platform: Logan Wright
- SRE validation: Rafael Mendes
- Control plane signals: Ethan Park
- Security review: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

Last reviewed with stakeholders: 2025-08-28

## Goals
1. **Turnkey baseline observability** for Redwood Private installs with minimal customer configuration.
2. **Standardize** the minimum set of dashboards + alerts that Redwood Support/SRE will rely on during incidents.
3. Provide a **supported log pipeline** with safe defaults (structured logs + redaction guidance) and **export options** for common private environment sinks.
4. Ensure the pack is **upgrade-safe** (dashboards/alerts continue to work or have documented migrations) and **air-gap compatible**.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Building a “premium observability” offering (multi-cluster federation, long retention, customer-specific dashboards, advanced tracing controls, deep historical analytics).
- Replacing customer-standard tooling (e.g., “Redwood SIEM”). We integrate with it.
- Guaranteed cross-vendor parity with Hosted/Dedicated Redwood Console observability. v1 focuses on a supported minimum for Private.
- Full distributed tracing for all components in all environments. (Traces are “best-effort / optional” in v1; metrics + logs are the baseline.)

---

## Personas & primary use cases
### Persona A: Customer Platform/Infra team (primary)
**Who:** Engineers operating Kubernetes, observability, and security controls for the customer.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Install Redwood Private and know it is “healthy” without building custom dashboards.
- Integrate logs/metrics with existing monitoring/SIEM.
- Operate upgrades/rollbacks safely and verify monitoring still works.
- Avoid high-cardinality metrics, excessive resource usage, and unsafe logs.

### Persona B: Redwood Support & SRE (primary)
**Who:** Redwood Support engineers and on-call SREs assisting customers.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Diagnose common failures quickly (scrape failures, saturation, OOM/restarts, network policy issues).
- Have **consistent** dashboards and alert semantics across customer installs.
- Avoid alert noise and unclear “what do I do now?” next steps.

### Persona C: Solutions Engineering / Professional Services (secondary)
**Who:** SE/PS deploying Private in complex customer environments.

**Needs / jobs-to-be-done:**
- Reference architectures and sizing guidance.
- Clear “supported vs customizable” lines.
- Export recipes for Splunk/ELK/Loki/OTLP where applicable.

---

## Definitions
### What “minimum supported” means
A Private install is considered **minimum-pack supported** when:
- The customer deploys the observability components using Redwood-provided Helm/installer integration (or provides equivalent endpoints and wiring), and
- The default dashboards render data, and
- The baseline alert rules are installed (even if some are disabled by default per policy), and
- Logs are collected in a supported structured format with recommended redaction settings.

### Components in scope
**In-scope (v1):**
- Private control plane services (auth/RBAC integration points, config/policy service, upgrade controller)
- API gateway / ingress
- Routing/policy engine
- Serving runtime pods (inference)
- Model registry integration points
- Installer/upgrade controller

**Out of scope (v1):**
- Customer-managed apps that call Redwood API
- Customer model hosting outside Redwood runtime

---

## Supported deployment environments (v1)
The minimum pack must work (validated) in:
- **VPC Private** (customer-managed cluster)
- **On-prem / air-gapped** (offline image mirroring and artifact bundling)

Assumptions / compatibility targets:
- Kubernetes: supported Redwood Private versions (see Private release notes); validate at least two recent minor versions in CI/staging.
- Metrics: Prometheus-compatible scraping (Prometheus operator or standalone Prometheus).
- Dashboards: Grafana-compatible JSON dashboards.
- Logs: Kubernetes DaemonSet/sidecar-based collection where appropriate.

---

## Requirements

### R1. Packaging & installation (must-have)
**R1.1** Observability pack ships as a **versioned artifact bundle** (dashboards + alert rules + log collector config) that is included with:
- `redwood-helm-charts` (values + templates)
- `redwood-private-installer` (preflight + postinstall validation)

**R1.2** Air-gapped installs must be supported:
- All required images must be mirrorable
- All dashboards/rules/config must be packaged in the offline bundle
- Checksums/signing must be compatible with existing installer bundle verification

**R1.3** Defaults must be “safe” for typical customer clusters:
- Bounded metric cardinality
- Conservative resource requests/limits for observability components
- Log redaction defaults that avoid secrets/PII

**R1.4** Easy opt-out/override:
- Customers can disable specific exporters/collectors (where permissible) and still run Redwood
- Customers can point to existing Prometheus/Grafana/log sinks instead of installing bundled ones

### R2. Metrics requirements (must-have)
Metrics must cover golden signals across key planes:
- **Data plane / runtime:** request rate, error rate, latency (p50/p95/p99), token throughput, batching/queueing signals, saturation (GPU utilization, memory), OOM/restarts
- **Control plane:** API availability/latency, auth failures, policy/routing health, registry integration errors
- **Infra integration:** scrape health, TSDB ingestion health, exporter health

Constraints:
- Must follow Private metric naming and label standards (see standards page).
- Labels must be cardinality-safe for customer Prometheus. Avoid unbounded `tenant_id`, `request_id`, raw `prompt`, full model identifiers if high-cardinality. Prefer aggregated labels and allow explicit opt-in for more detailed labels.

### R3. Dashboards (must-have)
The pack must include a curated set of dashboards with consistent taxonomy, quick links to runbooks, and “top-of-page health summary” panels.

**Required dashboard set (v1):**
1. **Runtime Overview (Private)**
   - Latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate, request volume
   - Token throughput, generation speed
   - Batch size distribution / continuous batching effectiveness
   - Queue depth / backpressure signals
   - KV cache hit rate / utilization (where available)
   - GPU utilization + GPU memory utilization; CPU/memory basics per pod

2. **Control Plane Health (Private)**
   - API gateway availability and latency
   - Auth/RBAC integration failures (e.g., OIDC/JWT validation errors)
   - Routing/policy engine health and configuration reload errors
   - Model registry integration (sync failures, fetch errors)
   - Installer/upgrade controller health (reconcile errors)

3. **GPU / Node Capacity (Private)**
   - GPU saturation over time
   - GPU OOM events / throttling indicators
   - Node-level CPU/memory/disk pressure for nodes hosting runtime pods

4. **Kubernetes Workloads (Private)**
   - Pod restarts and crash loops for Redwood namespaces
   - Pending pods / scheduling failures (resource constraints)
   - Network policy / service discovery indicators (e.g., missing endpoints)

5. **Observability Pipeline Health (Private)**
   - Prometheus scrape success for Redwood targets
   - TSDB ingestion and storage pressure
   - Log collector health: dropped logs, queue/backpressure, retry errors

Dashboard design requirements:
- Dashboard variables must not cause high-cardinality queries by default.
- “Last 15m” view should be actionable for incidents; include longer windows for trend triage.
- Each dashboard includes a “What changed?” section: deploy version/installer version panels when feasible.

### R4. Alerting (must-have)
The pack must include Prometheus alert rules aligned to SLOs and “highest-impact failure modes.”

**Alert philosophy:**
- **Page-worthy (Sev2/Sev1)**: customer-facing availability/latency SLO burn, widespread errors, capacity exhaustion causing request failures.
- **Ticket-only (Sev3/Sev4)**: early warning signals (disk filling, scrape flakiness) with sufficient time to remediate.
- Alerts must include: summary, impact, likely causes, and a **first 3 steps** section + runbook link.

**Required alert families (v1):**
1. **Availability & error rate**
   - API request error rate above threshold (fast burn)
   - Elevated 5xx from gateway/runtime

2. **Latency SLO burn**
   - Multi-window burn rate for p95 latency where SLO guidance exists

3. **Saturation / resource pressure**
   - GPU sustained saturation above threshold (actionable)
   - GPU memory near capacity / OOM kill detected
   - Pod restart loops (CrashLoopBackoff) for runtime/control-plane
   - CPU/memory pressure for critical components (only if actionable; avoid noisy “>80% CPU” by default)

4. **Queueing/backpressure**
   - Queue depth sustained above threshold causing latency impact

5. **Observability failure**
   - Prometheus scrape failing for Redwood targets
   - Log collector dropping >X% logs or persistent send failures

**Noise controls:**
- Provide default thresholds tuned for typical Private footprints.
- Allow customer override via Helm values.
- Some alerts may be **disabled by default** if they are known to be noisy without customer-specific thresholds (must be documented and still shipped).

### R5. Logs: collection, structure, redaction, exports (must-have)
**R5.1 Structured logging**
- Redwood services must emit structured logs (JSON) with a consistent core schema.

Minimum required fields (where applicable):
- `timestamp`, `level`, `service`, `component`, `namespace`, `pod`, `node`
- `request_id` (generated ID; do not log full payload)
- `route` (bounded set or hashed/normalized per standards)
- `model` (bounded/normalized name; avoid per-request unique identifiers)
- `tenant` as a **non-reversible hash** if needed for debugging (must be optional / per standards)
- `error_code` / `grpc_code` / `http_status`
- `latency_ms` (for key request logs)

**R5.2 Redaction / safety**
- Default config must not log raw prompts, completions, authorization headers, tokens, cookies, or customer secrets.
- Provide documented guidance for safe debug escalation (e.g., short-lived debug modes with explicit customer approval).

**R5.3 Supported export sinks (v1)**
The pack must include validated configurations/examples for exporting logs from Private to:
- **Loki** (Grafana stack)
- **Elasticsearch/OpenSearch**
- **Splunk HEC**

Optional / best-effort (documented, not a hard requirement for GA):
- **OTLP logs endpoint** (if included, must be clearly labeled “experimental” for v1)

**R5.4 Backpressure/durability defaults**
- Collector config must define queue sizes, batching, retry policies, and an explicit drop behavior.
- Defaults prioritize **cluster safety** (do not OOM nodes) while making drops visible via metrics and alerts.

### R6. Validation, smoke tests, and supportability (must-have)
- Installer must include **preflight checks** for common breakages:
  - Storage class availability (PVC provisioning)
  - Network policies that would block scraping/collector egress
  - Minimal resource availability
- Post-install validation must verify:
  - Prometheus can scrape Redwood targets
  - Dashboards are installed and queryable
  - Baseline alerts are loaded
  - Log pipeline is running and can deliver to the configured sink

- Provide a smoke test harness suitable for CI and customer-like staging.

---

## Requirements table (condensed)
Priority levels: P0 = must-have for v1 GA; P1 = should-have; P2 = nice-to-have.

P0
- Bundle versioned dashboards + rules + log config into Helm/installer, including air-gap support.
- Runtime Overview dashboard and Control Plane Health dashboard.
- Baseline alert families for availability, latency burn, GPU OOM/restarts, scrape failures.
- Structured logs + redaction defaults + validated exports to Splunk HEC and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch and Loki.
- Installer preflight + postinstall validation + CI smoke tests.

P1
- Observability Pipeline Health dashboard with collector drop metrics.
- Optional OTLP export (best-effort; clearly labeled).
- Automated “runbook links required” linting for alert annotations.

P2
- Tracing end-to-end with sampling controls and exemplars.
- Multi-cluster federation and long retention guidance beyond sizing defaults.

---

## UX / Documentation requirements (must-have)
- Publish a customer-facing (or customer-shareable) install/config guide for enabling the pack.
- Provide clear instructions for:
  - Using customer-provided Prometheus/Grafana vs deploying bundled components
  - Exporting logs to each supported sink
  - Tuning retention, resources, and thresholds
- Provide Support-facing known issues + workarounds and day-2 runbooks.

---

## Acceptance criteria (GA)
A release is GA-ready when all items below are true:

### Installation & packaging
1. Observability artifacts (dashboards, rules, log collector configs) are versioned and shipped with the Private installer/Helm charts.
2. Air-gapped bundle contains all required images and artifacts; install completes without internet access.
3. Upgrade and rollback across at least one prior supported Private version does not break dashboards/alerts (or provides documented migrations).

### Dashboards
4. The required dashboards render data in reference environments (VPC + on-prem-like), with no empty panels due to missing metrics.
5. Dashboard variables and default queries do not generate unbounded cardinality or overload Prometheus in typical cluster sizes.

### Alerting
6. Baseline alerts load successfully and include: severity, summary, impact, and runbook link.
7. Alert noise is reviewed in staging: page-worthy alerts are not firing during normal steady-state, and any disabled-by-default alerts are documented with rationale.

### Logging
8. Logs are structured, include the minimum required fields, and do not include prohibited sensitive data by default.
9. Export configurations for Splunk HEC + Elasticsearch/OpenSearch + Loki are documented and validated in at least one reference environment each.
10. Log pipeline metrics exist for dropped logs/backpressure and are surfaced on a dashboard and (where appropriate) an alert.

### Validation & supportability
11. Installer preflight checks catch common misconfigurations (storage, network policies for scrapes/egress).
12. Postinstall validation provides a clear pass/fail signal for scrapes, dashboards, and alert loading.
13. CI smoke tests run on every release branch and include failure-injection for at least: scrape failure, runtime pod crash loop, and GPU OOM simulation (or closest feasible proxy).

---

## Dependencies
- Observability standards for metric naming/labels and alert severity taxonomy (Owner: SRE/Architecture).
- Runtime/control plane instrumentation changes to ensure required metrics exist and are cardinality-safe.
- Installer/Helm integration work for packaging + toggles + validations.
- Security review for logging schema, redaction defaults, and retention guidance.

---

## Risks & mitigations
1. **Metric cardinality explosion** (models/routes/tenants). Mitigation: enforce label constraints, provide recording rules and aggregation; default dashboards use aggregated views.
2. **Alert noise** in heterogeneous customer environments. Mitigation: multi-window burn alerts, conservative defaults, disable certain alerts by default with documentation.
3. **Air-gap packaging misses artifacts** (dashboards/rules/config). Mitigation: bundle manifest + automated bundle verification test in CI.
4. **Customer network policies block scrape/egress**. Mitigation: installer preflight checks and a documented minimal NetworkPolicy exception set.
5. **Security concerns over log content**. Mitigation: explicit prohibited fields list, redaction defaults, and documented debug escalation process.

---

## Open questions
1. Do we make OTLP export a v1 requirement or “documented best-effort”? (Current: best-effort / optional.)
2. Which alerts should be disabled by default for GA (if any) and what is the exact policy for enabling them?
3. Do we ship a bundled Prometheus/Grafana stack by default, or default to integrating with customer tooling (with an optional bundled deployment)?

---

## Owners
- Product: Vivek Kulkarni
- Program management: Talia Benson
- Telemetry platform: Logan Wright
- SRE validation: Rafael Mendes
- Control plane signals: Ethan Park
- Security review: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

Last reviewed with stakeholders: 2025-08-28

## Summary
Redwood Private customers frequently ask “what’s the supported way to run observability?” Today, installs are inconsistent: some customers integrate Prometheus/Grafana and logs cleanly; others ship no dashboards, have incomplete scrapes, or produce unsafe/unstructured logs. This creates Support/SRE escalations and increases time-to-resolution.

The **Private observability minimum pack** defines and ships a **baseline, supported** observability bundle for Redwood Private installs (VPC and on-prem), packaged with the installer/Helm charts and validated in reference environments.

This page is the **v1 PRD**: personas, requirements, non-goals, and acceptance criteria.

---
